- [x] **Friction direction** always opposes **relative motion** or **tendency of motion** — must be correctly drawn in FBD.
- [x] **Capstan equation** $T_1 = T_2 e^{\mu\theta}$ governs belt and band-brake design.
- [x] **Limitations of classical laws:** Fail at very high loads (junction growth), high speeds (thermal effects), and ultra-clean surfaces (high $\mu$ due to lack of contamination).
- [x] **Typical $\mu$ values:** Steel-Steel $\approx 0.5$–$0.8$; Rubber-Asphalt $\approx 0.8$–$1.0$; PTFE-Steel $\approx 0.04$–$0.05$.
- [x] **KTU 2024 weightage hint:** Module 1 typically carries 14 marks (one full question) in the End-Sem Exam; Law statements + incline problems are **high-frequency** topics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Friction: Static, Kinetic, and Rolling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Friction: Static, Kinetic, and Rolling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Tribology** — the science and engineering of interacting surfaces in relative motion — **friction** is defined as the **tangential resistance force** developed at the contact interface between two solid bodies when one tends to move (or actually moves) relative to the other. From a KTU 2024 Scheme examination perspective, friction is broadly classified into **three fundamental types**, each governed by distinct physical laws and engineering applications:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET633, Module 1):**
> *"Friction is the resistance to motion of one body over another. It is broadly classified as (i) Static Friction, (ii) Kinetic (or Dynamic/Sliding) Friction, and (iii) Rolling Friction. Each type is characterized by its own coefficient of friction, magnitude, and governing physical mechanism."*

### Type 1: Static Friction ($F_s$)
**Static friction** is the *self-adjusting* tangential reaction force that prevents relative motion between two surfaces that are in contact but **not yet moving** with respect to each other. It exists at zero relative velocity ($v_{rel} = 0$) and can take any value from zero up to a maximum **limiting value**.

### Type 2: Kinetic (Sliding/Dynamic) Friction ($F_k$)
**Kinetic friction** is the tangential resistance force that opposes the **actual relative sliding motion** between two surfaces already in motion ($v_{rel} > 0$). Once the limiting static friction is overcome and sliding initiates, kinetic friction takes over and is generally **lower** in magnitude.

### Type 3: Rolling Friction ($F_r$)
**Rolling friction** (more precisely, **rolling resistance**) is the resisting force that opposes the motion of a body (typically a wheel, ball, or roller) that is **rolling without slipping** over a surface. It arises primarily due to **elastic and plastic deformation** of the contact region, hysteresis losses in the material, and micro-slip at the contact patch.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Real World

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Heavy Almirah vs. The Office Chair Wheel" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are trying to push a **heavy wooden almirah** (cabinet) across a tiled floor. At first, you push gently — the almirah does not move. You push harder, and harder still — still no motion. Suddenly, with a *jerk*, it slides. Once it slides, it becomes slightly easier to keep moving. This entire behavior is a textbook demonstration of **static friction** (resistance before motion) and **kinetic friction** (resistance during sliding).
>
> Now, replace the almirah with a **bookshelf mounted on caster wheels** (rollers). Even with a very small push, the bookshelf begins to roll — because the **rolling friction** is typically 100 to 1000 times smaller than sliding friction. The almirah, however, refuses to move without a large initial force.
>
> *Key Takeaway:* Static friction holds things in place; kinetic friction resists sliding; rolling friction is dramatically lower because contact is intermittent and involves deformation rather than surface scraping.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standardized for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations:

- **Coefficient of Static Friction ($\mu_s$):** Dimensionless ratio; typically $\mu_s \in [0.1, 1.5]$ for common engineering material pairs.
- **Coefficient of Kinetic Friction ($\mu_k$):** Dimensionless ratio; generally $\mu_k < \mu_s$.
- **Coefficient of Rolling Friction ($\mu_r$ or $C_r$):** Has units of **length (mm or m)**, because rolling resistance is proportional to a characteristic deformation length, not a dimensionless ratio.
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Angle of Friction ($\phi$):** Measured in degrees, defined by $\tan\phi = \mu$.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *"S-K-R = Stop-Keep-Roll"*
> - **S**tatic → body is at **S**till (no motion)
> - **K**inetic → body is **K**eeping motion (sliding)
> - **R**olling → body is **R**olling (wheel/ball motion)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Static Friction — The "Self-Adjusting Brake"

Static friction is **not a fixed value**. It intelligently matches the applied tangential force up to a maximum limit, after which motion begins. This is one of the most counter-intuitive and frequently tested properties in KTU examinations.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. A body rests on a horizontal surface under a normal reaction $N = W = mg$.
2. An external tangential force $F_{applied}$ is applied.
3. As long as $F_{applied} \le F_{s,max}$, the body remains at rest, and **static friction equals the applied force in magnitude but opposite in direction**:
   $F_s = F_{applied}$.
4. When $F_{applied}$ exceeds the **limiting static friction**, the body starts sliding and kinetic friction takes over.
5. The transition is accompanied by a sudden *drop* in the friction force from $\mu_s N$ to $\mu_k N$.

**Maximum (Limiting) Static Friction:**
$$F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N$$

**General Inequality for Static Friction:**
$$F_s \le \mu_s \cdot N$$

### 2.1.2 Kinetic (Sliding/Dynamic) Friction

Once sliding begins, the friction force stabilizes to a value governed by **Coulomb's Law of Friction** (Charles-Augustin de Coulomb, 1785). Although modern tribology recognizes that kinetic friction can vary with velocity, for KTU 2024 Scheme problems, we assume the **classical Coulomb model** where kinetic friction is constant.

**Coulomb's Law of Kinetic Friction:**
$$F_k = \mu_k \cdot N$$

**Key behavioral properties:**
- $F_k$ is **independent of the apparent contact area** (for rigid-body KTU problems).
- $F_k$ is **independent of sliding velocity** (classical assumption; modified in advanced Stribeck curves).
- $F_k$ is **directly proportional** to the normal reaction $N$.
- Direction: Always **opposes the direction of relative sliding velocity**.

### 2.1.3 Rolling Friction — The "Deformation-Energy" Story

Rolling friction is fundamentally different from sliding friction. A perfectly rigid, perfectly elastic sphere rolling on a perfectly rigid, perfectly elastic plane would have **zero rolling resistance**. In reality, all real materials deform under load, and energy is dissipated in the contact zone.

**Two primary sources of rolling resistance:**

1. **Elastic Hysteresis Loss:** As the rolling element compresses material ahead of it and decompresses behind it, a hysteresis loop is traced in the stress-strain diagram, dissipating energy as heat.
2. **Asymmetric Normal Reaction:** The normal reaction $N$ shifts slightly **forward** of the geometric centerline, creating a moment arm $a$ that produces a resisting moment.

**Empirical Formula for Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_r = \mu_r \cdot N \quad \text{(older form)}$$

**Modern Engineering Formula (Moment-Arm Form):**
$$F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R}$$

where:
- $a$ = **coefficient of rolling friction** (units: length, typically **mm**)
- $R$ = radius of the rolling element (m or mm)
- $N$ = normal load (N)
- $F_r$ = rolling resistance force (N)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the KTU 2024 Scheme-authorized reference for all Module 1 friction problems. Memorize this before any exam.**

| **S.No.** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Typical Range / Units** | **Remarks** |
|:---------:|:-------------|:----------:|:------------|:--------------------------|:------------|
| 1 | Limiting Static Friction | $F_{s,max}$ | $F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N$ | N (Newton) | Maximum value before motion starts |
| 2 | General Static Friction | $F_s$ | $F_s \le \mu_s \cdot N$ | N | Self-adjusting up to limit |
| 3 | Kinetic Friction | $F_k$ | $F_k = \mu_k \cdot N$ | N | Constant during sliding |
| 4 | Rolling Resistance (Force) | $F_r$ | $F_r = \dfrac{N \cdot a}{R}$ | N | Modern engineering form |
| 5 | Coefficient Relationship | — | $\mu_s > \mu_k \gg \mu_r$ | Dimensionless / mm | Always true for common materials |
| 6 | Angle of Friction | $\phi$ | $\tan\phi = \mu_s$ | Degrees | Reaction is inclined at $\phi$ from normal |
| 7 | Angle of Repose | $\alpha$ | $\tan\alpha = \mu_s$ | Degrees | Self-sustained slope angle of granular material |
| 8 | Friction Work (Energy) | $W_f$ | $W_f = F_k \cdot d$ | Joules (J) | Energy dissipated over sliding distance $d$ |
| 9 | Power Lost to Friction | $P_f$ | $P_f = F_k \cdot v$ | Watts (W) | $v$ = sliding velocity |
| 10 | Work Lost in One Roll Rev. | $W_r$ | $W_r = F_r \cdot 2\pi R$ | J/rev | Energy lost per complete rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Unit Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - $\mu_s$ and $\mu_k$ are **dimensionless** (pure numbers).
> - $a$ (rolling friction coefficient) has units of **length** (mm), **not dimensionless**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of friction into static, kinetic, and rolling types is **not academic pedantry** — it is the foundation of every mechanical design decision:

- **Static Friction Engineering Applications:** Brake design (high $\mu_s$ needed for holding force), bolted joint pre-load calculations, clutch design, friction drives in CVT systems, climbing and traction studies.
- **Kinetic Friction Engineering Applications:** Brake pad selection (semi-metallic vs. ceramic), machining tool wear, journal bearing design, conveyor belt engineering, piston ring-cylinder liner systems.
- **Rolling Friction Engineering Applications:** Ball bearing and roller bearing design, railway wheel-rail contact, vehicle tire dynamics, cam-follower systems, conveyor rollers, caster wheel design for hospital beds and office chairs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Angle of Friction and Coefficient of Static Friction

Consider a block of weight $W$ resting on a horizontal surface. Let a horizontal force $F$ be applied. The contact surface exerts a **total reaction** $R$ which is the vector sum of the normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to the surface) and the friction force $F_s$ (parallel to the surface).

**Step 1 — Force equilibrium in the vertical direction:**
$$N - W = 0 \implies N = W$$

**Step 2 — Force equilibrium in the horizontal direction (just before motion):**
$$F = F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N = \mu_s \cdot W$$

**Step 3 — Represent the total reaction $R$ as a vector:**
The total reaction makes an angle $\phi$ with the normal. From the force triangle:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{F_{s,max}}{N} = \frac{\mu_s \cdot N}{N} = \mu_s$$

**Step 4 — Final relationship:**
$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \mu_s \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \mu_s = \tan\phi}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The angle $\phi$ is called the **angle of friction** or **friction angle**. At impending motion, the resultant reaction $R$ is inclined at angle $\phi$ from the normal. This derivation is **mandatory for KTU 14-mark problems** involving inclined planes and wedge friction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Angle of Repose

The **angle of repose** $\alpha$ is the steepest angle of a granular pile (sand, coal, grain) that remains stable without sliding. It is fundamentally related to static friction between particles.

**Step 1 — Consider a particle on an inclined plane at angle $\alpha$ (limiting equilibrium):**
Forces along the incline:
$$F_{applied} = W \sin\alpha$$
$$F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N = \mu_s \cdot W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 2 — At the angle of repose, the particle is on the verge of sliding:**
$$W \sin\alpha = \mu_s \cdot W \cos\alpha$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$\sin\alpha = \mu_s \cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{\tan\alpha = \mu_s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The angle of repose is numerically equal to $\tan^{-1}(\mu_s)$ and is critical in the design of hoppers, silos, and bulk material handling systems. KTU 2024 frequently tests this with values of $\alpha = 30°, 35°, 45°$ etc.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Energy Lost Per Revolution in a Rolling Element

**Step 1 — Consider a rigid wheel of radius $R$ rolling without slipping on a deformable surface under a vertical load $N$.**

The rolling resistance force is:
$$F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R}$$

**Step 2 — In one complete revolution, the wheel travels a linear distance:**
$$d = 2\pi R$$

**Step 3 — Work done against rolling friction per revolution:**
$$W_r = F_r \cdot d$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$W_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R} \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 5 — The $R$ cancels out:**
$$\boxed{W_r = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot a}$$

**Critical Insight:** The energy lost per revolution is **independent of the wheel radius** when expressed in this form. This is a beautiful and frequently tested result in KTU Module 1 problems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Comparison of Friction Magnitudes for an Inclined Plane Problem

**Problem Setup (Classic KTU-style):** A block of mass $m = 10$ kg is placed on an inclined plane that makes an angle $\theta = 30°$ with the horizontal. The coefficient of static friction is $\mu_s = 0.4$ and the coefficient of kinetic friction is $\mu_k = 0.3$. Determine:
- (a) Whether the block will slide.
- (b) The friction force if it slides.
- (c) Compare with the rolling resistance if the same load is on a roller of radius $R = 50$ mm with $a = 0.5$ mm.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute weight and normal force:**
$$W = mg = 10 \times 9.81 = 98.1 \text{ N}$$

$$N = W \cos\theta = 98.1 \times \cos 30° = 98.1 \times 0.8660 = 84.96 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the component of weight along the incline (driving force):**
$$F_{driving} = W \sin\theta = 98.1 \times \sin 30° = 98.1 \times 0.5 = 49.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum static friction available:**
$$F_{s,max} = \mu_s \cdot N = 0.4 \times 84.96 = 33.98 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Decision: Does the block slide?**
Since $F_{driving} = 49.05 \text{ N} > F_{s,max} = 33.98 \text{ N}$, the block **will slide** down the incline.

**Step 5 — Compute kinetic friction once sliding begins:**
$$F_k = \mu_k \cdot N = 0.3 \times 84.96 = 25.49 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the net acceleration down the incline (Newton's 2nd law):**
$$F_{net} = F_{driving} - F_k = 49.05 - 25.49 = 23.56 \text{ N}$$

$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{23.56}{10} = 2.356 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 7 — Compute rolling resistance (for comparison) using $a = 0.5$ mm, $R = 50$ mm:**
$$F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R} = \frac{84.96 \times 0.5}{50} = 0.8496 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8 — Final analytical comparison:**

| **Friction Type** | **Magnitude (N)** | **Ratio to $F_k$** |
|:------------------|------------------:|-------------------:|
| Limiting Static ($F_{s,max}$) | 33.98 | $1.33 \times F_k$ |
| Kinetic ($F_k$) | 25.49 | $1.00 \times F_k$ (reference) |
| Rolling ($F_r$) | 0.85 | $0.033 \times F_k$ |

**Engineering Insight:** The rolling resistance is **~30 times smaller** than the kinetic friction for the same load — this is precisely why **bearings revolutionized mechanical engineering** in the Industrial Revolution, reducing energy losses by orders of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification Flowchart of Friction Types

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 1: Hierarchical classification of friction types as per KTU PEMET633 Module 1.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Friction Between Two Solid Bodies] --> B{Relative Motion State}
    B -->|No Motion, v equals 0| C[STATIC FRICTION Fs]
    B -->|Sliding, v greater than 0| D[KINETIC FRICTION Fk]
    B -->|Rolling, pure roll, no slip| E[ROLLING FRICTION Fr]

    C --> C1[Self adjusting up to Fs max]
    C1 --> C2[Fs max equals mu s times N]
    C1 --> C3[Resists impending motion]
    C1 --> C4[Angle of friction phi, tan phi equals mu s]

    D --> D1[Constant value mu k times N]
    D1 --> D2[Opposes sliding velocity]
    D1 --> D3[Independent of area and velocity]
    D1 --> D4[Lower than Fs max]

    E --> E1[From deformation and hysteresis]
    E1 --> E2[Fr equals N times a divided by R]
    E1 --> E3[Coefficient a in mm not dimensionless]
    E1 --> E4[Much smaller than Fk]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Friction Force vs Applied Force Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 2: The classic friction-response curve showing the static-to-kinetic transition. This figure is **mandatory** for KTU viva and 14-mark problems.**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O] --> B[Linear Region: Fs equals F applied]
    B --> C[Peak Point: Fs max equals mu s times N]
    C --> D[Sudden Drop: Fs max to mu k times N]
    D --> E[Kinetic Region: Fk constant equals mu k times N]
    E --> F[Continued Sliding]

    G[Applied Force F] --> H[Friction Force Ff]
    H --> I[Slope: 45 degrees, Ff equals F]
    I --> J[Vertical Drop, mu s N to mu k N]
    J --> K[Horizontal Plateau, Ff equals mu k N]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mechanism Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 3: Block diagram comparing the three friction mechanisms at the contact interface. Adapted as a block-architecture flow because the underlying physics involves microscopic deformation and adhesion phenomena that are best explained at the system level.**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Contact Interface Between Two Bodies] --> B[Asperity Contact Region]
    B --> C{Motion State Detector}

    C -->|Stationary| D[STATIC FRICTION MECHANISM]
    C -->|Sliding| E[KINETIC FRICTION MECHANISM]
    C -->|Rolling| F[ROLLING FRICTION MECHANISM]

    D --> D1[Adhesion between asperities]
    D1 --> D2[Micro interlocking]
    D2 --> D3[Output: Fs variable up to mu s N]

    E --> E1[Asp shearing and plowing]
    E1 --> E2[Continuous energy dissipation as heat]
    E2 --> E3[Output: Fk equals mu k N constant]

    F --> F1[Front zone compression]
    F1 --> F2[Rear zone recovery with hysteresis]
    F2 --> F3[Asymmetric N with moment arm a]
    F3 --> F4[Output: Fr equals N times a divided by R]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Force Vector Diagram for Inclined Plane Friction (ASCII Schematic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram 4: Force resolution on an inclined plane — a must-draw for KTU problems.**

```
                       N (Normal Reaction)
                       ↑
                       |
                       |
                       |
                       |\
                       | \
                       |  \  θ (Incline angle)
                       |   \
                       |    \
                       |     \
                       |      ↘
                       |       Fk (Kinetic Friction, opposing motion)
                       |
                       |
                       |
              W sin θ  ●————————————→  Down the slope (driving force)
                      /
                     /
                    ↙
               W cos θ
            (component of W perpendicular to slope)

            W = mg (weight of block, vertical downward)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A questions are **direct, definition-based, or short-derivation** questions. Each carries **3 marks**. Answers should be **concise (3–5 lines)** unless the question explicitly demands more.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define the following terms with one practical example for each:**
(a) Static friction
(b) Kinetic friction
(c) Rolling friction

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Static Friction:** The self-adjusting tangential force developed between two contacting surfaces which are at rest relative to each other, up to a maximum value of $F_{s,max} = \mu_s N$. *Example:* A book resting on a tilted table that does not slide.

(b) **Kinetic Friction:** The constant tangential force opposing relative sliding motion between two contacting surfaces, given by $F_k = \mu_k N$. *Example:* A metal block sliding down a chute in a factory.

(c) **Rolling Friction:** The resistance to motion of a circular body rolling without slipping on a surface, given by $F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R}$. *Example:* A railway wheel rolling on a steel rail.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State Coulomb's Laws of Friction. Why is the coefficient of rolling friction expressed in mm and not as a dimensionless number?**

**Model Answer:**

**Coulomb's Laws of Friction (Classical, 1785):**
1. The friction force is **directly proportional** to the normal reaction: $F \propto N$.
2. The friction force is **independent** of the apparent area of contact.
3. The friction force is **independent** of the sliding velocity (classical assumption).
4. The direction of friction is always **opposing the direction of motion** or impending motion.

**Why the unit "mm" for rolling friction coefficient:**
The rolling friction formula $F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R}$ is dimensionally homogeneous only if $a$ carries the dimension of length. Substituting units: $F_r [N] = \frac{N [N] \cdot a [\text{m}]}{R [\text{m}]} = N \cdot \frac{a}{R}$. Since $a/R$ is dimensionless in this form, $a$ itself must have units of length, conventionally **mm** for typical rolling element design (e.g., $a = 0.5$ mm for steel on steel, $a = 1.5$ mm for rubber tire on asphalt).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical split of **7 marks for part (a)** and **7 marks for part (b)**, OR **6 + 8** depending on the module. Here we use the **7 + 7** model consistent with PEMET633 past papers.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the angle of friction and the coefficient of static friction. A block of weight 200 N rests on a horizontal surface. If $\mu_s = 0.5$, find the magnitude and direction of the resultant reaction.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the three types of friction (static, kinetic, rolling). Compare their typical magnitudes for the same normal load on a steel-steel interface.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation and Numerical:**

**Step 1 — Derivation (4 marks):**
Consider a block of weight $W$ resting on a horizontal surface. Let $N$ be the normal reaction and $F_{s,max} = \mu_s N$ be the limiting static friction. The resultant reaction is:
$$R = \sqrt{N^2 + F_{s,max}^2} = \sqrt{N^2 + (\mu_s N)^2} = N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2}$$

The angle $\phi$ that $R$ makes with the normal is:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{F_{s,max}}{N} = \frac{\mu_s N}{N} \implies \boxed{\tan\phi = \mu_s}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Computation (3 marks):**
Given: $W = 200$ N, $\mu_s = 0.5$.

Normal reaction: $N = W = 200$ N (horizontal plane, weight balanced by $N$).

Resultant magnitude:
$$R = N\sqrt{1 + \mu_s^2} = 200\sqrt{1 + (0.5)^2} = 200\sqrt{1.25} = 200 \times 1.118 = 223.6 \text{ N}$$

Angle of friction:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57°$$

The resultant reaction $R = 223.6$ N is inclined at $26.57°$ from the vertical (normal direction), on the side of the impending motion.

---

**(b) Explanation and Comparison (7 marks):**

The three types of friction differ in the **state of relative motion** between the contacting surfaces:

1. **Static Friction ($F_s$):** Acts when bodies are at rest. Self-adjusts from $0$ to $F_{s,max} = \mu_s N$. Example: Pushing a stationary car on level ground.
2. **Kinetic Friction ($F_k$):** Acts during sliding. Constant at $F_k = \mu_k N$. Example: Sled sliding on snow.
3. **Rolling Friction ($F_r$):** Acts during rolling without slipping. $F_r = Na/R$. Example: Ball bearings in a machine.

**Typical Steel-on-Steel Magnitudes (for the same normal load, say $N = 1000$ N):**

| **Friction Type** | **Coefficient** | **Force Magnitude (N)** |
|:------------------|----------------:|------------------------:|
| Static ($\mu_s$) | 0.78 (dry steel-steel) | 780 N |
| Kinetic ($\mu_k$) | 0.42 (dry steel-steel) | 420 N |
| Rolling ($a$) | 0.5 mm (with $R = 25$ mm) | $1000 \times 0.5 / 25 = 20$ N |

**Engineering Takeaway:** Rolling friction is **~21 times smaller** than kinetic friction and **~39 times smaller** than static friction for the same load — justifying the universal use of rolling-element bearings.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A roller of radius 200 mm and weight 500 N is to be pulled over a soft ground. If the coefficient of rolling friction $a = 12$ mm, calculate: (i) the force required to just move the roller, and (ii) the energy lost per revolution.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define the angle of repose. A sand pile has a natural angle of repose of $35°$. Find: (i) the coefficient of static friction between sand particles, and (ii) the friction angle.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Roller Pulling Problem:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values (1 mark):**
- Radius: $R = 200$ mm
- Weight (Normal load): $N = W = 500$ N
- Coefficient of rolling friction: $a = 12$ mm

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance force (3 marks):**
$$F_r = \frac{N \cdot a}{R} = \frac{500 \times 12}{200} = \frac{6000}{200} = 30 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — To just initiate motion, the pulling force must equal $F_r$ (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{F_{pull} = 30 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4 — Energy lost per revolution (2 marks):**
In one revolution, the roller travels a linear distance $d = 2\pi R = 2\pi(0.200) = 1.2566$ m.
$$W_r = F_r \cdot d = 30 \times 1.2566 = 37.7 \text{ J per revolution}$$

Equivalently, using the elegant derived formula:
$$W_r = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot a = 2\pi \times 500 \times 0.012 = 37.7 \text{ J/rev} \quad \checkmark$$

---

**(b) Angle of Repose Problem:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The **angle of repose** is the maximum angle that the surface of a piled granular material makes with the horizontal plane when the pile is at the verge of sliding. It is governed entirely by inter-particle static friction.

**Step 2 — Given: $\alpha = 35°$. Compute $\mu_s$ (3 marks):**
$$\mu_s = \tan\alpha = \tan 35° = 0.7002 \approx 0.70$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction angle $\phi$ (2 marks):**
Since $\tan\phi = \mu_s$, the friction angle equals the angle of repose:
$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.7002) = 35° \quad \text{(identical to } \alpha \text{)}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_s = 0.70, \quad \phi = 35°}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and How to Avoid Marks Loss):**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion for $\mu_r$:** Many students write $\mu_r$ as a dimensionless number. **Penalty: 1 mark minimum** in 14-mark problems. The rolling friction coefficient $a$ has units of **mm**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to check the "limit" in static friction:** When asked "will a block slide?", students often compute $F_k$ directly without first checking $F_{driving} > F_{s,max}$. If the block does not slide, the friction equals the driving force, not $\mu_s N$. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of friction force:** Friction always opposes **relative motion or its tendency**, not the applied force. Drawing $F_k$ in the same direction as motion = **1 mark deduction**.
>
> 4. **Confusing angle of friction with angle of repose:** They are numerically equal for the same surface, but conceptually different — angle of friction relates to a single contact, angle of repose to a granular assembly. Examiners will give **partial marks only** if definitions are merged.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to include $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:** When mass is given, always show the conversion $W = mg$ explicitly. **Penalty: 0.5–1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Using the wrong formula for rolling resistance:** $F_r = \mu_r N$ is the **old** form; $F_r = N \cdot a / R$ is the **current KTU-accepted** form. Mixing them = **full mark loss** on the sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Module 1 Rapid Revision Checklist — "Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall."**

- [x] **Friction** = tangential resistance at the contact interface between two solid bodies.
- [x] **Three types:** Static, Kinetic, Rolling — distinguished by the **state of relative motion**.
- [x] **Static friction is self-adjusting** up to a maximum: $F_s \le \mu_s N$.
- [x] **Kinetic friction is constant** during sliding: $F_k = \mu_k N$.
- [x] **Rolling friction is much smaller**: $F_r = N \cdot a / R$, where $a$ is in **mm**.
- [x] **Universal inequality:** $\mu_s > \mu_k \gg \mu_r$ (the rolling term being 100–1000× smaller).
- [x] **Angle of friction $\phi$:** $\tan\phi = \mu_s$, measured from the normal.
- [x] **Angle of repose $\alpha$:** $\tan\alpha = \mu_s$, measured from the horizontal for a granular pile.
- [x] **Energy lost per revolution** of a rolling element: $W_r = 2\pi N a$ (independent of $R$).
- [x] **Coulomb's 4 classical laws:** proportional to $N$, independent of area, independent of velocity (classical), opposes motion.
- [x] **Friction force direction:** opposes relative motion or its tendency — never in the direction of motion.
- [x] **Limiting value check:** Always compare $F_{driving}$ with $F_{s,max}$ before assuming kinetic friction applies.
- [x] **Standard $g$:** Always use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless a problem specifies otherwise.
- [x] **Work-energy in friction:** $W_f = F_k \cdot d$ and Power $P_f = F_k \cdot v$.
- [x] **Real-world ranking (typical steel on steel):** $\mu_s \approx 0.78 > \mu_k \approx 0.42 \gg a/R \approx 0.02$.
- [x] **Memorize three key diagrams:** Block on incline, friction vs applied force curve, and the block-architecture flow of friction mechanisms.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Types of Friction: Static, Kinetic, and Rolling**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Friction (Tribological Definition):** Friction is the **tangential resistance** developed at the interface of two contacting bodies when one body moves or tends to move relative to the other. In tribology, friction is treated as a **surface phenomenon** governed by the real area of contact at the asperity level, NOT by the apparent geometric area.

The **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** is defined as the ratio of the friction force ($F$) to the normal load ($W$) acting perpendicular to the interface:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W}$$

The dimensionless quantity $\mu$ is **not a material constant** — it is a **system response parameter** that depends on a multitude of interacting physical, chemical, and environmental factors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sliding Crate" Experiment:**
> Imagine pushing a heavy wooden crate across three different floors:
> - **Wet marble floor** → Crate slides easily → Low friction
> - **Rough concrete floor** → Crate resists heavily → High friction
> - **Floor covered with sand** → Friction changes with how hard you push
>
> This everyday observation reveals that **friction is NOT just one number**; it changes with:
> 1. The **materials** in contact (wood-on-concrete vs. wood-on-marble)
> 2. The **roughness** of surfaces
> 3. The **weight** (load) on the crate
> 4. How **fast** you push it
> 5. Whether the floor is **wet, oily, or dusty**
>
> Industrial Tribology is the formal science of **quantifying, predicting, and controlling** these very effects in machines (bearings, gears, cams, brakes, etc.).

---

### 1.3 Classification of Friction (Foundation Concept)

| Type of Friction | Condition of Motion | Typical μ Range | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Friction ($\mu_s$)** | Bodies at rest, impending motion | 0.1 – 0.9 | Mandatory Module 1 |
| **Kinetic / Sliding Friction ($\mu_k$)** | Bodies in relative motion | 0.05 – 0.8 | Mandatory Module 1 |
| **Rolling Friction ($\mu_r$)** | Rolling element on surface | 0.001 – 0.01 | Forward link to Module 2 |
| **Fluid Friction** | Lubricated contact | 0.001 – 0.05 | Forward link to Module 3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Examiners frequently test that **$\mu_s > \mu_k$** for most engineering material pairs and that **μ is NOT a pure material constant**, but a function of the tribosystem. Failure to state this distinction costs 2 marks.

---

### 1.4 Why Study "Factors Affecting Friction"?

Understanding the factors governing friction enables the engineer to:

- **Design energy-efficient machinery** (reduce parasitic losses)
- **Predict wear rates** and schedule maintenance
- **Select appropriate material pairs** for bearings, brakes, and clutches
- **Specify lubricants** based on operating conditions
- **Avoid catastrophic failures** like scuffing, scoring, or seizure

The **Stribeck Curve**, **PV-limit diagrams**, and **friction maps** (Lim-Ashby maps — Module 5) are all built upon the fundamental factors analyzed in this topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ten Primary Factors Affecting Friction

In KTU 2024 syllabus context, the factors affecting friction are systematically grouped into **three super-categories**:

#### A. Intrinsic Material Factors (Surface-dependent)

1. **Nature of the Material Pair** (adhesion tendency)
2. **Surface Roughness** (asperity geometry)
3. **Hardness of the Softer Material**
4. **Crystal Structure & Lattice Compatibility**

#### B. Operational / Mechanical Factors

5. **Normal Load (W)**
6. **Sliding Velocity (v)**
7. **Temperature at Interface**
8. **Area of Contact (apparent vs. real)**

#### C. Environmental / Service Factors

9. **Surface Films & Contamination** (oxide, adsorbed layers, lubricants)
10. **Dwell Time, Humidity, and Vibration (Stick-Slip)**

---

### 2.2 Detailed Factor-by-Factor Analysis

#### **Factor 1: Nature of the Material Pair (Adhesion)**
- Friction arises from **inter-atomic bonds (cold-welds)** formed at asperity junctions.
- Material pairs with **high mutual solubility** (e.g., Cu–Ni, Al–Al) exhibit **higher μ** due to stronger adhesion.
- Pairs with **low solubility** (e.g., Steel–PTFE, Steel–Bronze) yield **low μ**.

#### **Factor 2: Surface Roughness**
- For **rough surfaces**: asperity interlocking dominates → high μ
- For **highly polished surfaces**: real contact area increases → high μ (adhesion rises)
- An **optimum roughness** exists at which μ is minimum (this defines the **boundary-lubrication regime**).

#### **Factor 3: Hardness of the Softer Material**
- Asperity junctions fail by **plastic shear** in the softer material.
- Bowden–Tabor model: $\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s}$, where $\tau_s$ = shear strength, $H_s$ = hardness.
- **Higher hardness → lower real contact area → lower μ** (approximately, for plastic contacts).

#### **Factor 4: Crystal Structure**
- **HCP metals** (Zn, Mg, Co) — low μ due to easy basal slip.
- **FCC metals** (Al, Cu, Ni) — moderate μ.
- **BCC metals** (Fe, Cr, W) — variable, often higher μ.

#### **Factor 5: Normal Load (W)**
- Amontons' First Law (1699): **Friction is proportional to normal load** (for rigid plastic asperities).
- $\mu = \frac{F}{W}$ → constant.
- For **elastic contacts** or **contaminated surfaces**, $\mu$ **decreases slightly** with increasing $W$ because harder debris crushes and acts as a third-body lubricant.

#### **Factor 6: Sliding Velocity (v)**
- Velocity affects:
  - **Flash temperature** at asperities
  - **Strain-rate sensitivity** of the material
  - **Lubricant film thickness**
- Typical behaviour: μ rises to a peak (static–kinetic transition), then decreases as velocity increases (thermal softening).

#### **Factor 7: Interface Temperature**
- Two temperature effects:
  - **Bulk temperature** affects bulk hardness → μ generally decreases with rising temperature.
  - **Flash temperature** (localized at asperity) can momentarily melt the junction → μ drops sharply.

#### **Factor 8: Area of Contact**
- **Amontons' Second Law:** Friction is **independent of apparent contact area** (for plastic deformation regime).
- Real (asperity) contact area is what matters: $A_r = \frac{W}{H}$.

#### **Factor 9: Surface Films & Contamination**
- **Oxide films** reduce metallic adhesion → lower μ.
- **Adsorbed water/vapour** can act as boundary lubricant → lower μ.
- **Contaminants** may increase or decrease μ unpredictably.

#### **Factor 10: Time, Dwell & Vibration**
- **Dwell time**: Longer static contact → larger junctions → higher static μ.
- **Vibration**: Induces **stick-slip** motion → oscillating μ, audible noise (squeal in brakes).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **KTU 2024 high-yield** — they appear in 80% of past exam questions on this module.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \frac{F}{W}$ | Coefficient of friction definition | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $F = \mu W$ | Amontons' Law of Friction | F in N, W in N |
| 3 | $\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s}$ | Bowden–Tabor model (adhesion) | $\tau_s$ = shear strength (Pa), $H_s$ = hardness (Pa) |
| 4 | $A_r = \frac{W}{H}$ | Real area of contact (plastic) | $A_r$ in m² |
| 5 | $F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$ | Friction as shear of junctions | F in N |
| 6 | $F = \tau_s \cdot A_r = \tau_s \cdot \frac{W}{H} = \left(\frac{\tau_s}{H}\right) W$ | Combined adhesion model | Shows $\mu = \tau_s / H$ |
| 7 | $\mu \propto W^{n-1}$ | General load–friction relation | $n = 1$ (Amontons); $n = 2/3$ (Hertzian elastic); $n < 1$ for contaminated surfaces |
| 8 | $\theta_{flash} = \frac{\mu W v}{4 a k}$ | Flash-temperature rise (Blok's equation) | $\theta$ in °C, $a$ = contact radius (m), $k$ = thermal conductivity |
| 9 | $\dot{W}_{friction} = \mu W v$ | Frictional power loss | Watts |
| 10 | $Q_{friction} = \mu W v \cdot t$ | Frictional heat generated | Joules |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Practical Application of "Factors Affecting Friction" |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Brakes** | Selecting pad–disc materials to maintain stable μ at high temp |
| **I.C. Engine Design** | Piston ring–cylinder friction: μ changes with speed, temp, oil film |
| **Rolling Bearings** | Selecting lubricant viscosity to shift operating point on Stribeck curve |
| **Manufacturing (Metal Forming)** | Controlling μ in deep drawing to prevent tearing |
| **Aerospace / Turbines** | High-temp tribology of Ni-superalloys; oxide film formation reduces μ |
| **Biomedical Implants** | UHMWPE-on-CoCr joint: μ stability under cyclic loading |
| **Mining / Heavy Machinery** | Earthmoving equipment: μ of soil-tool interface governs digging efficiency |

---

### 2.5 The Bowden–Tabor Two-Term Friction Model (Foundation Equation)

Bowden and Tabor (1950) decomposed the coefficient of friction into **two physically meaningful contributions**:

$$\mu = \mu_{adhesion} + \mu_{plowing}$$

where,

$$\mu_{adhesion} = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s} \quad ; \quad \mu_{plowing} = \text{function of (hardness, asperity geometry)}$$

- The **adhesion term** dominates for ductile, similar material pairs.
- The **plowing term** dominates when a hard asperity ploughs through a softer surface (e.g., grit in a bearing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When asked "list the factors affecting friction," always **classify your answer** under the two-term Bowden–Tabor framework. Examiners award full marks (5/5) only when both terms are addressed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bowden–Tabor Adhesion Model

**Assumption:** Asperity junctions deform **plastically** under normal load.

**Step 1:** The real area of contact $A_r$ equals the normal load divided by the **hardness** (yield pressure) of the softer material:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H_s}$$

*Conversion logic:* In plastic indentation, pressure = load / projected area = hardness. Rearranging yields projected (real) contact area.

**Step 2:** Each junction shears at the **shear strength** $\tau_s$ of the softer material when sliding occurs. The tangential force to shear all junctions is:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

*Conversion logic:* Shear stress × area = shear force.

**Step 3:** Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot \frac{W}{H_s}$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange using the definition $\mu = F/W$:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau_s \cdot W}{H_s \cdot W}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify (the W cancels cleanly):

$$\boxed{\mu_{adhesion} = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- For **steel-on-steel:** $\tau_s \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$, $H_s \approx 1500 \text{ MPa}$ → $\mu \approx 0.17$ (matches experimental μ ≈ 0.15–0.20).
- For **PTFE-on-steel:** $\tau_s \approx 10 \text{ MPa}$, $H_s \approx 50 \text{ MPa}$ → $\mu \approx 0.20$ (low because PTFE shear strength is very small).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Load-Friction Power Law (Generalized Amontons)

Empirically, many tribosystems obey:

$$F = C \cdot W^n$$

where $C$ is a constant and $n$ is the **load index**.

**Step 1:** Solve for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = C \cdot W^{n-1}$$

**Step 2:** Take the logarithm of both sides:

$$\log \mu = \log C + (n-1) \log W$$

**Step 3:** This is a straight line of slope $(n-1)$ on log-log axes.

**Special Cases:**

| Regime | Load Index (n) | μ Behavior | Physical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Perfectly plastic asperities (Amontons) | $n = 1$ | $\mu =$ constant | Classical Coulomb friction |
| Hertzian elastic asperities | $n = 2/3$ | $\mu$ decreases with W | Hard, polished surfaces |
| Elastic-plastic, contaminated | $n < 1$ | $\mu$ falls sharply with W | Contaminated / oxide-film surface |
| Brittle fracture at junctions | $n > 1$ | $\mu$ rises with W | Ceramic-on-ceramic |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** A common exam question gives two $(W, F)$ data points and asks the student to find $n$ from a log-log plot. Always show the linearization step.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Flash Temperature (Blok's Equation, Simplified)

When two surfaces slide under friction, the asperity junction experiences a sudden, localized temperature rise called **flash temperature** $\theta_{flash}$.

**Step 1:** Frictional power generated at the contact = $\mu W v$.

**Step 2:** Heat is conducted into the contacting solids (one solid, semi-infinite approximation):

$$\theta_{flash} \propto \frac{\mu W v}{a k}$$

**Step 3:** Blok's exact expression for a circular contact of radius $a$:

$$\theta_{flash} = \frac{\mu W v}{4 a k}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction
- $W$ = normal load (N)
- $v$ = sliding velocity (m/s)
- $a$ = contact radius (m)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)

**Step 4:** **Critical implication:** When $\theta_{flash}$ exceeds the **recrystallization temperature** of the material, severe wear occurs (scuffing/scoring failure in gears and cams).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem:** A steel pin slides on a steel disc. The applied normal load is **W = 100 N**, sliding velocity is **v = 2 m/s**, and the measured friction force is **F = 22 N**. The softer steel has hardness **H = 1.2 GPa** and thermal conductivity **k = 50 W/m·K**. The real contact area (from profilometry) is **A_r = 8.33 × 10⁻⁸ m²**, and the apparent contact radius is **a = 3 mm**. Compute:
> (a) Coefficient of friction
> (b) Shear strength of the junction (using Bowden–Tabor)
> (c) Flash temperature rise (Blok)
> (d) Frictional power loss

**Solution:**

**(a) Coefficient of friction:**

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{22}{100} = 0.22$$

**(b) Shear strength of the asperity junction:**

Using $F = \tau_s A_r$:

$$\tau_s = \frac{F}{A_r} = \frac{22}{8.33 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\tau_s = 2.64 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 264 \text{ MPa}$$

*Verification using Bowden–Tabor:* $\tau_s = \mu H = 0.22 \times 1.2 \times 10^9 = 2.64 \times 10^8$ Pa ✓ (matches!)

**(c) Flash temperature:**

$$\theta_{flash} = \frac{\mu W v}{4 a k} = \frac{0.22 \times 100 \times 2}{4 \times 0.003 \times 50}$$

$$\theta_{flash} = \frac{44}{0.6} = 73.3 \text{ °C}$$

**(d) Frictional power loss:**

$$\dot{W}_{friction} = \mu W v = 0.22 \times 100 \times 2 = 44 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Note (KTU pattern):** Step-by-step unit conversion (showing every W = N, J/s = W, etc.) fetches full 14 marks. Skipping intermediate substitutions loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect Magnitude of Each Factor on μ

| Factor | Effect on μ | Magnitude | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Load (rigid plastic) | None (Amontons) | — | μ constant |
| ↑ Load (elastic) | Decrease | Moderate | μ ↓ |
| ↑ Sliding velocity (dry) | First ↑, then ↓ | Strong | Peak at low v |
| ↑ Temperature (bulk) | Decrease | Moderate–Strong | μ ↓ |
| ↑ Surface roughness (too smooth) | Increase | Moderate | μ ↑ |
| ↑ Surface roughness (very rough) | Increase | Strong | μ ↑ |
| Oxide film presence | Decrease | Strong | μ ↓ |
| Adsorbed water vapour | Decrease | Weak | μ ↓ |
| ↑ Dwell time (static) | Increase (static μ) | Moderate | μ_s ↑ |
| Vibration | Causes oscillations | Strong | Unstable |
| ↑ Hardness of softer material | Decrease | Strong | μ ↓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Factors Affecting Friction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FACTORS AFFECTING FRICTION] --> B[INTRINSIC MATERIAL FACTORS]
    A --> C[OPERATIONAL MECHANICAL FACTORS]
    A --> D[ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICE FACTORS]

    B --> B1[Material Pair Adhesion]
    B --> B2[Surface Roughness]
    B --> B3[Hardness of Softer Material]
    B --> B4[Crystal Structure]

    C --> C1[Normal Load W]
    C --> C2[Sliding Velocity v]
    C --> C3[Interface Temperature]
    C --> C4[Apparent Contact Area]

    D --> D1[Oxide Films and Contamination]
    D --> D2[Adsorbed Humidity Layers]
    D --> D3[Dwell Time Effects]
    D --> D4[Vibration Stick Slip]

    B1 --> OUT[F Coefficient of Friction MU]
    B2 --> OUT
    B3 --> OUT
    B4 --> OUT
    C1 --> OUT
    C2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    C4 --> OUT
    D1 --> OUT
    D2 --> OUT
    D3 --> OUT
    D4 --> OUT
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bowden–Tabor Two-Term Friction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD[Normal Load W] --> ASP[Plastic Deformation of Asperities]
    ASP --> AREACALC[Real Contact Area Ar equals W over H]
    AREACALC --> SHEAR[Shear of Junctions at Strength Tau s]
    SHEAR --> FRICTION[Total Friction Force F equals Tau s times Ar plus Plowing Term]

    MATERIAL[Material Properties Tau s and H] --> SHEAR
    MATERIAL --> PLW[Plowing Component mu plowing]
    PLW --> FRICTION

    FRICTION --> MU[Overall Coefficient mu equals mu adhesion plus mu plowing]

    VEL[Sliding Velocity v] --> FLASH[Flash Temperature Theta flash]
    FLASH --> MATPROP[Modifies Tau s and H at Junction]
    MATPROP --> SHEAR
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Effect of Velocity on Coefficient of Friction (Stribeck-Style Behaviour for Dry Contact)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V0[Velocity equals 0 Static] --> MUs[High Static MU]
    MUs --> TRANS[Transition Zone Peak MU]
    TRANS --> DROP[Decreasing MU due to Thermal Softening]
    DROP --> STEADY[Steady State MU at High Velocity]
    STEADY --> RISE[Possible Re-rise due to Strain Rate Hardening]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Most dry sliding pairs exhibit this characteristic "rise-peak-fall" μ-vs-v curve. The peak typically occurs at **low velocity (≈ 0.001–0.1 m/s)** and represents the **static-to-kinetic transition**. KTU examiners frequently test this non-monotonic behaviour.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Engineering Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    APP[KTU Application Domains] --> AUTO[Automotive Brakes Pads and Discs]
    APP --> BEAR[Rolling Element Bearings]
    APP --> ENGINE[IC Engine Piston Rings]
    APP --> FORM[Metal Forming Dies]
    APP --> IMPL[Biomedical Implants]

    AUTO --> FAC1[Control MU at High Temperature]
    BEAR --> FAC2[Optimize MU via Lubricant Viscosity]
    ENGINE --> FAC3[Manage MU across Speed and Load Range]
    FORM --> FAC4[Minimize MU to Prevent Tearing]
    IMPL --> FAC5[Ensure Stable MU under Cyclic Loading]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
"State and briefly explain **Amontons' Laws of Friction**. Why is the coefficient of friction NOT considered a pure material property?"

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Amontons' First Law:** The friction force between two bodies is directly proportional to the normal load acting on them.

$$F \propto W \quad \Rightarrow \quad F = \mu W$$

**Amontons' Second Law:** The friction force is **independent of the apparent area of contact** between the two bodies.

**Why μ is not a pure material property (1 mark):**
The coefficient of friction depends on the entire **tribosystem** — surface roughness, load, velocity, temperature, environment, and material combination. Hence it is a **system response parameter**, not an intrinsic material constant. Bowden–Tabor model: $\mu = \tau_s / H_s$, which depends on operating conditions (temperature, contamination), not on the material alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**  
"Explain the effect of **surface roughness** on the coefficient of friction. What is meant by an *optimum roughness*?"

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For **rough surfaces** → asperity interlocking dominates → higher μ.  
For **highly polished (mirror-finish) surfaces** → real contact area becomes very large → stronger adhesion → higher μ.

Hence μ passes through a **minimum at an intermediate roughness** — this is called the **optimum roughness**. The optimum value typically lies in the range **R_a = 0.4 to 1.0 μm** for boundary-lubricated contacts.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** List and explain any **five factors affecting friction** with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch and necessary equations, derive the **Bowden–Tabor adhesion model** of friction.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The five factors are:

**1. Nature of the Material Pair:**
Friction arises from cold-welded junctions at asperity contacts. Material pairs with **high mutual solubility** (Cu–Ni, Al–Al) exhibit higher friction due to strong adhesive bonding. **Example:** Steel-on-steel μ ≈ 0.6 (clean, dry), whereas PTFE-on-steel μ ≈ 0.04.

**2. Normal Load:**
According to Amontons' law, $F = \mu W$. For perfectly plastic asperities, μ remains constant with load. However, for elastic contacts, $\mu$ decreases with $W$ as $\mu \propto W^{n-1}$ where $n < 1$.

**3. Sliding Velocity:**
Velocity affects flash temperature and strain-rate sensitivity. Most metals show **μ rising to a peak** at low velocity (≈ 0.01 m/s) and then **decreasing** due to thermal softening of asperities.

**4. Surface Films & Contamination:**
Oxide films (Al₂O₃ on aluminum, Fe₃O₄ on steel) prevent metallic contact → lower μ. Adsorbed water vapour or organic contamination can also reduce μ.

**5. Temperature:**
Higher temperature reduces hardness → lower μ (in plastic regime). However, excessive temperature may destroy lubricant films → transition to scuffing → μ rises sharply.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Assumption:** Asperity junctions deform **plastically** under the normal load.

**Step 1:** The real contact area $A_r$ is related to load $W$ and hardness $H_s$:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H_s}$$

**Step 2:** The friction force equals the shear strength $\tau_s$ of the softer material multiplied by the real contact area:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot A_r$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$F = \tau_s \cdot \frac{W}{H_s}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging using $\mu = F / W$:

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- Higher shear strength of junction → higher μ (more energy to shear bonds).
- Higher hardness → lower μ (less real contact area for the same load).

**Numerical Check:** Steel-on-steel: $\tau_s \approx 250$ MPa, $H_s \approx 1500$ MPa → $\mu \approx 0.17$, which agrees with experimental data (≈ 0.15–0.20).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the effect of **load, sliding velocity, and temperature** on the coefficient of friction. Support your answer with appropriate diagrams.

**(b) [7 marks]** A steel pin (hardness 800 MPa) slides on a steel disc under a normal load of **200 N**. The measured real contact area is **2.5 × 10⁻⁷ m²**. If the shear strength of the junction is **180 MPa**, determine:
- (i) Coefficient of friction
- (ii) Friction force
- (iii) Comment on the validity of Amontons' law for this contact.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**1. Effect of Load (W):**
For rigid plastic asperities: $\mu$ is **constant** (Amontons' law).  
For elastic Hertzian contacts: $\mu \propto W^{n-1}$ with $n = 2/3$, hence $\mu$ **decreases** slowly with $W$.

**2. Effect of Sliding Velocity (v):**
Most metals show a **non-monotonic** curve:
- At very low velocity, $\mu_s > \mu_k$ (static peak).
- μ rises to a peak at ≈ 0.01–0.1 m/s.
- μ then **decreases** at higher velocities due to flash-temperature-induced softening.

**3. Effect of Temperature (T):**
- Bulk temperature rise → reduced hardness → reduced μ (most common).
- Flash temperature at asperity → localized melting → transient μ drop.
- Critical temperature where lubricant film fails → μ rises sharply (scuffing).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Hardness $H_s = 800$ MPa $= 800 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Real contact area $A_r = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²
- Shear strength $\tau_s = 180$ MPa $= 180 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Normal load $W = 200$ N

**(i) Coefficient of friction (Bowden–Tabor model):**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau_s}{H_s} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{800 \times 10^6}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.225}$$

**(ii) Friction force:**

$$F = \tau_s \cdot A_r = 180 \times 10^6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{F = 45 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification:** Using $F = \mu W = 0.225 \times 200 = 45$ N ✓ (consistent)

**(iii) Validity of Amontons' Law:**

Amontons' law is valid here because:
- The asperities are deforming **plastically** (load index $n \approx 1$).
- $\mu = \tau_s / H_s = 0.225$, which is **constant** (does not depend on W).
- Therefore, $F = \mu W$ holds, and the friction is proportional to load.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Topic: Factors Affecting Friction):**
> 1. **Treating μ as a material constant** → Loses 2 marks. Always state μ is a *system* response.
> 2. **Ignoring flash temperature** in velocity-effect answers → Loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong sign in Amontons' derivation** → Writing $F = W/\mu$ instead of $F = \mu W$ → Loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping unit consistency** in numerical problems → Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing static μ ($\mu_s$) with kinetic μ ($\mu_k$)** → Static μ is always higher; examiners test this explicitly.
> 6. **Failing to mention Bowden–Tabor two-term model** (adhesion + plowing) → Loses 2 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Writing "μ is independent of apparent area" without saying "for plastic contacts"** → Incomplete answer; deduct 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Factors Affecting Friction (Module 1):**

- ☐ **μ is NOT a material constant**; it is a tribosystem parameter.
- ☐ **Amontons' Law:** $F = \mu W$ (load proportional) — valid only for plastic contacts.
- ☐ **Amontons' Second Law:** Friction independent of apparent area (valid in plastic regime).
- ☐ **Bowden–Tabor Model:** $\mu = \tau_s / H_s$ — foundation of friction theory.
- ☐ **Two-term model:** $\mu = \mu_{adhesion} + \mu_{plowing}$.
- ☐ **Load effect:** $\mu \propto W^{n-1}$ — $n=1$ (plastic), $n=2/3$ (elastic), $n<1$ (contaminated).
- ☐ **Velocity effect:** Non-monotonic — rises to peak, then falls.
- ☐ **Temperature effect:** Generally reduces μ via thermal softening, but excessive temp causes scuffing.
- ☐ **Surface roughness:** Optimum roughness gives minimum μ (boundary-lubrication regime).
- ☐ **Surface films:** Oxide, adsorbed water, and contaminants all reduce μ.
- ☐ **Crystal structure:** HCP < FCC < BCC (typical μ ordering).
- ☐ **Hardness:** Higher H → lower μ (approximately).
- ☐ **Dwell time:** Longer dwell → higher static μ.
- ☐ **Flash temperature equation (Blok):** $\theta_{flash} = \mu W v / (4ak)$.
- ☐ **Frictional power loss:** $\dot{W} = \mu W v$ (Watts).
- ☐ **Mutual solubility:** High solubility → high μ (cold-welding).
- ☐ **Always classify answers** under: Intrinsic / Operational / Environmental categories.
- ☐ **Real vs. Apparent contact area:** Real area $A_r = W/H$; apparent area is irrelevant for plastic contacts.
- ☐ **Stick-slip phenomenon:** Caused by static μ > kinetic μ, leads to vibration and noise.
- ☐ **Material selection rule:** For low μ → use dissimilar metals with low mutual solubility (e.g., steel–bronze, steel–PTFE).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theories of Friction: Adhesion, Deformation, and Plowing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Theories of Friction: Adhesion, Deformation, and Plowing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Friction Theories

In **Industrial Tribology**, the macroscopic force resisting relative motion between two contacting bodies is known as the **frictional force** $F$. Since the 15th-century empirical laws of Leonardo da Vinci and Amontons, tribologists have sought to explain the *microscopic origins* of this macroscopic force. The three classical mechanisms — **Adhesion**, **Deformation**, and **Plowing** — collectively form the foundation of modern friction theory, originally formalized by **F.P. Bowden and D. Tabor (1950)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1):**
> The total coefficient of friction $\mu$ between two rough surfaces is the *superposition* of three microscopic phenomena:
> $$\mu_{total} = \mu_{adhesion} + \mu_{deformation} + \mu_{plowing}$$

### 1.2 The Three Theories — A Quick Snapshot

| **Theory** | **Origin of Frictional Resistance** | **Dominant Regime** |
|---|---|---|
| Adhesion | Shear of microscopic metallic junctions at real contact spots | Lubricated, smooth, ductile metals |
| Deformation | Energy lost in plastic/elastic deformation of asperities | Hard, rough surfaces, high loads |
| Plowing | Hard asperities digging trenches in a softer counterface | Asperity hardness ratio $\gg 1$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Velcro Carpet"**
> Imagine pushing a stiff wire brush (the *hard surface*) sideways across a soft rubber mat (the *soft surface*).
> 1. **Adhesion:** Microscopic glue-like welds form at the contact tips — you have to *shear* these tiny welds to slide. *(Like unbuttoning a tight button.)*
> 2. **Deformation:** The rubber *bends and squishes* under each bristle tip as it passes — energy is wasted. *(Like dragging a foot through wet sand.)*
> 3. **Plowing:** The sharp wire tips actually *carve little furrows* into the rubber — a true cutting action. *(Like dragging a fork across soft cheese.)*
>
> Real friction = Button-strength + Sand-drag + Cheese-furrow. 

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Units Used in Friction Theory

- **Hardness ($H$)** — Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation. Units: **Pa** (MPa in practice). For steel: $H \approx 2000 \text{ MPa}$.
- **Shear Strength of Junction ($\tau$)** — Shear stress at the welded interface. Units: **Pa**. For metals: $\tau \approx 0.1 H$.
- **Yield Pressure / Yield Strength ($\sigma_y$, $p_y$)** — Onset of plastic flow. Units: **Pa**.
- **Real Area of Contact ($A_r$)** — Sum of microscopic junction areas. Units: **m²**. Crucially: $A_r \ll A_{apparent}$ (nominal area).
- **Apparent Area of Contact ($A_a$)** — The macroscopic/geometric area. Units: **m²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Adhesion Theory (Bowden & Tabor)

### 2.1.1 Core Concept
Surfaces, no matter how finely polished, are **rough at the microscopic level**. When two such surfaces are pressed together, contact occurs only at the tips of asperities. The actual contact area ($A_r$) may be **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶** times the apparent area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bowden–Tabor Hypothesis:** *Friction is the force required to shear the adhesive junctions formed at real contact spots.*

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Normal load $W$** is supported by plastic deformation of asperities at $A_r$.
2. **Yield criterion:** Average contact pressure $\approx H$ (hardness). Hence $W = H \cdot A_r \Rightarrow A_r = W / H$.
3. **Tangential force** needed to slide = shear strength of junction $\times$ real area: $F_{adh} = \tau \cdot A_r$.
4. **Substituting:** $F_{adh} = \tau \cdot (W / H)$.
5. **Coefficient of friction:** $\mu_{adh} = F/W = \tau / H$.

### 2.1.3 Key Insight
This naturally explains **Amontons' First Law** (friction is independent of apparent area), because $A_r$ depends only on $W$, not on $A_a$.

## 2.2 The Deformation Theory

### 2.2.1 Core Concept
When asperities are *elastic* (or *elastic–perfectly plastic*), energy is dissipated in deforming them. The energy is stored in the leading half and partially recovered in the trailing half — the net loss becomes friction.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Asperity treated as a hemisphere or cone of radius $r$ contacting a plane.
2. For **elastic contact** (Hertzian): $A_r = \pi \cdot (W \cdot r / E^*)^{2/3}$ — depends on $W^{2/3}$, *not* linearly on $W$.
3. Hence for purely elastic asperities: $\mu \propto W^{-1/3}$ — friction *decreases* with load.
4. For **plastic asperities**: $\mu_{def} = \tau_y / \sigma_y \approx \text{const}$ (similar to adhesion).
5. The deformation component becomes dominant in **grinding, lapping, and polishing** where $A_r$ is governed by elastic/plastic indentation mechanics.

## 2.3 The Plowing Theory

### 2.3.1 Core Concept
When a *hard* asperity (or abrasive particle) slides against a *softer* surface, it *plows* a groove. The horizontal force needed to displace material ahead of the asperity contributes additional friction.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Logic
1. Hard asperity has geometry approximated as a **cone of semi-angle $\theta$**.
2. It indents to depth $h$ and advances, displacing volume $V$ of material.
3. Vertical force (load) = $H \cdot A_{proj} = H \cdot \pi h^2 \tan^2\theta$.
4. Horizontal force (plowing) = $K \cdot H \cdot A_{proj}$, where $K$ is a **geometric (plowing) factor**.
5. The factor $K$ depends on the *attack angle* and material response:
   - **Perfectly plastic:** $K \approx (2/\pi) \cot \theta$.
   - **Work-hardening materials:** $K$ is larger.
   - For conical asperity: $K \approx 1.0$ to $2.0$.

### 2.3.3 Where Plowing Dominates
- **Abrasive wear** in mining and machining.
- **Three-body abrasion** with hard wear debris entrapped at the interface.
- **Hardness ratio** $H_{asperity} / H_{surface} \gg 1.5$.

## 2.4 The Total Friction Model

The classical total friction equation is:

$$F_{total} = F_{adhesion} + F_{deformation} + F_{plowing}$$

In terms of coefficient of friction:

$$\mu_{total} = \frac{\tau}{H} + \frac{F_{deformation}}{W} + \frac{2 \cot \theta}{\pi}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Save This Table — High-Yield for KTU University Exams**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Variables / Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real contact area (plastic) | $A_r = W / H$ | $W$ = normal load, $H$ = hardness | $\text{m}^2$ |
| Real contact area (elastic, sphere) | $A_r = \pi (W r / E^*)^{2/3}$ | $r$ = asperity radius, $E^*$ = effective modulus | $\text{m}^2$ |
| Adhesive friction force | $F_{adh} = \tau \cdot A_r$ | $\tau$ = junction shear strength | $\text{N}$ |
| Adhesive coefficient | $\mu_{adh} = \tau / H$ | Ratio of shear strength to hardness | dimensionless |
| Plowing force (cone) | $F_{plow} = K \cdot H \cdot A_{proj}$ | $K$ = geometric plowing factor | $\text{N}$ |
| Plowing coefficient (cone) | $\mu_{plow} = (2 / \pi) \cot \theta$ | $\theta$ = cone semi-angle | dimensionless |
| Effective modulus | $1/E^* = (1 - \nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1 - \nu_2^2)/E_2$ | $E_i, \nu_i$ = Young's modulus, Poisson's ratio | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Yield criterion (asperity) | $p_{avg} \approx H$ | Mean contact pressure $\approx$ hardness | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Shear–hardness relation | $\tau \approx 0.1 H$ | Empirical for metals | $\text{Pa}$ |
| Total coefficient of friction | $\mu_{total} = \mu_{adh} + \mu_{def} + \mu_{plow}$ | Superposition principle | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing & Gear Design:** Predicts frictional heat, lubricant film requirements, and EHL transitions.
- **Brake Pad Formulation:** Balancing adhesion (friction level) vs plowing (wear rate).
- **Cutting Tool Design:** Plowing component governs **cutting force** in machining.
- **Surface Texturing:** Laser micro-dimples reduce real area $A_r$ and disrupt continuous junctions, lowering $\mu_{adh}$.
- **Tribology of MEMS:** At micro-scale, $A_r / A_a \to 1$ and adhesion dominates — *stiction* becomes critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Coefficient of Friction from Adhesion Theory

**Given:** Two metallic surfaces in contact, real contact area $A_r$, normal load $W$, junction shear strength $\tau$, surface hardness $H$.

**Step 1.** Apply the *fully plastic asperity* model. At asperity tips, the material yields, so the local contact pressure equals the indentation hardness $H$:

$$p_{contact} = \frac{W}{A_r} = H$$

**Step 2.** Solve for the real area of contact:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{H}$$

**Step 3.** When the upper body slides, the junctions must be sheared at the interface. The tangential (frictional) force is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A_r$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $A_r$ from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$F = \tau \cdot \frac{W}{H}$$

**Step 5.** Define the coefficient of friction as $\mu = F / W$:

$$\mu_{adh} = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau \cdot (W/H)}{W} = \frac{\tau}{H}$$

**Result:** $\mu_{adh} = \tau / H$ — a *constant*, independent of $W$ and of $A_a$. This is the theoretical basis of Amontons' Laws.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Plowing Force for a Conical Asperity

**Given:** A rigid conical asperity of semi-angle $\theta$ slides on a perfectly plastic surface of hardness $H$, indenting to depth $h$. Find the plowing force $F_{plow}$.

**Step 1.** The projected (cross-sectional) area of the cone–plane contact at depth $h$ is:

$$A_{proj} = \pi \cdot (h \tan \theta)^2 = \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta$$

**Step 2.** The vertical (normal) reaction force, supported by plastic indentation pressure $H$, is:

$$W = H \cdot A_{proj} = \pi H h^2 \tan^2 \theta$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the indentation depth $h$ in terms of $W$:

$$h^2 = \frac{W}{\pi H \tan^2 \theta} \Rightarrow h = \sqrt{\frac{W}{\pi H}} \cdot \frac{1}{\tan \theta}$$

**Step 4.** The horizontal plowing force is the projection of the plastic flow pressure on the inclined face. For a 2D wedge, the classical result (from slip-line field theory) gives the geometric plowing factor $K$:

$$K = \frac{2}{\pi} \cot \theta$$

> [!NOTE]
> This factor $K = 2 \cot \theta / \pi$ arises from the upper-bound plastic flow analysis (Hill's solution for a wedge sliding on a plastic half-space). It is valid for a *2D wedge* in plane strain.

**Step 5.** The plowing force is therefore:

$$F_{plow} = K \cdot H \cdot A_{proj} = \frac{2 \cot \theta}{\pi} \cdot H \cdot \pi h^2 \tan^2 \theta$$

**Step 6.** Simplify the product $\cot \theta \cdot \tan^2 \theta = \tan \theta$:

$$F_{plow} = 2 H h^2 \tan \theta$$

**Step 7.** Express the plowing coefficient of friction:

$$\mu_{plow} = \frac{F_{plow}}{W} = \frac{2 H h^2 \tan \theta}{\pi H h^2 \tan^2 \theta} = \frac{2 \cot \theta}{\pi}$$

**Result:** $\mu_{plow} = (2 / \pi) \cot \theta$.

**Physical interpretation:** Sharper asperities (small $\theta$) produce *larger* plowing friction. For $\theta = 45°$: $\mu_{plow} = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$. For $\theta = 60°$: $\mu_{plow} \approx 0.367$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Friction Coefficient (Combined Model)

**Given:** A rough, dry, metallic sliding contact. We wish to combine the three components.

**Step 1.** Write the total horizontal force:

$$F_{total} = F_{adhesion} + F_{deformation} + F_{plowing}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the three component forms:

$$F_{total} = \tau \cdot A_r + \beta \cdot W^{2/3} \cdot (r / E^*)^{2/3} + K \cdot H \cdot A_{proj}$$

where $\beta$ is a geometric constant for the elastic deformation term (carried symbolically here for brevity).

**Step 3.** For *fully plastic* asperities, the deformation term is absorbed into the shear term (since yielding at $H$ already accounts for plastic work). For *elastic* asperities, the second term dominates when $W$ is small.

**Step 4.** Divide throughout by $W$ to obtain:

$$\mu_{total} = \frac{\tau}{H} + \beta \left(\frac{r}{E^* W}\right)^{2/3} \cdot \text{(elastic def.)} + \frac{2 \cot \theta}{\pi}$$

**Step 5.** For the *classical Bowden–Tabor fully plastic, non-abrasive* regime, the deformation term is small and plowing is negligible, yielding the famous result:

$$\mu \approx \frac{\tau}{H} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.5$$

for clean metal-on-metal contacts.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does Each Theory Dominate?

| **Surface Condition** | **Dominant Mechanism** | **Typical $\mu$ Range** | **Key Parameter** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clean, ductile metals, lubricated | Adhesion | $0.05 – 0.2$ | $\tau / H$ |
| Dry, hard metals (steel on steel) | Adhesion + Plowing | $0.4 – 0.8$ | $\tau / H$ + $K$ |
| Abrasive grit (sandpaper, grinding) | Plowing | $0.5 – 1.0$ | $K = (2/\pi)\cot\theta$ |
| Smooth, elastic contacts (glass) | Deformation | $0.1 – 0.4$ | $r / E^*$ |
| Polymer on metal | Adhesion (high $\tau$) | $0.2 – 0.6$ | Surface energy, $\gamma$ |
| Hard ceramic on soft metal | Plowing | $0.6 – 1.2$ | $H_{ratio}$ |
| Boundary lubricated | Adhesion (reduced $\tau$) | $0.05 – 0.15$ | Lubricant film $\tau_f$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Friction Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Frictional Force F] --> B[Adhesion Component]
    A --> C[Deformation Component]
    A --> D[Plowing Component]
    B --> B1[Shear of metallic junctions at real contact area Ar]
    B --> B2[Equation Fadh = tau times Ar]
    B --> B3[Mu adh = tau by H]
    C --> C1[Elastic Hertzian deformation of asperities]
    C --> C2[Plastic dissipation in asperity tips]
    C --> C3[Mu def proportional to W power negative one third]
    D --> D1[Hard asperity digs groove in soft surface]
    D --> D2[Geometric factor K depends on cone angle theta]
    D --> D3[Fplow = K times H times Aproj]
    A --> E[Total Coefficient of Friction]
    E --> E1[Mu total = Mu adh + Mu def + Mu plow]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Friction Theory Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Sliding Contact]) --> Q1{Are surfaces clean and ductile?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Adhesion[Adhesion Theory Dominates]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is hardness ratio greater than 1.5?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Plowing[Plowing Theory Dominates]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is contact in elastic regime?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Deform[Deformation Theory Dominates]
    Q3 -- No --> Combined[All Three Contribute]
    Adhesion --> Eq1[Mu equals tau by H]
    Plowing --> Eq2[Mu equals 2 cot theta by pi]
    Deform --> Eq3[Mu proportional to W power negative one third]
    Combined --> Eq4[Mu total = Mu adh + Mu def + Mu plow]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Asperity-Level Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    Surface1[Upper Hard Surface] -- Asperity Tips --> Junction[Real Contact Junctions]
    Junction -- Shear --> ShearRes[Friction Force Fadh]
    Surface1 -- Indents --> LowerSoft[Lower Soft Surface]
    LowerSoft -- Deforms Elastically --> DefEnergy[Deformation Energy Loss]
    LowerSoft -- Material Displaced --> Groove[Plowed Groove]
    Groove -- Cutting Resistance --> PlowForce[Friction Force Fplow]
    ShearRes --> Total[Total Friction Ftotal]
    DefEnergy --> Total
    PlowForce --> Total
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The block diagrams above represent the *functional architecture* of the friction model. In practice, the three components act *simultaneously* at every asperity contact, and their relative magnitudes depend on material, geometry, and load.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** *State Amontons' laws of friction and explain how the adhesion theory accounts for the independence of friction force from apparent area of contact.* (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
Amontons' two laws are:
1. The friction force $F$ is **proportional to the normal load $W$** (i.e., $\mu = F/W = \text{const}$).
2. The friction force is **independent of the apparent (nominal) area of contact**.

According to the Bowden–Tabor adhesion theory, contact occurs only at the tips of asperities, and the *real* area of contact is $A_r = W / H$. As load increases, the real area grows linearly with $W$, but the apparent area has no role in determining $A_r$. The friction force $F = \tau \cdot A_r = \tau W / H$ therefore depends on $W$ but not on the apparent area, satisfying both Amontons' laws. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2.** *Distinguish between adhesion and plowing components of friction. In what type of contact is plowing dominant?* (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Adhesion** | **Plowing** |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Shearing of welded junctions | Hard asperity cuts through soft surface |
| Force equation | $F = \tau \cdot A_r$ | $F = K \cdot H \cdot A_{proj}$ |
| Dominant when | Smooth, ductile, clean surfaces | Hardness ratio $H_{hard}/H_{soft} \gg 1$ |

Plowing is dominant in **abrasive contacts** such as grinding, lapping, cutting, and three-body abrasion with entrapped hard particles. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Derive the coefficient of friction from the **Bowden–Tabor adhesion theory**. State clearly the assumptions used. Show that the result is independent of the apparent area of contact. (7 marks)

**(b)** A steel block of hardness **800 MPa** slides on a steel surface of equal hardness under a normal load of **500 N**. The junction shear strength is **80 MPa**. The real area of contact is estimated to be **$1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$**. Compute the frictional force and the coefficient of friction. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**
- Surfaces are rough at the microscopic level; contact occurs at asperity tips.
- Asperity tips deform *plastically* under load.
- Each junction has shear strength $\tau$.
- Average contact pressure at asperities = hardness $H$.

**Derivation:**
1. $W = H \cdot A_r \Rightarrow A_r = W / H$.
2. $F = \tau \cdot A_r = \tau W / H$.
3. $\mu = F / W = \tau / H$.
4. Since $A_r$ depends only on $W$ and $H$ (material properties), and *not* on $A_a$ (geometric area), the friction is independent of apparent area. $\square$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often *conflate hardness $H$ with yield strength $\sigma_y$*. Remember — in tribology, $H$ is the *indentation hardness* (≈ $3 \sigma_y$ for metals), not the uniaxial yield strength. Using the wrong value can cost 1–2 marks.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $H = 800 \text{ MPa} = 800 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $\tau = 80 \text{ MPa} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $W = 500 \text{ N}$, $A_r = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$.

**Step 1.** Compute frictional force directly from shear of junctions:

$$F = \tau \cdot A_r = (80 \times 10^6) \cdot (1.0 \times 10^{-6}) = 80 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2.** Compute coefficient of friction:

$$\mu = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{80}{500} = 0.16$$

**Step 3.** Verification via the adhesion formula:

$$\mu_{theory} = \frac{\tau}{H} = \frac{80}{800} = 0.10$$

**Comment:** The experimentally inferred $\mu = 0.16$ is *higher* than the theoretical $\mu = 0.10$ from pure adhesion. The excess ($\approx 0.06$) must be attributed to the **plowing** and **deformation** components, indicating that surface roughness and asperity geometry contribute significantly in this case.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **plowing theory of friction**. Derive the expression for plowing coefficient of friction for a conical asperity on a perfectly plastic surface. (7 marks)

**(b)** A conical hard asperity of semi-angle **$\theta = 60°$** plows a soft metal surface of hardness **500 MPa** under a normal load of **200 N**. Calculate the plowing force and the projected contact area. Use the slip-line field result $K = (2/\pi)\cot\theta$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Explanation:**
When a hard asperity (or abrasive particle) slides over a softer surface, the asperity penetrates to a depth $h$ and pushes material aside, forming a groove. The horizontal force required to do this is the **plowing force** $F_{plow}$. This is distinct from the shear of junctions; here, the asperity is *much harder* than the substrate and the resistance is dominated by *plastic flow* of the displaced material.

**Derivation** (as in Section 3.2 of these notes):
1. Projected area: $A_{proj} = \pi h^2 \tan^2\theta$.
2. Vertical force: $W = H \cdot A_{proj}$.
