> **[b] Chemisorption (Chemical Adsorption):**
> - Involves **chemical bond formation** (strong, $\approx 1$–$10$ eV/bond)
> - Typically a **monolayer** strongly anchored
> - Stable at **moderate temperatures** ($70$–$200$ °C)
> - Higher film strength than physisorbed films

### Step 3: Chemical Reaction Film Formation
Under the combined action of high contact temperature (**flash temperature** at asperity junctions can reach $500$–$1000$ °C momentarily) and high local pressure, the additive molecules **react chemically with the metal substrate** to form **sacrificial reaction films** (e.g., iron sulfide, iron chloride, iron phosphate). These films are **low-shear-strength compounds** that prevent direct metal-to-metal welding.

### Step 4: Load Support by Boundary Film
The boundary film supports the asperity contact load by virtue of its **shear strength $\tau_f$** being much lower than the shear strength of the bare metal $\tau_m$. The boundary friction coefficient can be approximated as:

$$\mu_b \approx \frac{\tau_f}{p_y}$$

where $p_y$ is the **yield pressure** of the asperity material.

### Step 5: Wear and Film Removal (Steady State)
During sliding, the boundary film is continuously **plowed away** and **re-formed**. The dynamic balance between **film formation rate** and **film removal rate** determines the steady-state friction and wear.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all boundary-lubrication formulas expected in KTU 2024 End Semester Examinations. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Description / Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ | Lambda ratio; defines lubrication regime | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\mu_b = \alpha \mu_a + (1-\alpha)\mu_f$ | Friction decomposition across asperity-fluid contact | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\mu_b = \tau_f / p_y$ | Boundary friction coefficient (simplified) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\theta = \dfrac{KP}{1+KP}$ | **Langmuir adsorption isotherm** (fractional surface coverage) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\ln P = \ln K - \Delta H_{ads} / (RT)$ | **Temkin-type integrated form** (linear in 1/T) | — |
| 6 | $W_c = A_r \cdot p_y$ | Load capacity of asperity contact | N |
| 7 | $\tau = \tau_0 + k \cdot p$ | Simple shear-stress model for boundary film (Bowden–Tabor) | Pa |
| 8 | $\mu = s + \alpha(1-s)$ | Friction with **shear-strength ratio** $s = \tau_f/\tau_m$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{flash} = \dfrac{\mu F_N v}{4 a J K}$ | Flash temperature rise at an asperity (K) | K |
| 10 | $k_w = K \cdot F_N \cdot s$ | **Archard wear coefficient** form for boundary film | m³/N·m |
| 11 | $r_f = \dfrac{\mu_b}{\mu_d} = \dfrac{\text{static friction}}{\text{kinetic friction}}$ | **Friction ratio** (stiction index) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $h_{min} = 3.63 R U^{0.68} G^{0.49} W^{-0.073} (1 - e^{-0.68k})$ | **Hamrock–Dowson EHL minimum film thickness** | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Legend for Boundary Lubrication:**
> - $\theta$ = fractional surface coverage, $P$ = adsorbate pressure/concentration
> - $K$ = adsorption equilibrium constant
> - $\Delta H_{ads}$ = enthalpy of adsorption
> - $R$ = universal gas constant ($8.314$ J/mol·K)
> - $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
> - $s$ = shear-strength ratio
> - $F_N$ = normal force, $v$ = sliding velocity
> - $a$ = asperity contact radius, $J$ = Joule's equivalent
> - $K$ = thermal conductivity
> - $R$ = effective radius, $U$ = speed parameter, $G$ = material parameter, $W$ = load parameter, $k$ = ellipticity parameter

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility of the lambda ratio** $\lambda$:
> - Used by **OEMs** (e.g., automotive, aerospace) to design lubricant grades
> - SKF uses $\lambda < 1$ to flag **boundary lubrication risk** in bearing selection software
> - Helps determine whether **synthetic lubricants** or **EP/AW additives** are mandatory

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The boundary lubrication regime dominates the entire operational life of **starting and stopping machinery**. In an automotive engine, for example, $80$–$90$ % of all wear occurs during **cold start**, which is by definition a boundary-lubrication condition (insufficient hydrodynamic film at low rpm). The correct formulation of **engine oil with ZDDP (zinc dialkyldithiophosphate)** creates a sacrificial phosphate-glass tribofilm that protects cam-follower contacts during these critical moments. Similarly, in **hypoid gearboxes** of rear-axle drives, the combination of high sliding and high contact stress makes EP additives (sulfur–phosphorus compounds) essential.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friction Coefficient in Boundary Lubrication (Bowden–Tabor Model)

The classical **Bowden–Tabor model** treats the boundary film as a thin, easily sheared layer interposed between the metal asperities. The total friction force $F$ is the sum of:
- The **shear force of the boundary film** (over real area of contact)
- The **plowing component** (often neglected for smooth surfaces)

**Starting assumptions:**

The real (microscopic) area of contact $A_r$ is determined by the **plastic deformation** of asperities under the normal load $W$:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y}$$

where $p_y$ is the **yield pressure** of the softer asperity material.

The **shear force** required to slide the contact is:

$$F = A_r \cdot \tau_f$$

where $\tau_f$ is the **shear strength of the boundary film**.

**Combining the two equations:**

$$F = \left(\frac{W}{p_y}\right) \tau_f$$

**Dividing both sides by the normal load W:**

$$\frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau_f}{p_y}$$

The **left-hand side** is the **coefficient of friction $\mu$**, therefore:

$$\mu_b = \frac{\tau_f}{p_y}$$

**Introducing the shear-strength ratio $s$:**

$$s = \frac{\tau_f}{\tau_m}, \quad \text{where } \tau_m \text{ = shear strength of bare metal}$$

Since $p_y \approx 2 \tau_m$ (von Mises / Tresca yield criterion for metals):

$$\mu_b = \frac{\tau_f}{p_y} = \frac{s \cdot \tau_m}{2 \tau_m} = \frac{s}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_b = \frac{s}{2} \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10}$$

This is the **fundamental Bowden–Tabor equation** and explains why effective boundary lubrication can reduce friction to $\approx 0.05$ even when the surfaces are in direct contact.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm Applied to Boundary Films

Consider a metallic surface with $N$ total adsorption sites. Let $\theta$ be the **fraction of sites occupied** by adsorbed lubricant molecules. The rate of adsorption is proportional to the **bare fraction $(1-\theta)$** and the **adsorbate pressure $P$**, with proportionality constant $k_a$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a (1-\theta) P$$

The rate of desorption is proportional to the **covered fraction $\theta$**, with proportionality constant $k_d$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

**At equilibrium (steady state):**

$$k_a (1-\theta) P = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

**Define the adsorption equilibrium constant $K = k_a / k_d$:**

$$\theta = \frac{K P}{1 + K P}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{KP}{1 + KP}}$$

This is the **Langmuir isotherm**. The constants:
- $K \to 0$: weak adsorption (poor boundary film)
- $K \to \infty$: complete monolayer coverage (strong boundary film)
- At $KP = 1$: $\theta = 0.5$ (half-coverage point)

**Temperature dependence via van't Hoff relation:**

$$K(T) = K_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{RT}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}\right) = \ln K_0 + \ln P - \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{RT}$$

A plot of $\ln[\theta/(1-\theta)]$ versus $1/T$ is **linear** with slope $-\Delta H_{ads}/R$, allowing experimental determination of the heat of adsorption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Asperity Load Fraction $\alpha$ in Mixed/Boundary Lubrication

The total normal load $W$ is supported jointly by the **asperity contacts** and the **fluid film**:

$$W = W_a + W_f$$

where $W_a$ is the load carried by asperities and $W_f$ is the load carried by the fluid film. The **asperity load fraction** is:

$$\alpha = \frac{W_a}{W}$$

The average contact pressure $p_a$ on the asperities and the fluid pressure $p_f$ are related by:

$$W = p_a A_r + p_f (A - A_r)$$

where $A$ is the apparent (macroscopic) contact area and $A_r$ is the real (asperity) contact area.

**Using the Reynolds equation for fluid pressure and the yield criterion for asperity pressure:**

For a smooth EHL contact, the film thickness scales as $h \propto U^{0.7} W^{-0.1}$. As load $W$ increases, $h$ decreases, and asperity contact becomes more likely. A widely used semi-empirical relation (Tallian, 1967) is:

$$\alpha = 1 - \exp\left[-c \left(\frac{h}{\sigma}\right)^\beta\right]$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 1 - \exp\left[-c \cdot \lambda^\beta\right]}$$

where $c$ and $\beta$ are **empirical constants** (typical values $c \approx 0.5$–$1.0$, $\beta \approx 1.5$–$2.0$).

**Limiting cases:**
- $\lambda \to 0$ (no film): $\alpha \to 1$ (all load on asperities — pure boundary)
- $\lambda \to \infty$ (thick film): $\alpha \to 0$ (all load on fluid — full film)
- $\lambda = 1$ (mixed): $\alpha \approx 0.3$–$0.5$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Flash Temperature at an Asperity Junction

When two asperities slide against each other, frictional heat is generated at the junction. The **flash temperature rise** $\Delta T$ is the transient temperature spike at the contact:

The heat generated per unit time is:

$$Q = \mu F_N v$$

where $F_N$ is the normal force on the asperity and $v$ is the sliding velocity.

The heat is conducted into a hemisphere of radius $a$ (the contact radius) over a time $t = a/v$ (the residence time):

$$Q \cdot t = \mu F_N a$$

The thermal mass of the hemisphere is $\frac{2}{3} \pi a^3 \rho c_p$ and the heat dissipated is:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\mu F_N a}{J \cdot \frac{2}{3} \pi a^3 \rho c_p}$$

Combining with thermal conductivity $K = k_{th} \rho c_p$ (where $k_{th}$ is thermal diffusivity):

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{flash} = \frac{\mu F_N v}{4 a J K}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **flash temperature** is what activates the **EP additive chemistry** at the asperity junctions. The peak $\Delta T$ can be **$500$–$1000$ K above bulk temperature**, sufficient to crack hydrocarbon chains and form metal-sulfide or metal-chloride films.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel cam (yield pressure $p_y = 1.5$ GPa) operates against a steel follower in boundary lubrication. The boundary film has a shear strength $\tau_f = 50$ MPa. The normal load is $W = 2000$ N. The real area of contact is $A_r = 1.33 \times 10^{-6}$ m². The flash temperature is $400$ °C. Determine:
1. Coefficient of friction $\mu_b$
2. Friction force $F$
3. Asperity load fraction $\alpha$, given fluid-film load $W_f = 200$ N

**Solution:**

**(1) Coefficient of friction:**

$$\mu_b = \frac{\tau_f}{p_y} = \frac{50 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.0333$$

$$\boxed{\mu_b \approx 0.033}$$

**(2) Friction force:**

$$F = \mu_b \cdot W = 0.0333 \times 2000 = 66.67 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 66.67 \text{ N}}$$

**Cross-check using shear:**

$$F = A_r \cdot \tau_f = (1.33 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50 \times 10^{6}) = 66.5 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

**(3) Asperity load fraction:**

$$\alpha = \frac{W_a}{W} = \frac{W - W_f}{W} = \frac{2000 - 200}{2000} = 0.9$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 0.9 \text{ (90 \% load on asperities — pure boundary regime)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note for students:** Always cross-verify friction force from both $\mu W$ and $A_r \tau_f$ — they **must** agree (within rounding). This is a favourite KTU examiner's verification step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Mechanism of Boundary Lubrication (Asperity + Film)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Bulk Lubricant Oil] -->|Polar Head Attraction| B1[Physisorption]
    A1 -->|Chemical Bond| B2[Chemisorption]
    A1 -->|High T and P Reaction| B3[Sacrificial Reaction Film]

    B1 --> C1[Multilayer Weak Film]
    B2 --> C2[Monolayer Strong Film]
    B3 --> C3[Metal Sulfide or Phosphate Film]

    C1 --> D1[Bare Asperity Contact]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1

    D1 --> E1[Asperity Load Wa]
    D1 --> E2[Fluid Film Load Wf]
    E1 --> F1[Total Normal Load W]
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Friction Force F = µb W]
    G1 --> H1[Wear Rate from Archard Equation]
    H1 --> I1[Steady State Tribofilm]
    I1 -.Replenishment.-> A1
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Stribeck Curve and Boundary-Lubrication Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Low Hersey Number] --> B1[Boundary Lubrication]
    B1 --> C1[High Friction µ = 0.1 to 0.3]
    B1 --> D1[Asperity Contact Dominant]
    B1 --> E1[Lambda less than 1]
    B1 --> F1[Film less than 100 nm]
    B1 --> G1[Governed by Surface Films]

    H1[Intermediate Hersey] --> I1[Mixed Lubrication]
    I1 --> J1[Lambda between 1 and 3]
    I1 --> K1[Partial Asperity and Fluid Load]

    L1[High Hersey Number] --> M1[Full Film Hydrodynamic]
    M1 --> N1[Lambda greater than 3]
    M1 --> O1[Fluid Pressure Dominant]
    M1 --> P1[Minimum Friction µ about 0.001]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hersey number** = $\frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$, where $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, $N$ is rotational speed (rpm), and $P$ is load per unit projected area. The **Stribeck curve** plots $\mu$ versus the Hersey number; boundary lubrication is the **leftmost plateau** where $\mu$ is high and approximately constant.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Functional Architecture of Boundary Film Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1[Step 1 Adsorption]
        A1[Polar Molecule Approaches Surface] --> A2[Van der Waals Physisorption]
        A1 --> A3[Chemisorption with Metal]
    end
    subgraph P2[Step 2 Reaction]
        A3 --> B1[Flash Temperature Spike]
        B1 --> B2[Additive Decomposition]
        B2 --> B3[Metal Sulfide Film]
        B2 --> B4[Metal Phosphate Film]
    end
    subgraph P3[Step 3 Load Support]
        B3 --> C1[Bearing Load Transmitted via Film]
        B4 --> C1
        A2 --> C1
    end
    subgraph P4[Step 4 Shear]
        C1 --> D1[Shear at Low Film Strength]
        D1 --> E1[Low Friction Coefficient]
    end
    subgraph P5[Step 5 Steady State]
        E1 --> F1[Continuous Film Replenishment]
        F1 --> A1
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Additive Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LUB[Lubricant Additives] --> ON[Organic]
    LUB --> IN[Inorganic]

    ON --> FA[Fatty Acids - Stearic Acid]
    ON --> ES[Esters - Glycerol Esters]
    ON --> EP[Extreme Pressure - Sulfur Phosphorus]
    ON --> AW[Anti Wear - ZDDP]
    ON --> MOD[Organic Molybdenum - MoDTC]

    IN --> SL[Solid Lubricants]
    IN --> CL[Chlorinated Compounds]

    SL --> MOS[Molybdenum Disulfide MoS2]
    SL --> GR[Graphite]
    SL --> PTFE[Polytetrafluoroethylene PTFE]
    SL --> WS2[Tungsten Disulfide WS2]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **interaction between boundary additives and metal substrates** is what makes tribology an interdisciplinary field spanning **chemistry (additive reactions), materials science (substrate response), and mechanical engineering (load/speed regimes)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> Define **boundary lubrication**. What is the typical range of film thickness in this regime? State the **lambda ratio** range that characterizes it.

**Model Answer:**

Boundary lubrication is a regime of lubrication in which the lubricant film between two contacting surfaces becomes so thin (only a few molecular layers, typically **$1$–$100$ nm**) that the load is supported primarily by **asperity contacts** rather than by the hydrodynamically generated fluid pressure. Friction is governed by the shear strength of adsorbed or chemically reacted surface films.

The regime is characterized by a **lambda ratio** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma < 1$, indicating that the film is thinner than the composite surface roughness. The typical coefficient of friction is **$0.05$–$0.30$**.

---

### Question 2

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> Differentiate between **physisorption** and **chemisorption** in the context of boundary film formation.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Bond type | Van der Waals (weak) | Chemical bond (strong) |
| Bond energy | $0.1$–$0.5$ eV | $1$–$10$ eV |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Monolayer only |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Often irreversible |
| Temperature stability | Low ($< 70$ °C) | Moderate ($70$–$200$ °C) |
| Activation energy | Not required | Often required |

**Physisorbed films** (e.g., long-chain paraffins on steel) provide good lubrication at low temperatures but **desorb** above their transition temperature. **Chemisorbed films** (e.g., fatty acids reacting with iron oxide) are far more durable and are preferred in **engine and gear lubricants**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Bowden–Tabor equation** for the coefficient of friction in boundary lubrication. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail the **mechanism of extreme-pressure (EP) lubrication**. With the help of neat sketches, discuss the role of **sulfur-based and phosphorus-based EP additives** in forming sacrificial reaction films. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Assumptions of Bowden–Tabor model:**

1. Asperity contacts are **plastic** (i.e., $A_r = W / p_y$).
2. The **boundary film is continuous** over the real contact area.
3. The film has a uniform **shear strength $\tau_f$**.
4. The **plowing component** of friction is negligible.
5. The substrate follows **von Mises / Tresca** yield criterion, so $p_y \approx 2 \tau_m$.

**Derivation:**

Real area of contact under plastic deformation:

$$A_r = \frac{W}{p_y}$$

Shear force on the film:

$$F = A_r \cdot \tau_f = \left(\frac{W}{p_y}\right) \tau_f$$

Coefficient of friction:

$$\mu_b = \frac{F}{W} = \frac{\tau_f}{p_y}$$

Introduce the **shear-strength ratio** $s = \tau_f / \tau_m$. Using $p_y \approx 2\tau_m$:

$$\boxed{\mu_b = \frac{s}{2}}$$

For steel with $s \approx 0.1$, $\mu_b \approx 0.05$ — which matches observed boundary friction values.

> **Mark Distribution [as per KTU 2024 rubric]:**
> - Assumptions clearly stated: 2 marks
> - Derivation of $A_r$ and $F$: 3 marks
> - Final expression and physical interpretation: 2 marks

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Mechanism of Extreme-Pressure (EP) Lubrication:**

EP additives are **reactive sulfur, phosphorus, and chlorine compounds** blended into the base oil. They are designed to **chemically react with the metal surface** at the high flash temperatures of asperity contacts, forming **sacrificial low-shear films** (typically metal sulfides, phosphates, or chlorides) that prevent metal-to-metal welding and scuffing.

**Sulfur-based EP additives (e.g., sulfides, disulfides):**
- At asperity flash temperatures ($> 600$ °C), they react with iron to form **iron sulfide (FeS)**, which has a layered crystal structure (similar to MoS₂) with low shear strength parallel to the layers.
- Reaction: $\text{Fe} + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS}$
- Film thickness: $50$–$500$ nm
- Effective for heavily loaded **hypoid gears**, **rolling-mill bearings**

**Phosphorus-based EP additives (e.g., tricresyl phosphate, TCP):**
- React at moderate temperatures to form **iron phosphate (FePO₄)** glassy films.
- Reaction: $\text{Fe} + \text{ROP(O)(OR')}_2 \rightarrow \text{FePO}_4 + \text{organic byproducts}$
- Film thickness: $20$–$200$ nm
- Effective for **worm gears**, **rolling-sliding contacts**

**Sketches to be drawn (descriptive):**

```
   Asperity Contact with EP Film
   ─────────────────────────────
    Steel 1   ████████▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ Steel 2
             ↑         ↑
         Substrate   Sacrificial
                     FeS / FePO4
                     Film Layer
         (asperity flashes heat → additive
          decomposes → film forms → low shear)
```

The **sacrificial** nature is key: the film is **continuously consumed and re-formed** during sliding, protecting the underlying metal.

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - EP mechanism description: 3 marks
> - Role of S and P additives (with reactions): 3 marks
> - Neat sketch: 1 mark

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With the help of the **Stribeck curve**, classify the different regimes of lubrication. Identify the position of **boundary lubrication** and explain why the coefficient of friction is high in this region. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **classification and function of boundary-lubrication additives** in detail. Include fatty acids, ZDDP, MoS₂, and graphite in your discussion. (7 marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Stribeck Curve (Description):**

The Stribeck curve plots the **coefficient of friction $\mu$** (y-axis) against the **Hersey number $\eta N / P$** (x-axis, where $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity, $N$ = rotational speed, $P$ = load per unit projected area).

```
   μ
   ↑
   │  ████
   │  ████
   │  ████  ← Boundary (constant high μ)
   │  ████
   │   ████
   │    ███ ← Mixed (decreasing μ)
   │     ███
   │      ████
   │         ████ ← Hydrodynamic (μ rises with ηN/P)
   │             ████
   └──────────────────────→  ηN/P
```

**Three regimes:**

1. **Boundary Lubrication** (left plateau): $\eta N / P$ is very low. The film is too thin to separate the surfaces; load is on asperities. $\mu$ is **high and nearly constant** ($0.1$–$0.3$). Friction is governed by the **shear strength of the boundary film** $\mu_b = \tau_f / p_y$, not by viscosity.

2. **Mixed Lubrication** (descending): Film partially supports the load. Asperity + fluid share the load. $\mu$ decreases as $\eta N / P$ increases.

3. **Full-Film (Hydrodynamic/EHL)** (rising branch on the right): Film completely separates the surfaces. $\mu$ rises with speed because viscous drag in the fluid increases.

> **Mark Distribution:**
> - Curve description with axes and three regimes: 3 marks
> - Identification of boundary region: 2 marks
> - Reason for high $\mu$ in boundary: 2 marks

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 Marks]

**Classification and Function of Boundary-Lubrication Additives:**

> **[1] Fatty Acids (e.g., Stearic Acid, Palmitic Acid):**
> - Function: Form **chemisorbed monolayers** on metal oxides via the **polar carboxyl group (-COOH)**.
> - Bonding: $\text{R-COOH} + \text{Fe-O} \rightarrow \text{R-COO-Fe} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$
> - Effective temperature: up to $120$–$150$ °C
> - Limitation: Desorb at higher temperatures

> **[2] ZDDP (Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate):**
> - Most widely used **anti-wear (AW)** additive in engine oils
> - Function: At asperity temperatures ($150$–$300$ °C), it decomposes to form a **polyphosphate glass film** of composition $\text{Zn}_{3}(\text{PO}_4)_2$ / $\text{FeZn}_2(\text{PO}_4)_2$ on the metal surface
> - Film thickness: $50$–$150$ nm, sacrificial and self-replenishing
> - Typical concentration: $0.5$–$1.5$ % by mass in engine oils
> - Also acts as an **antioxidant**

> **[3] MoS₂ (Molybdenum Disulfide):**
> - Inorganic **solid lubricant**; lamellar structure with weak van der Waals bonding between S–Mo–S layers
> - Shear strength: $\tau_f \approx 25$ MPa (very low)
> - Effective temperature: up to $350$ °C in inert atmosphere
> - Use: Greases for heavily loaded **rolling-element bearings**, space applications

> **[4] Graphite:**
> - Layered **carbon** structure; weak inter-layer bonding
> - Requires **adsorbed water vapor** to be effective (humidity > $5$ %)
> - Effective temperature: up to $450$ °C in air
> - Use: Foundry molds, high-temperature furnace bearings, locks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student errors in this question:**
> - Confusing **EP** and **AW** additives. **AW** (like ZDDP) works at *moderate* loads and temperatures; **EP** (like sulfur compounds) works at *extreme* loads and temperatures.
> - Forgetting that **graphite** needs moisture to be effective — students often list it as a universal dry lubricant.
> - Not mentioning the **polar head / non-polar tail** structure of fatty acids (this is critical to understanding the orientation of the monolayer).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Boundary Lubrication questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the lambda ratio with the Hersey number.** The lambda ratio is dimensionless film thickness ($\lambda = h/\sigma$); the Hersey number is a viscosity–speed–load parameter ($\eta N / P$). They are **not** the same.
> 2. **Not converting units consistently** in numerical problems. Always express pressure in Pa, area in m², film thickness in m. Marks are explicitly deducted for mixing mm and m or MPa and Pa.
> 3. **Omitting the assumptions** in the Bowden–Tabor derivation. Examiners award 2 marks *specifically* for stating assumptions.
> 4. **Writing "boundary lubrication is when oil is thin"** — this is informal and earns partial credit. Use the formal definition with $\lambda < 1$.
> 5. **Confusing the Stribeck curve's three regions.** Memorize the order: Boundary → Mixed → Hydrodynamic (left to right).
> 6. **Skipping reaction equations** for EP/AW additives. KTU examiners award marks for chemical equations like $\text{Fe} + \text{S} \rightarrow \text{FeS}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boundary Lubrication**

- **Definition:** Lubrication regime where the film is too thin to separate asperities; load carried by metal contacts plus adsorbed/reaction films. Film thickness **$1$–$100$ nm**.

- **Lambda ratio:** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$. Boundary lubrication: $\lambda < 1$.

- **Stribeck curve position:** Leftmost plateau. $\mu \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ (high and approximately constant).

- **Two types of adsorption:**
  - Physisorption (weak, reversible, multilayer, low T)
  - Chemisorption (strong, monolayer, high T)

- **Bowden–Tabor equation:** $\mu_b = \tau_f / p_y = s/2$ (where $s$ = shear-strength ratio). **Must state assumptions** for full marks.

- **Friction decomposition:** $\mu_b = \alpha \mu_a + (1-\alpha) \mu_f$.

- **Langmuir isotherm:** $\theta = KP / (1 + KP)$. Used to quantify fractional surface coverage by the additive.

- **Flash temperature:** $\Delta T = \mu F_N v / (4 a J K)$. Activates EP additive chemistry at asperities.

- **Additives to remember:**
  - **Fatty acids** (stearic acid) → chemisorbed monolayer → effective up to $150$ °C
  - **ZDDP** → polyphosphate glass film → AW additive
  - **Sulfur compounds** → FeS film → EP additive
  - **MoS₂** → lamellar solid lubricant → dry and grease lubrication
  - **Graphite** → needs adsorbed moisture → high-T applications

- **Engineering applications:** Engine valve trains, gear teeth, slow-speed journal bearings, metal cutting, cold-start of engines, hypoid gearboxes.

- **Transition temperature:** The temperature at which the boundary film desorbs and friction rises sharply. Critical design parameter for **engine oil** and **EP grease** selection.

- **Stiction:** The ratio $r_f = \mu_{static} / \mu_{kinetic}$ — important in **precision instruments, MEMS, and space mechanisms**.

- **Rule of thumb:** For every $10$ °C rise in operating temperature above the transition temperature, **friction can increase by $20$–$50$ %** in boundary lubrication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** is the regime of fluid-film lubrication in which the pressure developed in the lubricant film is high enough to cause **significant elastic deformation of the contacting surfaces**, while simultaneously the lubricant viscosity is enhanced by **pressure-viscosity (piezoviscous) effects**. EHL is the dominant lubrication mechanism in **highly loaded, non-conforming contacts** such as rolling element bearings (ball/roller bearings), gear teeth meshing, and cam–follower pairs.

The defining feature of EHL is that **both the hydrodynamic action and the elastic flattening of the asperities act together** to produce a film that is typically 0.1 µm to 1 µm thick — thin enough that surface roughness still matters, yet thick enough to prevent direct metal-to-metal contact under ideal conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squash Ball on a Soft Mattress"

Imagine pushing a rigid steel ball very hard onto a soft rubber mattress covered with a thin film of honey.

- The **honey** tries to escape sideways → it generates a pressure that **flattens the rubber** → this flattening creates a wider, nearly parallel-sided gap that traps the honey.
- The **steel ball** is "rigid" relative to the rubber, but the **rubber deforms elastically** under the contact pressure.
- That trapped pressurized honey film is exactly what an **EHL film** looks like in a ball bearing.

> [!NOTE]
> In a gear or rolling bearing, the asperities don't actually touch — the elastic "squish" of the metal itself (steel is not perfectly rigid at gigapascal pressures) creates a flattened region that confines the lubricant.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & System Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude (Steel–Steel EHL) |
|--------|----------|-------------------------------------|
| **E′** | Effective Elastic Modulus | ≈ 230 GPa |
| **η₀** | Atmospheric Viscosity | 0.01 – 0.1 Pa·s |
| **α** | Pressure-Viscosity Coeff. | 1.0 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ (mineral oil) |
| **p_H** | Hertzian Contact Pressure | 0.5 – 3.0 GPa |
| **h_min** | Minimum EHL Film Thickness | 0.1 – 1.0 µm |
| **λ** | Specific Film Thickness | 0.5 – 3.0 |

## 1.4 Where EHL Occurs in Real Machines

- **Ball & roller bearings** (rolling + sliding)
- **Spur, helical and hypoid gears**
- **Cam–follower contacts** in IC engines
- **Rolling element fatigue testers**
- **Metal-forming / rolling-mill roll necks**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 typically weights EHL as the *most-asked* topic. Always link EHL to **Stribeck curve** and the **lambda ratio (λ)** in the answer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pure Hydrodynamic Theory Fails in Concentrated Contacts

In classical hydrodynamic lubrication (Reynolds equation), the lubricant film thickness depends on geometry, speed, and viscosity — and is computed assuming surfaces are **rigid**. In a real ball bearing, the contact area is *tiny* (a few hundred microns across) and the load is *enormous* — pressures reach **1–3 GPa**.

At such pressures:
1. The **steel surfaces elastically deform** by tens of nanometres, creating a flattened "plateau" of contact.
2. The **oil viscosity increases exponentially** with pressure (10–100× stiffer than at atmospheric pressure).
3. The film thickness becomes **far larger** than what rigid-body hydrodynamic theory predicts.

Therefore, an EHL theory must couple:
- **Reynolds equation** (flow)
- **Elastic deformation equation** (surface conformity)
- **Barus / Roelands equation** (pressure-viscosity)

## 2.2 The Four EHL Regimes (Hamrock–Dowson Classification)

The operating regime is governed by the **dimensionless groups** U, W, and G:

$$U = \frac{\eta_0 \, u}{E' \, R_x}$$

$$W = \frac{F}{E' \, R_x^{2}}$$

$$G = \alpha \, E'$$

| Regime | Description | Approximate λ |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| **Isoviscous-Rigid** | Low pressure, no viscosity change, rigid surfaces | < 0.01 µm film |
| **Piezoviscous-Rigid** | Viscosity rises with p, but bodies stay rigid | 0.01 – 0.1 µm |
| **Isoviscous-Elastic** | Elastic deformation, but no viscosity change | ~ 0.1 µm |
| **Piezoviscous-Elastic (Full EHL)** | Both effects active — **the true EHL regime** | 0.1 – 1.0 µm |

> [!NOTE]
> Real machine components like **gear teeth and rolling bearings** operate in the **Piezoviscous-Elastic** regime, where both elastic flattening AND pressure-viscosity are critical.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Expression | Use |
|---|----------|-----------|-----|
| 1 | Equivalent Radius | $\frac{1}{R_x} = \frac{1}{R_{1x}} + \frac{1}{R_{2x}}$ | Elliptical contact |
| 2 | Effective Modulus | $\frac{1}{E'} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1-\nu_1^{2}}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^{2}}{E_2}\right)$ | Mixed-material contact |
| 3 | Hertzian Half-Width | $a = \left(\frac{4 \, F \, R_x}{\pi \, E' \, L}\right)^{1/2}$ (line) / $a = \left(\frac{3 \, F \, R_x}{4 \, E'}\right)^{1/3}$ (point) | Pressure footprint |
| 4 | Hertzian Max. Pressure | $p_0 = \frac{2 \, F}{\pi \, a \, L}$ (line) / $p_0 = \frac{3 \, F}{2 \, \pi \, a^{2}}$ (point) | Contact stress |
| 5 | Barus Viscosity Law | $\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$ | Pressure–viscosity |
| 6 | Roelands Equation | $\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, \exp\!\left\{\left[\ln(\eta_0) + 9.67\right]\!\left[\left(1+\frac{p}{p_{R}}\right)^{z} - 1\right]\right\}$ | High-pressure viscosity |
| 7 | Dowson–Higginson (1975) | $h_{min} = 3.63 \, R_x \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \,\left(1-e^{-0.68 k}\right)$ | **Central film thickness (line contact)** |
| 8 | Hamrock–Dowson (Point Contact) | $H_c = 3.63 \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \,\left(1-e^{-0.68 k}\right)$ | **Central film thickness (elliptical)** |
| 9 | Minimum Film Thickness | $H_{min} = H_c \left(1 - \tfrac{7}{8}\bigl(1-e^{-1.18 k}\bigr)\right)$ | H_c → h_min |
| 10 | Specific Film Thickness | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}}$ | Lubrication regime indicator |
| 11 | Roll-Speed (entrainment) | $u = \dfrac{u_1 + u_2}{2}$ | Entraining velocity |

Where:
- $k$ = ellipticity parameter = $a \div b$ (semi-axis ratio of contact ellipse)
- $R_x$, $R_y$ = reduced radii in the x, y principal planes
- $R_{q}$ = RMS surface roughness

## 2.4 The Film-Thickness Map: Shape of the EHL Film

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiners love asking:** "Sketch the EHL film shape and label its features."

The EHL film has a characteristic **horse-shoe** or **lobe** profile:

- A **central plateau** (h_c) of nearly constant thickness.
- A pronounced **side-lobe minimum** (h_min) at the trailing exit.
- A **pressure spike** (sometimes 2× the Hertzian peak) just before the exit constriction.

This pressure spike is what generates the **side-lobe minimum** and is the key feature separating EHL from classical HD.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bearing life prediction** — fatigue life depends on λ.
- **Gear scoring / pitting resistance** — minimum film thickness vs. composite surface roughness.
- **Energy efficiency** — EHD friction is 10–100× lower than boundary friction.
- **Designing synthetic lubricants** — high α (high-VI oils) → thicker films.
- **Failure diagnosis** — low λ explains micropitting, scuffing, and white-etch cracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Radius and Effective Modulus

### Step 1: Geometry of the Contacting Pair

Consider two cylinders crossing at an angle. The principal curvatures in the *x* and *y* directions are:

$$\frac{1}{R_{x}} = \frac{1}{R_{1x}} + \frac{1}{R_{2x}}, \qquad \frac{1}{R_{y}} = \frac{1}{R_{1y}} + \frac{1}{R_{2y}}$$

These two reduced curvatures define the elliptical Hertzian contact.

### Step 2: Composite Elastic Modulus

For two bodies pressed together, Boussinesq–Cerruti's solution gives the displacement at the surface. The combined elastic compliance must add. With the assumption that the contact patch is small compared to body radii, one obtains:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_{z,\text{body 1}} &= \frac{(1-\nu_{1}^{2})}{\pi \, E_{1}} \iint \frac{p(x',y')}{r}\,dx'\,dy' \\
u_{z,\text{body 2}} &= \frac{(1-\nu_{2}^{2})}{\pi \, E_{2}} \iint \frac{p(x',y')}{r}\,dx'\,dy' \\
\text{Total } u_z &= \frac{1}{\pi}\!\left(\frac{1-\nu_{1}^{2}}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_{2}^{2}}{E_2}\right)\!\iint \frac{p}{r}\,dx'\,dy' \\
\text{Defining } \frac{1}{E'} &= \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{1-\nu_{1}^{2}}{E_{1}} + \frac{1-\nu_{2}^{2}}{E_{2}}\right) \\
\end{aligned}$$

The factor **½** appears because two bodies of equal stiffness halve the system compliance, hence the effective modulus is the **harmonic mean** of the two.

### Step 3: Hertzian Pressure Distribution (Point Contact, Re-derived)

In an axisymmetric point contact, the pressure profile is semi-ellipsoidal:

$$p(r) = p_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}}$$

The total normal load must equal the integral of the pressure over the contact area:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\int_{0}^{a} p(r) \, r\,dr\,d\theta \\
  &= p_0 \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!d\theta \int_{0}^{a} r \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}}\,dr \\
  &= 2\pi \, p_0 \int_{0}^{a} r \sqrt{1 - \tfrac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}}\,dr \\
\text{Let } t &= 1 - \tfrac{r^{2}}{a^{2}}, \quad dt = -\tfrac{2r}{a^{2}}dr \\
F &= 2\pi \, p_0 \cdot \frac{a^{2}}{2} \int_{0}^{1} t^{1/2}\,dt \\
  &= \pi \, a^{2} \, p_0 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \\
p_0 &= \frac{3F}{2 \pi a^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This recovers the **classic Hertzian maximum pressure** for a point contact.

### Step 4: Hertzian Contact Radius for a Sphere on a Sphere

Equating the Hertzian pressure solution to the elasticity-driven deformation:

$$\begin{aligned}
a^{3} &= \frac{3 F R_x}{4 E'} \\
a &= \left(\frac{3 F R_x}{4 E'}\right)^{1/3} \\
p_0 &= \frac{3F}{2\pi a^{2}} = \frac{3F}{2\pi}\!\left(\frac{4E'}{3 F R_x}\right)^{\!2/3}
\end{aligned}$$

For a steel–steel contact with F = 1000 N, R_x = 10 mm, E′ = 2.3 × 10¹¹ Pa:

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \left(\frac{3 \times 1000 \times 0.010}{4 \times 2.3\times 10^{11}}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= \left(\frac{30}{9.2 \times 10^{11}}\right)^{1/3} \\
  &= (3.26 \times 10^{-11})^{1/3} \\
  &= 3.19 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.319\,\text{mm} \\
p_0 &= \frac{3 \times 1000}{2\pi \times (3.19\times 10^{-4})^{2}} \\
    &= 2.34 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa} = 2.34\,\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **GPa pressure regime** where EHL becomes essential.

## 3.2 Derivation Sketch — Hamrock–Dowson Central Film Thickness

The full Hamrock–Dowson equation is **semi-empirical** (fitted to thousands of numerical EHL simulations by Hamrock & Dowson, 1977). The dimensionless central film thickness is:

$$H_c = 3.63 \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \,(1 - e^{-0.68\,k})$$

### Logical Steps that Produce the Exponents

1. **Start with Reynolds equation** in dimensionless form, add elastic deformation, add piezoviscous η(p).
2. **Non-dimensionalise** by Hertzian radius *a* and Hertzian pressure *p₀*.
3. **Use Barus law** for viscosity.
4. **Solve numerically** for a wide matrix of U, W, G, k.
5. **Curve-fit** the dimensionless h_c as a power-law in U, W, G and an exponential in k.

The exponents (0.68, 0.49, −0.073) and the constant 3.63 have **no closed-form derivation** — they come from regression of computed data.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners sometimes ask "Where do the exponents 0.68 and 0.49 come from?" — the correct answer is **"from regression of numerically computed EHL solutions"**, NOT from first principles.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — EHL Film in a Ball Bearing

**Given:**
- Ball radius R₁ = 10 mm; inner-race radius R₂ = 12 mm
- Ball load F = 50 N
- Rolling (entrainment) speed u = 5 m/s
- Lubricant: mineral oil, η₀ = 0.05 Pa·s, α = 1.5 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹
- E′ = 2.3 × 10¹¹ Pa; ν = 0.3 (both bodies)

**Step 1: Reduced Radius**

$$R_x = \left(\frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{1}{0.010} + \frac{1}{0.012}\right)^{-1} = 5.45 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 5.45\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Dimensionless Groups**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\eta_0 \, u}{E' \, R_x} = \frac{0.05 \times 5}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times 5.45 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.99 \times 10^{-10} \\
W &= \frac{F}{E' \, R_x^{2}} = \frac{50}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \times (5.45 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} = 7.0 \times 10^{-8} \\
G &= \alpha \, E' = 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.3 \times 10^{11} = 3450 \\
k &= 1 \quad (\text{ball-on-race, approximately conforming — take } k \approx 1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Central Film Thickness (H_c)**

$$H_c = 3.63 \times (1.99\times 10^{-10})^{0.68} \times (3450)^{0.49} \times (7.0\times 10^{-8})^{-0.073} \times (1 - e^{-0.68})$$

Computing term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.99\times 10^{-10})^{0.68} &= 7.32 \times 10^{-7} \\
(3450)^{0.49} &= 50.3 \\
(7.0\times 10^{-8})^{-0.073} &= 1.86 \\
(1 - e^{-0.68}) &= 1 - 0.5066 = 0.4934 \\
H_c &= 3.63 \times 7.32\times 10^{-7} \times 50.3 \times 1.86 \times 0.4934 \\
    &= 1.10 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Central Film Thickness in Metres**

$$h_c = H_c \times R_x = 1.10 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.45 \times 10^{-3} = 6.0 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.60\,\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 5: Minimum Film Thickness**

$$H_{min} = H_c\left(1 - \tfrac{7}{8}(1 - e^{-1.18 k})\right) = 1.10\times 10^{-4} \times \left(1 - 0.875(1 - e^{-1.18})\right)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
1 - e^{-1.18} &= 0.692 \\
0.875 \times 0.692 &= 0.605 \\
1 - 0.605 &= 0.395 \\
H_{min} &= 1.10\times 10^{-4} \times 0.395 = 4.35\times 10^{-5} \\
h_{min} &= 4.35\times 10^{-5} \times 5.45\times 10^{-3} = 2.37\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.24\,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Lambda Ratio (assuming Rq1 = Rq2 = 0.1 µm)**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}} = \frac{0.24}{\sqrt{0.1^{2} + 0.1^{2}}} = \frac{0.24}{0.1414} = 1.70$$

**Step 7: Lubrication Regime Identification**

Since **1 < λ < 3**, this is the **Mixed Lubrication (EHL) regime** — exactly what we expect for a well-designed rolling element bearing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Regime vs. λ

| λ Range | Regime | Surface Interaction | Failure Mode to Watch |
|---------|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| λ < 1 | Boundary / Mixed | Asperity contact | Adhesive wear, scuffing |
| 1 < λ < 3 | **Mixed EHL** (most machine components) | Partial asperity load-sharing | Micropitting, mild wear |
| λ > 3 | Full Fluid Film EHL | No asperity contact | No wear; possible lubricant starvation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — EHL Film Pressure and Shape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Zone: Low pressure, oil at atmospheric viscosity] --> B[Convergent Wedge: Pressure rises sharply]
    B --> C[Hertzian Plateau: Elastic flattening, film nearly parallel]
    C --> D[Exit Constriction: Film thins to h_min]
    D --> E[Pressure Spike: p_spike up to 2 times p0]
    E --> F[Side Lobe Region: Film thickens then drops to h_min]
    F --> G[Cavitation or Reflow Zone]

    classDef zone fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef spike fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:1px
    classDef lobe fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#06c,stroke-width:1px

    class A,B zone
    class E spike
    class C,D,F,G lobe
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Coupling of the Three EHL Equations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reynolds Equation\nPressure drives flow] --> Film[Compute Pressure p x y]
    Film --> E[Elastic Deformation Eq.\nSurface conforms to p]
    E --> New[Updated Geometry]
    New --> V[Barus / Roelands Law\nViscosity rises with p]
    V --> Visc[Updated Viscosity eta]
    Visc --> R

    classDef loop fill:#e0f7e0,stroke:#070,stroke-width:1px
    class R,E,V loop
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Mapping of EHL Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Lambda Calculation] --> A{lambda less than 1}
    A -- Yes --> B[Boundary Lubrication]
    A -- No --> C{1 less than lambda less than 3}
    C -- Yes --> D[Mixed EHL Lubrication]
    C -- No --> E[Full Fluid Film EHL]

    classDef reg fill:#eef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    class B,D,E reg
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — EHL Film Cross-Section (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Rigid Body 1, Steel, E1, nu1] -->|Pressure p_xy| CF[Conformal Hertzian Contact Patch]
    S2[Rigid Body 2, Steel, E2, nu2] -->|Counter-pressure| CF
    CF --> FL[Trapped EHL Film]
    FL --> F1[Central Plateau, h_c]
    FL --> F2[Side Lobe, h_s]
    FL --> F3[Exit Minimum, h_min]
    FL --> F4[Pressure Spike Region]

    classDef film fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#a80,stroke-width:1px
    class F1,F2,F3,F4 film
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU students often **lose 2 marks** by failing to draw the **side-lobe minimum and pressure spike** in the EHL film. Always include these in a sketch.
