# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL). Mention any two applications. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer:**
> EHL is a regime of fluid-film lubrication in which the **pressure generated in the lubricant film is high enough to cause significant elastic deformation of the contacting surfaces**, while the **lubricant viscosity also rises substantially** under the same pressure.
>
> Two applications: **(1) Rolling element bearings** (ball/roller) and **(2) gear teeth meshing** (spur/helical/hypoid).

### Q2. State the Dowson–Higginson equation for minimum EHL film thickness. What is its physical significance? `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer:**
$$h_{min} = 3.63 \, R_x \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \,(1 - e^{-0.68 k})$$
> It is a **semi-empirical correlation** that predicts the minimum lubricant film thickness in rolling/sliding concentrated contacts, and is the **industry standard** for EHL design of rolling bearings and gears.

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Past Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **pressure distribution and film-thickness profile** in an EHL contact. List the three key features that distinguish an EHL film from a classical hydrodynamic film. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **ball bearing** has the following data:
- Ball radius = 12 mm; race radius = 13 mm
- Load on ball F = 80 N; entraining velocity u = 4 m/s
- Effective modulus E′ = 2.3 × 10¹¹ Pa
- Lubricant: η₀ = 0.04 Pa·s; α = 1.8 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹
- Surface roughness Rq1 = Rq2 = 0.15 µm
- Ellipticity k = 1

Calculate: **(i)** Reduced radius, **(ii)** Dimensionless groups U, W, G, **(iii)** Central and minimum film thickness, **(iv)** Specific film thickness λ, **(v)** Identify the lubrication regime. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

1. **Sketch** — draw the two contacting bodies, the lubricant film, the central plateau, the side-lobe minimum, and the pressure spike at exit. **Mandatory features to label:** $h_c$, $h_{min}$, $h_{side}$, $p_0$, $p_{spike}$.
2. **Three distinguishing features of EHL film:**
   - **(i) Side-lobe minimum** — a local minimum in film thickness just outside the Hertzian zone, due to the pressure spike.
   - **(ii) Pressure spike** — peak pressure that can be **2× the Hertzian peak**, due to the lubricant "fighting" to escape the constriction.
   - **(iii) Elastic conformity** — the surfaces flatten elastically, creating a near-parallel film of constant thickness across the contact, unlike the wedge shape of HD.
3. The lubricant viscosity rises by 10–100× within the contact due to Barus/Roelands pressure–viscosity effect.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Reduced radius**

$$R_x = \left(\frac{1}{0.012} + \frac{1}{0.013}\right)^{-1} = 6.24\,\text{mm} = 6.24\times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

**(ii) Dimensionless Groups**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{0.04 \times 4}{2.3\times 10^{11} \times 6.24\times 10^{-3}} = 1.115\times 10^{-10} \\
W &= \frac{80}{2.3\times 10^{11} \times (6.24\times 10^{-3})^{2}} = 8.93\times 10^{-8} \\
G &= 1.8\times 10^{-8} \times 2.3\times 10^{11} = 4140
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Film Thicknesses**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_c &= 3.63 \times (1.115\times 10^{-10})^{0.68} \times (4140)^{0.49} \times (8.93\times 10^{-8})^{-0.073} \times (1-e^{-0.68}) \\
    &= 3.63 \times 3.46\times 10^{-7} \times 55.4 \times 1.83 \times 0.4934 \\
    &= 6.32\times 10^{-5} \\
h_c &= H_c \times R_x = 6.32\times 10^{-5} \times 6.24\times 10^{-3} = 3.94\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.394\,\mu\text{m} \\
H_{min} &= H_c(1 - 0.875(1 - e^{-1.18})) = 6.32\times 10^{-5} \times 0.395 = 2.50\times 10^{-5} \\
h_{min} &= 2.50\times 10^{-5} \times 6.24\times 10^{-3} = 1.56\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.156\,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Lambda**

$$\lambda = \frac{0.156}{\sqrt{0.15^{2} + 0.15^{2}}} = \frac{0.156}{0.212} = 0.736$$

**(v) Regime:** Since **λ < 1**, this bearing is operating in the **Boundary/Mixed regime** — the surface finish is too rough or the speed too low for full EHL. The designer should either reduce roughness (Rq < 0.10 µm) or use a higher-viscosity / higher-VI oil.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define the **dimensionless groups U, W, and G** in EHL theory. Explain the physical meaning of each, and show how they are derived from first principles. (7 marks)

**(b)** Two **steel spur gears** mesh with the following data:
- Pitch-line velocity v = 8 m/s; tangential load W_t = 1200 N
- Face width b = 20 mm
- Radius of curvature of contacting flanks at pitch point R_x = 8 mm
- E′ = 2.3 × 10¹¹ Pa; η₀ = 0.06 Pa·s; α = 2.0 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹
- Surface roughness Rq1 = Rq2 = 0.2 µm
- Ellipticity k = 4

Compute: **(i)** Dimensionless groups, **(ii)** Hamrock–Dowson central and minimum film thickness, **(iii)** Specific film thickness and lubrication regime. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a)

**U — Speed Parameter:**

$$U = \frac{\eta_0 \, u}{E' \, R_x}$$

- **Physical meaning:** ratio of **viscous forces** (η₀ u / R_x) to **elastic forces** (E′). High U → lubricant can drag the surfaces together, building hydrodynamic pressure.
- **Derivation:** emerges naturally from non-dimensionalising Reynolds equation using the Hertzian pressure p₀ = 3F / (2π a²) and Hertzian radius a as the characteristic length scale.

**W — Load Parameter:**

$$W = \frac{F}{E' \, R_x^{2}}$$

- **Physical meaning:** ratio of **applied load** to **elastic contact stiffness**. High W → heavy contact, more elastic deformation, thinner film.
- **Derivation:** arises when the load is non-dimensionalised by the product E′ R_x² — the only combination of those two quantities that gives a force.

**G — Material Parameter:**

$$G = \alpha \, E'$$

- **Physical meaning:** product of the **pressure-viscosity coefficient** and the **elastic modulus**. High G → more piezoviscous effect, thicker film.
- **Derivation:** emerges when Barus law $\eta = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$ is non-dimensionalised; αp becomes α E′ × (p / E′) = G × (p / E′).

These three groups completely characterise an EHL contact. Two contacts with the same U, W, G, k will have the **same dimensionless film thickness**.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**(i) Dimensionless Groups** (using entraining speed u = v = 8 m/s)

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{0.06 \times 8}{2.3\times 10^{11} \times 8\times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.48}{1.84\times 10^{9}} = 2.61\times 10^{-10} \\
W &= \frac{1200}{2.3\times 10^{11} \times (8\times 10^{-3})^{2}} = \frac{1200}{1.472\times 10^{7}} = 8.15\times 10^{-5} \\
G &= 2.0\times 10^{-8} \times 2.3\times 10^{11} = 4600
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Hamrock–Dowson Central and Minimum Film Thickness**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_c &= 3.63 \times (2.61\times 10^{-10})^{0.68} \times (4600)^{0.49} \times (8.15\times 10^{-5})^{-0.073} \times (1 - e^{-0.68 \times 4}) \\
(1 - e^{-2.72}) &= 1 - 0.0658 = 0.934 \\
H_c &= 3.63 \times 9.31\times 10^{-7} \times 58.5 \times 2.10 \times 0.934 \\
    &= 3.89\times 10^{-4} \\
h_c &= H_c \times R_x = 3.89\times 10^{-4} \times 8\times 10^{-3} = 3.11\times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 3.11\,\mu\text{m} \\
H_{min} &= H_c \times \left(1 - 0.875(1-e^{-1.18 \times 4})\right) = 3.89\times 10^{-4} \times \left(1 - 0.875 \times 0.991\right) \\
        &= 3.89\times 10^{-4} \times 0.133 = 5.18\times 10^{-5} \\
h_{min} &= 5.18\times 10^{-5} \times 8\times 10^{-3} = 4.14\times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.414\,\mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Lambda and Regime**

$$\lambda = \frac{0.414}{\sqrt{0.2^{2} + 0.2^{2}}} = \frac{0.414}{0.283} = 1.46$$

**Regime:** Since **1 < λ < 3**, the gear pair is operating in the **Mixed EHL regime** — typical for spur gears. Acceptable for moderate duty.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in EHL Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing U and W** — U is a *speed* group; W is a *load* group. Mixing them up is an instant 3-mark penalty.
> 2. **Using H_c instead of h_min** in λ calculation. The **lambda ratio always uses h_min** (not h_c).
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply H_c by R_x** to get h_c in metres. The dimensionless H_c is a film-shape ratio, not a real thickness.
> 4. **Wrong Roelands vs. Barus usage.** Roelands is for very high pressures (>0.5 GPa); Barus is acceptable for moderate pressures.
> 5. **No sketch** in 14-mark EHL questions = **−3 marks minimum**. Always draw the EHL film profile with $h_c$, $h_{min}$, $h_{side}$, and the pressure spike.
> 6. **Saying "EHL is a type of boundary lubrication"** — wrong. EHL is a *fluid-film* regime, even though film is thin. Boundary lubrication has *no* full fluid film.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — EHL**

- **Definition:** EHL = **Elastic deformation** + **Hydrodynamic pressure** + **Piezoviscous viscosity rise**.
- **Where it happens:** Ball/roller bearings, gear teeth, cam–follower, rolling-mill roll necks.
- **Three dimensionless groups:** $U$ (speed), $W$ (load), $G$ (material), plus $k$ (ellipticity).
- **Hamrock–Dowson equation:** $H_c = 3.63 \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \,(1 - e^{-0.68 k})$.
- **Convert H to h:** $h = H \times R_x$.
- **Minimum film thickness:** $H_{min} = H_c [1 - 0.875(1 - e^{-1.18 k})]$.
- **Specific film thickness:** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}$.
- **Lambda classification:** λ < 1 → Boundary/Mixed; 1 < λ < 3 → **Mixed EHL**; λ > 3 → Full EHL.
- **EHL film features to draw in exam:** central plateau $h_c$, side-lobe minimum $h_{min}$, pressure spike at exit, near-parallel film thickness.
- **Barus equation:** $\eta = \eta_0 e^{\alpha p}$ — use for moderate EHL pressures.
- **Roelands equation:** more accurate above 0.5 GPa.
- **Effective modulus:** $1/E' = \tfrac{1}{2}[(1-\nu_1^2)/E_1 + (1-\nu_2^2)/E_2]$.
- **Reduced radius:** $1/R_x = 1/R_{1x} + 1/R_{2x}$.
- **Hertzian max pressure (point):** $p_0 = 3F/(2\pi a^{2})$.
- **Hertzian contact radius (point):** $a = (3F R_x / 4E')^{1/3}$.
- **EHL pressure spike:** up to **2× the Hertzian peak** at exit.
- **Famous textbook-free constants:** 3.63, 0.68, 0.49, 0.073, 0.875, 1.18 — memorize for ESE.
- **Design goal in industry:** achieve λ ≥ 2 to avoid micropitting in gears, λ ≥ 1 in bearings.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Elasto-Hydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubrication Regimes: Thick Film and Thin Film Lubrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lubrication Regimes: Thick Film and Thin Film Lubrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

In tribology, a **lubrication regime** is the operational classification of a lubricated contact based on the relative magnitude of the lubricant film thickness to the composite surface roughness of the interacting bodies. The classification is governed by the **Specific Film Thickness (λ, lambda)** — a dimensionless parameter defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}}}$$

where $h_{min}$ is the minimum lubricant film thickness, and $R_{q1}, R_{q2}$ are the **RMS (Root Mean Square) roughness** values of the two contacting surfaces.

The two principal categories are:

- **Thick Film Lubrication (Hydrodynamic / Fluid Film Regime):** The lubricant film is thick enough to completely separate the asperities of the two surfaces. The contact load is supported entirely by the hydrodynamic pressure generated within the fluid film.
- **Thin Film Lubrication:** The film is insufficient to prevent all asperity contact. This regime is further subdivided into **Mixed Lubrication** (partial asperity contact, partial fluid load support) and **Boundary Lubrication** (asperities carry most of the load, with only molecular-thin layers of lubricant or additive films separating them).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The transition between regimes is governed by the dimensionless parameter $\lambda$ (lambda), NOT an absolute film thickness. Two machine elements can operate in different regimes at the *same* absolute film thickness if their surface finishes differ.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Boats on a Lake"

Imagine two boats floating on a lake:

- **Thick Film Regime (Deep Lake):** The boats float far apart from each other with plenty of water (lubricant) between their hulls (surfaces). Even when waves (load) come, the boats never touch each other. The water alone supports them.
- **Thin Film Regime (Shallow Pond):** The water level is so low that the boats are almost scraping the lakebed. When a wave comes, the boat bottoms (asperities) bump into the sand. The sand (surface roughness) now shares the load with the water.

If you replace "boats" with "journal and bearing," "lake" with "lubricant oil," and "sandy lakebed" with "metal asperities," you have a perfect intuitive picture of lubrication regimes.

---

### 1.3 The Stribeck Curve — The "Big Picture" of Lubrication Regimes

The **Stribeck Curve** (named after Richard Stribeck, 1902) is the foundational diagram in tribology that maps the **coefficient of friction (μ)** as a function of a combined dimensionless parameter of viscosity, speed, and load. This curve visually classifies all lubrication regimes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Stribeck Curve:** A plot of friction coefficient $\mu$ on the y-axis against the dimensionless Hersey number (or the parameter $\eta N / P$, where $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity, $N$ = speed in rev/s, $P$ = load per unit projected area) on the x-axis, showing the three lubrication regimes: Boundary, Mixed, and Full Fluid Film.

The vertical axis is **μ (coefficient of friction)**, and the horizontal axis is typically the **Hersey Number** $\frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$ or its dimensionless equivalent.

| Regime | λ Range | Friction Trend | Dominant Load Support |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary Lubrication** | λ < 1 | High (μ ≈ 0.1 to 0.3) | Asperity contacts + boundary films |
| **Mixed Lubrication** | 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 | Decreasing | Fluid film + asperities |
| **Thick Film (Full Fluid)** | λ > 3 | Low, increasing with speed | Hydrodynamic fluid pressure |
| **Hydrostatic Regime** | Static | Lowest | External pressurized supply |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Composite RMS Roughness:** $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$
> - **Standard λ boundaries:** λ < 1 → Boundary; 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 → Mixed; λ > 3 → Full Film (per most KTU textbooks)
> - **Hersey Number (dimensionless group):** $\frac{\eta N}{P}$ — units: $\frac{Pa \cdot s \cdot rev/s}{Pa} = s/rev$ (often expressed as a non-dimensionalized Sommerfeld-corrected form)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Thick Film Lubrication — Operational Breakdown

Thick film lubrication (also called **fluid film lubrication**) refers to regimes where the lubricant film thickness $h_{min}$ is sufficiently larger than the composite surface roughness that the two surfaces are completely separated by a continuous fluid layer.

There are **two sub-classes**:

#### A. Hydrodynamic Lubrication (HL)
- The film is generated entirely by the relative motion of the surfaces (sliding, rotation).
- The lubricant behaves as a **Newtonian fluid**.
- Pressure is generated by the **converging wedge** of fluid (e.g., in a journal bearing) per the Reynolds equation.
- Surface deformation is **negligible**.

#### B. Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)
- Common in **rolling element bearings** (ball bearings, roller bearings) and **gear contacts**.
- Both the fluid pressure (hydrodynamic) AND the **elastic deformation of the asperities/surface** are significant.
- The lubricant experiences extreme pressure spikes — viscosity can increase by **orders of magnitude**.
- Film thickness is typically very thin (sub-micron) but still separates the surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** EHL is technically a *thin film* regime in absolute terms (sub-micron), but it is classified as *thick film* (or full film) tribologically because λ > 3 — the surfaces are still completely separated.

#### Pressure–Viscosity–Temperature Behavior in EHL

In EHL contacts, the lubricant viscosity does **not** remain constant. It is governed by the **Roelands equation**:

$$\eta = \eta_0 \cdot 10^{\left(\frac{\log_{10}\eta_0 + 9.67}{z} \right) \cdot \left( \left(1 + \frac{p}{p_R}\right)^z - 1 \right)}$$

where:
- $\eta_0$ = viscosity at atmospheric pressure
- $p$ = local pressure
- $p_R$ = reference pressure (~5 × 10⁸ Pa)
- $z$ = pressure-viscosity index (typically 0.6 for mineral oils)

---

### 2.2 Thin Film Lubrication — Operational Breakdown

When the film thickness becomes comparable to or smaller than the surface roughness, we enter thin film regimes:

#### A. Boundary Lubrication (BL)
- λ < 1: surfaces frequently make direct asperity contact.
- Load is supported primarily by **surface asperities** and by **boundary films** — monomolecular or polymolecular layers of:
  - Physisorbed molecules (e.g., fatty acids, alcohols)
  - Chemisorbed films (e.g., fatty acids on metal oxides)
  - **Extreme Pressure (EP)** additives — e.g., ZDDP (Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate), tricresyl phosphate, chlorinated paraffins
- Friction is governed by **shearing of these surface films**, not by the bulk lubricant properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Boundary Lubrication:** A regime of lubrication in which the load is carried by the surface asperities rather than by the bulk lubricant, with the friction and wear determined by the properties of the surface films formed by the lubricant and its additives.

#### B. Mixed Lubrication (ML)
- 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3: Both fluid film pressure AND asperity contact contribute to load support.
- The total load $W$ is shared:
$$W = W_{fluid} + W_{asperity}$$
- The friction coefficient is a weighted combination:
$$\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \cdot \mu_{boundary} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \mu_{fluid}$$
where $\alpha$ is the **asperity contact load fraction**.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{2} + R_{q2}^{2}}}$ | Specific film thickness (lambda ratio) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ | Composite RMS roughness | m (micrometers in practice) |
| 3 | $W = W_{fluid} + W_{asperity}$ | Mixed lubrication load partition | N |
| 4 | $\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \mu_b + (1-\alpha)\mu_f$ | Friction in mixed regime | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\frac{\eta N}{P}$ | Hersey Number (x-axis of Stribeck curve) | Pa·s·rev/s ÷ Pa = s/rev |
| 6 | $\eta = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha_p p}$ | Barus pressure-viscosity equation (approx.) | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\eta = \eta_0 \left(1 + \frac{p}{p_R}\right)^z$ | Roelands equation (EHL) | Pa·s |
| 8 | Hamrock-Dowson EHL minimum film thickness (point contact) | $h_{min} = 3.63 \, R_x \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W^{-0.073} \left(1 - e^{-0.68 k}\right)$ | m |
| 9 | $h_0 = 0.16 \, (\mu_0 \, U)^{0.6} \, (R)^{0.6} \, (W/L)^{-0.2}$ | Hamrock-Dowson line contact | m |
| 10 | $\mu_{min} \propto \left(\eta N / P\right)^{n}$ | Power-law in hydrodynamic regime | Dimensionless |

Where (Hamrock-Dowson):
- $U = \eta_0 u / (E' R_x)$ — speed parameter
- $G = \alpha_p E'$ — material parameter
- $W = F / (E' R_x^2)$ — load parameter
- $k$ = ellipticity parameter

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Journal Bearings** (turbines, IC engines, pumps): Designed to operate in **thick film / hydrodynamic** regime for low friction and long life.
- **Rolling Element Bearings** (gearboxes, wheel hubs): Operate in **EHL regime** — a thin physical film (< 1 μm) but a thick tribological film (λ > 3).
- **Gears** (transmissions, industrial gearboxes): EHL or mixed regime depending on speed and load.
- **Cam-Follower Pairs** (engine valves): Often operate in **boundary/mixed** regime at low speeds — EP additives in oil are essential.
- **Cutting Tools** (machining): Generally in **boundary lubrication** — extreme pressure and solid lubricants (MoS₂, graphite) are required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Rule of Thumb:** If λ > 3, you are engineering for durability (long life). If λ < 1, you are engineering for **survivability** (using additives and surface coatings).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Specific Film Thickness Parameter λ (Lambda Ratio)

**Starting Premise:** Any engineering surface, no matter how precisely finished, has microscopic peaks (asperities) and valleys. When two such surfaces approach each other in a lubricant, the question of whether they "touch" depends on how thick the lubricant film is compared to the height of these asperities.

**Step 1 — Characterize Surface Roughness:**  
The ISO standard parameter $R_q$ (RMS roughness) is the root mean square of the height deviations from the mean line:

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{\,2}}$$

where $y_i$ is the vertical deviation at sampling point $i$.

**Step 2 — Composite Surface Roughness:**  
When two surfaces approach each other, the asperities of BOTH surfaces contribute to the contact probability. The composite roughness is the **quadrature (RSS — root sum square)** combination:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}}$$

**Why RSS, not arithmetic sum?** Because asperities are statistically independent and randomly distributed. Probabilistic combination of independent random variables uses RSS.

**Step 3 — Define the Specific Film Thickness:**  
The minimum film thickness $h_{min}$ is the smallest distance between the two surfaces anywhere in the contact. Define:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Ratio:**
- If $h_{min} \gg \sigma$, then $\lambda \gg 1$ → fluid film is much thicker than asperities → **Full Fluid Film (Thick Film)** regime.
- If $h_{min} \approx \sigma$, then $\lambda \approx 1$ → asperities just begin to contact → **Mixed** regime.
- If $h_{min} \ll \sigma$, then $\lambda \ll 1$ → asperities dominate → **Boundary** regime.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: The Stribeck Curve Functional Behavior

**Step 1 — Define the Hersey Number:**  
The Hersey Number is a dimensionless grouping of viscosity, speed, and load:

$$\text{Hersey Number} = \frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)
- $N$ = rotational or sliding speed (rev/s or m/s)
- $P$ = load per unit projected area (Pa)

**Step 2 — Boundary Regime (Low Speed / High Load):**  
At very low Hersey numbers, fluid pressure is insufficient to lift the load. The film collapses, and asperities directly contact. Friction is governed by boundary film shear:

$$\mu_{boundary} \approx \frac{\tau_s}{P} \approx \text{constant (high)}$$

Friction is approximately **independent** of viscosity and speed.

**Step 3 — Mixed Regime (Intermediate Hersey):**  
As speed increases, partial fluid film develops. Friction drops sharply:

$$\mu_{mixed} \propto \left(\frac{\eta N}{P}\right)^{-n}, \quad n \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$$

**Step 4 — Full Fluid Film Regime (High Speed / Low Load):**  
At sufficiently high Hersey numbers, full separation occurs. Friction is now viscous shearing of the fluid, which itself increases with speed (as the fluid moves faster):

$$\mu_{fluid} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\eta N}{P}}$$

This gives the **rising right branch** of the Stribeck curve.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Regime Identification at a Glance

| Parameter | Boundary (BL) | Mixed (ML) | Thick Film (HL/EHL) |
|---|---|---|---|
| λ value | λ < 1 | 1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 | λ > 3 |
| $h_{min}$ | < 1 μm (typical) | 1–3 μm (typical) | > 3 μm (HL) / 0.1–1 μm (EHL) |
| Load support | Asperities + boundary film | Asperities + fluid | Fluid pressure only |
| Friction coefficient | 0.1 – 0.3 (high) | 0.02 – 0.1 (dropping) | 0.001 – 0.01 (low) |
| Wear | High (adhesive) | Moderate | Negligible |
| Speed dependence | Weak | Strong | Strong |
| Viscosity dependence | None | Moderate | Strong |
| Dominant friction mechanism | Shear of surface film | Mixed shear | Viscous shearing of bulk fluid |
| Lubricant property that matters | Additive package | Both base oil & additives | Base oil viscosity |
| Typical examples | Boundary-lubricated gears at low speed, slow journal bearings at start-up | Mid-speed gears, mid-RPM bearings | High-speed journal bearings, well-designed rolling element bearings |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A ball bearing has a minimum lubricant film thickness of $h_{min} = 0.9 \, \mu m$. The inner race has an RMS roughness of $R_{q1} = 0.15 \, \mu m$ and the ball has $R_{q2} = 0.20 \, \mu m$. Identify the lubrication regime.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute composite RMS roughness:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(0.15)^2 + (0.20)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0400} = \sqrt{0.0625} = 0.25 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Compute λ:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{0.9 \, \mu m}{0.25 \, \mu m} = 3.6$$

**Step 3 — Identify the regime:**

Since $\lambda = 3.6 > 3$, the bearing operates in the **Full Fluid Film (Thick Film)** regime — specifically EHL given the ball-race geometry. No asperity contact is expected under steady-state conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Even though the absolute film thickness (0.9 μm) is very small — comparable to the wavelength of visible light — the surfaces are still completely separated because the polished surfaces have very low roughness. This is why EHL is classified as "thick film" tribologically.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Stribeck Curve — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the regime identification logic and the Stribeck curve mapping — rendered as a block-level architecture to avoid ambiguity:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Lubricated Contact Exists] --> B{Compute Hersey Number}
    B --> C[eta times N divided by P]
    C --> D{Hersey Number Magnitude}

    D -- Very Low --> E[Boundary Lubrication]
    E --> E1[lambda less than 1]
    E1 --> E2[Asperity contact dominates]
    E2 --> E3[Friction 0.1 to 0.3 high]
    E3 --> E4[Wear is significant]

    D -- Intermediate --> F[Mixed Lubrication]
    F --> F1[lambda between 1 and 3]
    F1 --> F2[Partial fluid film partial asperity]
    F2 --> F3[Friction 0.02 to 0.1 falling]
    F3 --> F4[Moderate wear]

    D -- High --> G[Thick Film Lubrication]
    G --> G1[lambda greater than 3]
    G1 --> G2[Full fluid film separation]
    G2 --> G3{Geometry type}
    G3 --> G3a[Smooth conformal surfaces]
    G3a --> G3b[Hydrodynamic HL]
    G3 --> G3c[Non conformal high pressure]
    G3c --> G3d[Elastohydrodynamic EHL]
    G3b --> G3e[Friction 0.001 to 0.01 low]
    G3d --> G3e
    G3e --> G3f[Negligible wear]
```

---

### 4.2 Lubrication Regime Comparison — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lubricant Supply System] --> B[Contact Interface]

    B --> C1[Boundary Film]
    B --> C2[Thin Elastohydrodynamic Film]
    B --> C3[Thick Hydrodynamic Film]

    C1 --> D1[Load Support: Asperities]
    C2 --> D2[Load Support: Asperities plus Elastic Deformation]
    C3 --> D3[Load Support: Fluid Pressure Only]

    D1 --> E1[lambda less than 1]
    D2 --> E2[lambda 1 to 3 mixed]
    D3 --> E3[lambda greater than 3]

    E1 --> F1[High friction High wear]
    E2 --> F2[Transitional behavior]
    E3 --> F3[Low friction Negligible wear]

    style C1 fill:#fdd
    style C2 fill:#ffd
    style C3 fill:#dfd
```

---

### 4.3 Film Thickness vs. Surface Roughness Schematic (Conceptual)

The following ASCII schematic shows how λ is interpreted physically:

```
SURFACE 1 (upper) ─────────────────────
              |              |
         ___  |  ___         |  ___          ___
        /   \ | /   \        | /   \        /   \      <- Asperities
_______/_____\|/_____\_______|/_____\______/_____\___
       (valleys filled with lubricant)

                  h_min (minimum film thickness)
   ←─────────────────────────────────────────────→

SURFACE 2 (lower) ─────────────────────

If h_min >> asperity height  → λ >> 1  → FULL SEPARATION (Thick Film)
If h_min ≈ asperity height  → λ ≈ 1   → OCCASIONAL CONTACT (Mixed)
If h_min <  asperity height  → λ < 1   → DIRECT CONTACT  (Boundary)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term **Specific Film Thickness (λ)** and state the ranges of λ that distinguish Boundary, Mixed, and Full Fluid Film Lubrication regimes.

**Model Answer:**

The specific film thickness λ is a dimensionless parameter that compares the minimum lubricant film thickness to the composite RMS roughness of the contacting surfaces:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}}}$$

It is the most reliable indicator of the lubrication regime:

- **λ < 1** → Boundary Lubrication
- **1 ≤ λ ≤ 3** → Mixed Lubrication
- **λ > 3** → Full Fluid Film (Thick Film) Lubrication

This parameter is preferred over absolute film thickness because it normalizes against the actual surface finish of the components.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Stribeck Curve** and identify the different lubrication regimes on it.

**Model Answer:**

The Stribeck Curve is a plot of the coefficient of friction (μ) on the y-axis versus the Hersey Number (ηN/P) on the x-axis. It identifies three regimes:

```
μ  │  
   │\                    
   │ \  ← BOUNDARY       (friction ≈ 0.1–0.3, weak speed dependence)
   │  \___                
   │      \              
   │       \  ← MIXED    (friction decreases sharply with speed)
   │        \________   
   │                 \  ← FULL FLUID FILM (friction rises with speed)
   │                  \  
   │                   \____
   └──────────────────────────── ηN/P
        ↑                  ↑
    Low speed            High speed
    High load           Low load
```

In the **boundary regime**, friction is governed by the shear strength of surface boundary films. In the **mixed regime**, partial fluid pressure and partial asperity contact share the load. In the **full fluid film regime**, asperities are completely separated by the lubricant, and friction is governed by viscous shear of the fluid — hence it increases with speed.

---

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different **lubrication regimes** based on the value of the specific film thickness λ. For each regime, describe the load-support mechanism, the typical friction coefficient range, and the dominant friction mechanism.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A journal bearing has a minimum lubricant film thickness of $h_{min} = 12 \, \mu m$. The journal has $R_{q1} = 0.8 \, \mu m$ and the bearing has $R_{q2} = 0.6 \, \mu m$.  
(i) Calculate the specific film thickness λ.  
(ii) Identify the lubrication regime.  
(iii) If the operating speed is increased such that $h_{min}$ doubles, what is the new regime?

**Model Solution for (a):**

The lubrication regime classification is based on the dimensionless specific film thickness λ. Three regimes exist:

**1. Boundary Lubrication (λ < 1):**
- **Load Support:** Carried primarily by asperity contacts between the surfaces. Boundary films (physisorbed or chemisorbed molecular layers from lubricant additives) prevent direct metal-to-metal welding.
- **Friction Coefficient:** High — typically **0.1 to 0.3**.
- **Friction Mechanism:** Shearing of the boundary film and asperity deformation.
- **Example:** Slow-moving heavily loaded gears, low-speed journal bearings during start-up/stop.

**2. Mixed Lubrication (1 ≤ λ ≤ 3):**
- **Load Support:** Shared between the partial fluid film (hydrodynamic pressure) and direct asperity contacts.
- **Friction Coefficient:** Moderate — typically **0.02 to 0.1**, decreasing with increasing speed.
- **Friction Mechanism:** Combination of viscous shear in the fluid and asperity shearing.
- **Example:** Moderate-speed gearboxes, mid-RPM journal bearings under variable load.

**3. Thick Film / Full Fluid Film Lubrication (λ > 3):**
- **Load Support:** Carried entirely by the hydrodynamic pressure generated in the lubricant film.
- **Friction Coefficient:** Low — typically **0.001 to 0.01** (but rises with speed).
- **Friction Mechanism:** Viscous shearing of the bulk lubricant.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Hydrodynamic (HL):** Smooth, conformal surfaces (e.g., journal bearings).
  - **Elastohydrodynamic (EHL):** Non-conformal, high-pressure contacts with elastic deformation (e.g., ball bearings, gears).
- **Example:** High-speed journal bearings, well-designed rolling element bearings.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Compute composite roughness:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^{\,2} + R_{q2}^{\,2}} = \sqrt{(0.8)^2 + (0.6)^2} = \sqrt{0.64 + 0.36} = \sqrt{1.00} = 1.0 \, \mu m$$

**(ii) Compute λ:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{12 \, \mu m}{1.0 \, \mu m} = 12$$

Since **λ = 12 > 3**, the bearing operates in the **Full Fluid Film (Thick Film)** regime — specifically **Hydrodynamic Lubrication (HL)** given the conformal journal-bearing geometry.

**(iii) New regime after doubling speed:**

If $h_{min}$ doubles:
$$h_{min,new} = 2 \times 12 = 24 \, \mu m$$
$$\lambda_{new} = \frac{24}{1.0} = 24$$

Since **λ_new = 24 > 3**, the bearing remains in the **Full Fluid Film regime**, but now with a stronger margin of safety. The friction coefficient will increase slightly (right branch of Stribeck curve) due to greater viscous shearing, but wear remains negligible.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)**  
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and operation of the **Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** regime. Why is EHL classified as a "thick film" regime even though absolute film thicknesses are very small (sub-micron)? What is the role of the pressure-viscosity relationship in EHL?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A rolling element bearing operates under the following conditions:  
- Equivalent radius of curvature: $R = 8 \, mm$  
- Dynamic viscosity at atmospheric pressure: $\eta_0 = 0.08 \, Pa \cdot s$  
- Mean entraining velocity: $u = 4 \, m/s$  
- Load per unit length (line contact): $F/L = 2000 \, N/mm$  
Using the simplified Hamrock-Dowson equation for **line contact**:

$$h_0 = 0.16 \, (\eta_0 \, u)^{0.6} \, (R)^{0.6} \, (F/L)^{-0.2}$$

Calculate the central film thickness $h_0$. The composite surface roughness is $\sigma = 0.4 \, \mu m$. Identify the regime.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**EHL — Construction and Operation:**

EHL occurs in **highly loaded, non-conformal contacts** such as ball bearings, roller bearings, and gear teeth. The contact geometry is initially "point" or "line" contact, but under load, the surfaces deform elastically over a contact patch.

The regime is governed by **three simultaneous physical effects**:

1. **Elastic Deformation of Surfaces:** The high contact pressure (typically 1–3 GPa) causes the asperities and the bulk surface to deform elastically, enlarging the contact patch into a "horseshoe" or "rectangular" shape.
2. **Hydrodynamic Action:** The relative motion of the surfaces entrains lubricant into the converging gap, generating hydrodynamic pressure that further separates the surfaces.
3. **Pressure-Viscosity Effect:** As pressure rises in the inlet zone, the lubricant viscosity increases dramatically (often by 1–3 orders of magnitude). This **viscosity rise** significantly enhances the load-carrying capacity.

**Why EHL is classified as Thick Film:**

Although the absolute film thickness in EHL is often sub-micron (e.g., 0.1–1 μm), the relevant metric is the **specific film thickness λ**, not absolute $h_{min}$. EHL contacts involve **highly polished surfaces** ($R_q$ values often < 0.05 μm). Hence:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} > 3 \quad \text{(typically)}$$

This indicates complete asperity separation, which is the *defining* feature of thick film lubrication.

**Role of Pressure-Viscosity Relationship:**

The **Barus equation** describes the exponential viscosity rise with pressure:

$$\eta(p) = \eta_0 \, e^{\alpha_p \, p}$$

where $\alpha_p$ is the pressure-viscosity coefficient (typically 1–3 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ for mineral oils). At EHL pressures (1–3 GPa), this equation (or its Roelands generalization) gives viscosity values **10² to 10⁴ times** higher than atmospheric. This:
- Increases the hydrodynamic load support.
- Creates a near-rigid "glass-like" film at the asperity peaks.
- Prevents film collapse even at extremely high contact pressures.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify inputs (convert to SI):**

$$\eta_0 = 0.08 \, Pa \cdot s, \quad u = 4 \, m/s$$
$$R = 8 \, mm = 0.008 \, m$$
$$F/L = 2000 \, N/mm = 2 \times 10^6 \, N/m$$

**Step 2 — Compute intermediate product $\eta_0 \cdot u$:**

$$\eta_0 \cdot u = 0.08 \times 4 = 0.32 \, Pa \cdot m / s = 0.32 \, N/m^2 \cdot m / s$$

Numerically: $0.32 \, (Pa \cdot m/s)$ — units reduce to N·s/m² × m/s = N·s/m. For Hamrock-Dowson we treat the dimensionless group.

**Step 3 — Apply Hamrock-Dowson line contact formula:**

$$h_0 = 0.16 \times (\eta_0 u)^{0.6} \times R^{0.6} \times (F/L)^{-0.2}$$

Compute each term:

$(\eta_0 u)^{0.6} = (0.32)^{0.6}$
Using logarithms: $\ln(0.32) = -1.139$, so $0.6 \times (-1.139) = -0.6834$, giving $(0.32)^{0.6} = e^{-0.6834} \approx 0.505$

$R^{0.6} = (0.008)^{0.6}$
$\ln(0.008) = -4.828$, $0.6 \times (-4.828) = -2.897$, $e^{-2.897} \approx 0.0552$

$(F/L)^{-0.2} = (2 \times 10^6)^{-0.2}$
$\ln(2 \times 10^6) = 14.508$, $-0.2 \times 14.508 = -2.902$, $e^{-2.902} \approx 0.0549$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$h_0 = 0.16 \times 0.505 \times 0.0552 \times 0.0549$$

$$h_0 = 0.16 \times (0.505 \times 0.0552 \times 0.0549)$$

Compute inner product: $0.505 \times 0.0552 = 0.0279$; $0.0279 \times 0.0549 = 0.001531$

$$h_0 = 0.16 \times 0.001531 = 2.45 \times 10^{-4} \, m = 245 \, \mu m$$

> **NOTE:** This seems unreasonably large for an EHL contact. The Hamrock-Dowson formula expects **dimensionless parameters** (U, G, W) rather than raw SI units. Students should use the **dimensionless form**. The simplified equation $h_0 = 0.16 (\eta_0 u)^{0.6} R^{0.6} (F/L)^{-0.2}$ requires inputs in **specific SI units**: $\eta_0$ in Pa·s, $u$ in m/s, $R$ in m, $F/L$ in N/m.

Re-check using the dimensionless form (Hamrock-Dowson line contact, central film thickness):

$$h_c = 2.69 \, R \, U^{0.67} \, G^{0.53} \, W^{-0.067}$$

where:
- $U = \eta_0 u / (E' R)$
- $G = \alpha_p E'$
- $W = F / (E' L R)$

This more accurate approach is recommended for EHL analysis and produces realistic sub-micron to few-micron film thicknesses.

**Step 5 — Regime Identification:**

Even using the rough estimate, the film thickness is far greater than the composite roughness $\sigma = 0.4 \, \mu m$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_0}{\sigma} \gg 3$$

This confirms operation in the **Full Fluid Film / EHL regime**. The bearing is well-lubricated with negligible asperity contact.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Errors in Hamrock-Dowson:** Using $\eta_0$ in cP (centipoise) or $R$ in mm directly produces **wildly incorrect** results. Always convert to SI base units first, or use the dimensionless form.
> 2. **Misclassification of EHL:** Many students incorrectly classify EHL as "thin film" because absolute $h_{min}$ is small. The correct classification depends on **λ**, not absolute $h_{min}$.
> 3. **Arithmetic Sum vs. RSS for Composite Roughness:** The composite roughness is the **root-sum-square** combination, NOT the arithmetic sum. Using $R_{q1} + R_{q2}$ will give an incorrect (lower) λ.
> 4. **Confusing Stribeck's Y-axis:** Some students plot load or speed on the y-axis. The y-axis is **always coefficient of friction (μ)**.
> 5. **Forgetting the Pressure-Viscosity Effect:** In EHL questions, simply invoking "Reynolds equation" without mentioning the **Barus or Roelands pressure-viscosity relationship** will lose 2–3 marks.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Specific Film Thickness λ** = $h_{min} / \sigma$ is the master parameter for regime identification. NOT absolute film thickness.
- **λ < 1 → Boundary**, **1 ≤ λ ≤ 3 → Mixed**, **λ > 3 → Full Fluid Film (Thick Film)**.
- **Composite Roughness σ** = $\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$ — always RSS, never arithmetic.
- **Stribeck Curve** maps μ vs. Hersey Number ηN/P — three regimes: Boundary (flat high), Mixed (falling), Full Film (rising).
- **Thick Film sub-types:** Hydrodynamic (HL) for conformal contacts; Elastohydrodynamic (EHL) for non-conformal contacts with elastic deformation.
- **EHL** is a thick film regime tribologically (λ > 3) even though absolute film is sub-micron — because surfaces are highly polished.
- **Pressure-Viscosity Relation** (Barus): $\eta = \eta_0 e^{\alpha_p p}$. Essential in EHL — viscosity can rise 10² to 10⁴ times at high pressure.
- **Mixed regime load partition:** $W = W_{fluid} + W_{asperity}$.
- **Hamrock-Dowson EHL Equations** (line contact central: $h_c = 2.69 R U^{0.67} G^{0.53} W^{-0.067}$) — always use dimensionless parameters or correct SI units.
- **Boundary Lubrication additives** (ZDDP, fatty acids, EP additives) form protective films when λ < 1 — these additives are critical at low speeds and high loads.
- **Industrial implication:** Engineering design typically aims for λ > 3 (thick film) for long service life; designs in λ < 1 require careful additive selection and surface engineering.
- **Friction in mixed regime:** $\mu_{mixed} = \alpha \mu_b + (1-\alpha) \mu_f$ where $\alpha$ = asperity load fraction.
- **Hersey Number** $\eta N / P$ is the traditional x-axis for the Stribeck curve, but modern analyses use the **Sommerfeld-corrected** form for bearing-specific analyses.
- **Key transitions on Stribeck curve** occur at specific λ values that depend on roughness ratio and material pair — typical industry values are 1 and 3, but these can shift to 0.5 and 1.5 for very rough surfaces.
- **EHL pressure regime** typically 1–3 GPa — at these pressures, the lubricant film becomes nearly solid (glass-like), supporting load without asperity contact.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Mixed Lubrication** is a transitional lubrication regime in which the lubricant film thickness is of the same order of magnitude as the composite surface roughness of the contacting bodies. In this regime, **both mechanisms operate simultaneously**: a portion of the applied load is supported by the hydrodynamic (or elastohydrodynamic) pressure generated within the lubricant film, while the remaining load is transmitted through direct **asperity-to-asperity contacts** of the opposing surfaces.

Mathematically, mixed lubrication exists when the dimensionless **Lambda ratio (λ)** lies in the approximate range:

$$1 \leq \lambda \leq 3 \quad \text{to} \quad 5$$

where the upper limit depends on the reference standard (BS 7201 / ISO 4406 conventions differ slightly).

## 1.2 The Governing Parameter: Lambda Ratio (λ)

The **specific film thickness** (or lambda ratio) is the single most important non-dimensional number that classifies the lubrication regime. It is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$$

where:
- $h_{min}$ = **minimum lubricant film thickness** (in metres, µm, or inches)
- $\sigma$ = **composite (combined) surface roughness**, given by:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{\,2} + \sigma_2^{\,2}}$$

with $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ being the RMS roughness values of the two contacting surfaces.

> [!NOTE] **Regime Classification via λ (Per Stribeck Curve)**
> - **λ < 1** → **Boundary Lubrication** (asperities fully dominate, film is discontinuous)
> - **1 ≤ λ ≤ 3** → **Mixed Lubrication** (load shared between film and asperities)
> - **λ > 3** → **Full-Film Lubrication** (Hydrodynamic or Elastohydrodynamic – EHL)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a heavy truck driving on a road that is only **partially flooded** with water:
- The **wheels still touch the road (asphalt)** in the higher dry patches → that is the **asperity contact load**.
- The **water that lies between the tyre and the road also pushes back** on the wheel, helping to support some of the truck's weight → that is the **hydrodynamic film load**.

Neither the road alone nor the water alone is doing all the work — **both are sharing the load**. This is exactly what happens in mixed lubrication. The lubricant is not thick enough to fully separate the two surfaces, but it is thick enough to take up *some* of the pressure. As a result, the friction coefficient sits in the *middle* of the Stribeck curve (typically $\mu \approx 0.02$ to $0.08$).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (KTU Board-Exam Emphasis)

- **λ (Lambda Ratio):** Dimensionless; key regime classifier.
- **Composite Roughness (σ):** Measured in **µm** (micrometres) or **µin** (microinches) — RMS values from profilometer traces.
- **Asperity Load Fraction (A_f):** A dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1 indicating the proportion of total load carried by asperity contacts.
- **Asperity Contact Pressure:** Local pressures can reach **1–5 GPa** even when nominal contact pressure is < 1 GPa.
- **Typical Viscosity Range of Interest:** $\eta \approx 0.01$ to $1.0$ **Pa·s** at operating temperature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Stribeck Curve — Locating Mixed Lubrication

The Stribeck curve is a foundational tribology diagram that plots the **friction coefficient (μ)** as a function of the **Hersey number (ηN/P)**, where:
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s)
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s or rad/s depending on convention)
- $P$ = load per unit projected area (Pa)

> [!NOTE] **Three Distinct Zones of the Stribeck Curve**
> 1. **Boundary Zone (low ηN/P):** Friction is determined primarily by surface chemistry and boundary films (e.g., EP additives, fatty acids). Friction coefficient is *high* (μ ≈ 0.1 to 0.3) and *decreases* as speed/viscosity increases.
> 2. **Mixed Zone (intermediate ηN/P):** Friction reaches its **minimum**. The transition from asperity-dominated to film-dominated load support is occurring here.
> 3. **Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Zone (high ηN/P):** Friction *rises* again because viscous drag in the lubricant itself becomes significant.

## 2.2 Load Partitioning in Mixed Lubrication

The defining physics of mixed lubrication is the **partitioning of the total normal load (W)** between two parallel mechanisms:

$$W_{total} = W_{film} + W_{asperity}$$

where:
- $W_{film}$ = load supported by the lubricant's hydrodynamic / EHL pressure
- $W_{asperity}$ = load transmitted through direct contact of surface asperities

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical KTU Insight**
> In the mixed regime, the friction coefficient is a *weighted sum* of the boundary friction and the hydrodynamic friction:
> $$\mu_{mixed} = A_f \cdot \mu_{boundary} + (1 - A_f) \cdot \mu_{film}$$
> where $A_f$ is the **asperity load fraction** (0 to 1).

## 2.3 Why Mixed Lubrication Matters in Engineering

Mixed lubrication is the **operating reality** for the vast majority of machine components during:
- **Start-up and shut-down** of machinery (low speed → low λ)
- **Highly loaded gears** (especially hypoid, spur, helical under high torque)
- **Cam-follower systems** in IC engines
- **Rolling element bearings** under heavy radial loads
- **Metal-forming operations** (rolling, drawing, extrusion)

If not properly managed, the asperity component leads to:
- Adhesive wear
- Surface fatigue (micropitting, macropitting)
- High contact temperatures
- Premature lubricant degradation (oxidation)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma}$ | Lambda ratio (specific film thickness) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{\,2} + \sigma_2^{\,2}}$ | Composite surface roughness (RMS) | m or µm |
| 3 | $W_{total} = W_{film} + W_{asperity}$ | Load partitioning in mixed regime | N |
| 4 | $A_f = \dfrac{W_{asperity}}{W_{total}}$ | Asperity load fraction (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_{b} + (1 - A_f) \mu_{f}$ | Mixed friction coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $h_{min} \approx 3.63 \, R' \, U^{0.68} \, G^{0.49} \, W'^{-0.073} \left(1 - e^{-0.68 k}\right)$ | Hamrock-Dowson EHL minimum film thickness (ball-on-flat, elliptical) | m |
| 7 | $R' = \left(\dfrac{1}{R_x} + \dfrac{1}{R_y}\right)^{-1}$ | Reduced radius of curvature | m |
| 8 | $U = \dfrac{\eta_0 \, u}{E' \, R'}$ | Speed parameter (dimensionless) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W' = \dfrac{w}{E' \, R'^{\,2}}$ | Load parameter (dimensionless) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $G = \alpha \, E'$ | Material parameter (pressure–viscosity index × reduced modulus) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $A_f = 0.99 \cdot \exp\left[-2.823 \, \lambda \cdot \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{R'}\right)^{0.111}\right]$ | Empirical asperity load fraction (Hamrock-Dowson correlation) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $A_c = \dfrac{\kappa}{8} \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\pi}} \cdot A_f \cdot \dfrac{W_{total}}{E' \sqrt{\sigma / R'}}$ | Total real area of asperity contact (asperity deformation term) | m² |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Variable Glossary for the Formulas Above**
> - $\eta_0$ = dynamic viscosity at atmospheric pressure (Pa·s)
> - $u$ = entraining surface velocity (m/s)
> - $E' = 2 \left[ \dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2} \right]^{-1}$ = reduced elastic modulus
> - $w$ = normal load on contact (N)
> - $\alpha$ = pressure–viscosity coefficient (Pa⁻¹)
> - $k$ = ellipticity parameter of contact (= a/b)
> - $\kappa$ = constant ≈ 0.798 for typical steel-steel contacts

## 2.5 Asperity Deformation Theories Used in Mixed Lubrication

There are two classical statistical models used to predict $W_{asperity}$:

1. **Hertzian-Based Model (Greenwood & Tripp, 1967):** Treats asperities as spherical summits with a Gaussian height distribution. Most commonly used in KTU-level work.
2. **Persson's Diffusion Model:** Treats contact mechanics from a contact mechanics + percolation perspective. More advanced — not typically required at the B.Tech level.

The Greenwood–Tripp approach gives the asperity load as:

$$W_{asperity} = \dfrac{8}{3\sqrt{2\pi}} \, (E' \sqrt{\sigma}) \, A_n \, F_{5/2}(h/\sigma)$$

where $A_n$ is the nominal contact area and $F_{5/2}$ is a surface integral function of the dimensionless interference $h/\sigma$.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Composite Surface Roughness (σ)

**Statement:** The composite RMS roughness of two contacting surfaces is the root-sum-square of the individual RMS roughness values.

**Starting Premise:** Surface heights of two independent surfaces are statistically uncorrelated random variables with variances $\sigma_1^2$ and $\sigma_2^2$.

**Step 1 — Express variance of the sum:**

For two independent random variables $X$ and $Y$:

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y) + 2\,\text{Cov}(X,Y)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the zero-covariance condition:**

Since the surface heights are independent (uncorrelated), $\text{Cov}(X,Y) = 0$:

$$\text{Var}(X + Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$$

**Step 3 — Convert variance to RMS roughness:**

By definition, the RMS roughness is the square root of the variance of surface heights:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X + Y)} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^{\,2} + \sigma_2^{\,2}}}$$

> This is the **standard KTU formula** to memorize.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mixed-Regime Friction Coefficient

**Starting Premise:** The applied normal load $W$ is split between asperity contacts and the lubricant film:

$$W = W_a + W_f$$

where subscript $a$ = asperity, $f$ = film.

**Step 1 — Define asperity load fraction $A_f$:**

$$A_f \equiv \frac{W_a}{W}$$

By definition, $0 \leq A_f \leq 1$.

**Step 2 — Express each friction force component:**

Friction force from asperity contact (Coulomb + boundary film shear):

$$F_a = \mu_b \, W_a$$

Friction force from viscous shear in the lubricant film:

$$F_f = \mu_f \, W_f$$

**Step 3 — Sum the friction forces:**

Total tangential friction force:

$$F_{total} = F_a + F_f = \mu_b W_a + \mu_f W_f$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of total load $W$:**

Substituting $W_a = A_f W$ and $W_f = (1 - A_f) W$:

$$F_{total} = \mu_b A_f W + \mu_f (1 - A_f) W = W \left[ A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f) \mu_f \right]$$

**Step 5 — Divide by total load to obtain the effective friction coefficient:**

$$\mu_{mixed} = \frac{F_{total}}{W} = A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f) \mu_f$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f) \mu_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Limiting-Case Verification**
> - When $A_f = 1$ (pure boundary): $\mu_{mixed} = \mu_b$ ✓
> - When $A_f = 0$ (pure film): $\mu_{mixed} = \mu_f$ ✓
> - When $0 < A_f < 1$ (mixed): $\mu_{mixed}$ is a **convex combination** of the two → exactly the physical expectation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: λ Computation and Regime Classification (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Past Paper Style):**

A steel pin ($\sigma_1 = 0.4 \, \mu m$, $E_1 = 210$ GPa, $\nu_1 = 0.30$) rotates against a steel disc ($\sigma_2 = 0.6 \, \mu m$, $E_2 = 210$ GPa, $\nu_2 = 0.30$) under a lubricant whose minimum film thickness at the operating point is calculated (via Hamrock–Dowson) as $h_{min} = 1.5 \, \mu m$. 

Tasks:
1. Calculate the lambda ratio.
2. Identify the lubrication regime.
3. If the boundary friction coefficient is $\mu_b = 0.12$ and the full-film friction coefficient is $\mu_f = 0.005$, and the asperity load fraction at this condition is $A_f = 0.45$, calculate the operating mixed-regime friction coefficient.

**Step 1 — Compute composite roughness:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2} = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (0.6)^2} \; \mu m$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.16 + 0.36} \; \mu m = \sqrt{0.52} \; \mu m$$

$$\boxed{\sigma \approx 0.7211 \; \mu m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute lambda ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{1.5 \; \mu m}{0.7211 \; \mu m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 2.08}$$

**Step 3 — Identify regime:**

Since $1 \leq \lambda \leq 3$, the contact operates in the **MIXED LUBRICATION REGIME**.

**Step 4 — Compute mixed friction coefficient:**

$$\mu_{mixed} = A_f \, \mu_b + (1 - A_f) \, \mu_f$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = (0.45)(0.12) + (1 - 0.45)(0.005)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.054 + (0.55)(0.005)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.054 + 0.00275$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} \approx 0.0568}$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Interpretation**
> Even though the film is contributing (55% of load on the film), the *asperity contact friction coefficient* ($\mu_b = 0.12$) is so much larger than the *film friction coefficient* ($\mu_f = 0.005$) that **the asperity load fraction dominates the friction** — a hallmark of mixed lubrication in real gear/bearing systems.

---

## 3.4 Derivation Summary Matrix

| # | Concept | Key Formula | Key Assumption |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Composite RMS roughness | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | Uncorrelated surface heights |
| 2 | Lambda ratio | $\lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$ | Steady-state EHL film |
| 3 | Load balance | $W = W_a + W_f$ | Quasi-static normal equilibrium |
| 4 | Asperity load fraction | $A_f = W_a / W$ | $0 \leq A_f \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Mixed friction coefficient | $\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_b + (1-A_f)\mu_f$ | Linear superposition of friction forces |
| 6 | Empirical $A_f$ (Hamrock) | $A_f = 0.99 \exp[-2.823 \lambda (\sigma/R')^{0.111}]$ | Smooth surfaces, elliptical EHL contact |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**Q:** Define *mixed lubrication* and state the range of the lambda ratio (λ) over which it occurs.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Answer (3 Marks)]**
> Mixed lubrication is the regime in which the lubricant film thickness is comparable to the composite surface roughness of the contacting bodies. The applied load is **shared** between the **fluid film** (which carries part of the load by hydrodynamic or elastohydrodynamic pressure) and the **asperity contacts** (which carry the remainder by direct metal-to-metal interaction).
>
> It is identified by the lambda ratio in the range:
> $$1 \le \lambda \le 3$$
> where $\lambda = h_{min}/\sigma$ and $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$.

### Question A.2
**Q:** What is the *asperity load fraction* $A_f$, and what are its limiting values in pure boundary and pure full-film lubrication?

> **[Model Answer (3 Marks)]**
> The asperity load fraction is the proportion of the total normal load that is transmitted through asperity-to-asperity contact:
> $$A_f = \frac{W_{asperity}}{W_{total}}$$
> - **Pure boundary lubrication:** $A_f = 1$ (entire load on asperities).
> - **Pure full-film (EHL) lubrication:** $A_f = 0$ (no asperity contact).
> - **Mixed lubrication:** $0 < A_f < 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 3]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* Derive an expression for the **composite surface roughness** $\sigma$ of two contacting bodies from first principles, and explain why the two surface roughnesses combine as a root-sum-square rather than a simple arithmetic sum.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* A steel shaft ($\sigma_1 = 0.3 \, \mu m$) supports a steel thrust bearing ($\sigma_2 = 0.5 \, \mu m$). Under operating conditions, the minimum EHL film thickness is $h_{min} = 0.85 \, \mu m$. 

(i) Compute the lambda ratio and identify the regime.
(ii) If $\mu_b = 0.10$, $\mu_f = 0.004$, and $A_f = 0.60$, compute the mixed-regime friction coefficient.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The surface heights $z_1$ and $z_2$ of the two surfaces are random variables with variances $\sigma_1^2$ and $\sigma_2^2$. The combined surface is described by the random variable $z_c = z_1 + z_2$.

For two independent random variables, the variance of the sum is the sum of the variances (cross-covariance term vanishes):

$$\sigma_c^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + 2\,\rho\,\sigma_1\sigma_2$$

Since the surfaces are statistically independent, the correlation coefficient $\rho = 0$:

$$\sigma_c^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2$$

Taking the square root gives the **composite RMS roughness**:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}}$$

The root-sum-square form arises because the random variations *add in quadrature* (variance space), not *linearly*. An arithmetic sum $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ would imply fully correlated surfaces (the worst possible case), which is unphysical for two independently manufactured or machined surfaces.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Regime Identification**

Composite roughness:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{(0.3)^2 + (0.5)^2} \; \mu m = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.25} = \sqrt{0.34} \; \mu m \approx 0.5831 \; \mu m$$

Lambda ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sigma} = \frac{0.85}{0.5831} \approx 1.458$$

Since $1 \le \lambda \le 3$ → **Mixed Lubrication Regime**.

**(ii) Mixed Friction Coefficient**

$$\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f)\mu_f$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = (0.60)(0.10) + (0.40)(0.004)$$

$$\mu_{mixed} = 0.060 + 0.0016 = 0.0616$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} \approx 0.0616}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 – Module 3]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)* With the aid of a labelled **Stribeck curve diagram (block schematic)**, discuss the three lubrication regimes — boundary, mixed, and full-film — and explain why the friction coefficient reaches a **minimum** in the mixed regime.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)* Derive the expression for the **effective friction coefficient** in the mixed-lubrication regime, starting from the principle of load partitioning. Clearly state all assumptions.

---

**Model Solution (a):**

In the Stribeck curve (friction coefficient μ vs Hersey number ηN/P):

1. **Boundary Zone (low ηN/P):** The lubricant film has collapsed; asperities are in direct contact. Friction is governed by boundary films (e.g., EP additives, oxide layers) and is *high* (μ ≈ 0.1–0.3). As ηN/P increases, asperity junctions form and break more easily, so μ *decreases*.

2. **Mixed Zone (intermediate ηN/P):** A partial fluid film begins to develop, sharing the load with the asperities. The friction contribution from asperity shearing is falling sharply, while viscous friction in the (still-thin) film is still small. The two effects cross over, producing a **minimum in μ** (typically 0.02–0.08).

3. **Full-Film Zone (high ηN/P):** Surfaces are fully separated by the lubricant film. Friction is now purely viscous (shearing of the fluid), which *increases* with speed and viscosity → μ *rises* again.

**Why a minimum occurs:** In the boundary regime, μ is dominated by a *high* asperity-friction term that is decreasing. In the full-film regime, μ is dominated by a *low-but-rising* viscous-friction term. The transition between these two regimes therefore exhibits a **convex dip** — the Stribeck minimum.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Assumption 1 — Load Partition:**
The total normal load is the sum of the load carried by asperities and the load carried by the fluid film:

$$W = W_a + W_f$$

**Assumption 2 — Asperity load fraction definition:**

$$A_f = \frac{W_a}{W}, \quad W_f = (1 - A_f) W$$

**Assumption 3 — Friction forces are additive (linear superposition):**

Tangential force from asperity contacts (Coulomb friction with boundary film):

$$F_a = \mu_b W_a$$

Tangential force from viscous film shear (hydrodynamic friction):

$$F_f = \mu_f W_f$$

**Assumption 4 — Total friction force is the sum:**

$$F = F_a + F_f = \mu_b W_a + \mu_f W_f$$

**Substitute load fractions:**

$$F = \mu_b (A_f W) + \mu_f (1 - A_f) W$$

**Divide by total normal load $W$:**

$$\boxed{\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f) \mu_f}$$

This expression interpolates **linearly** between $\mu_b$ (at $A_f = 1$) and $\mu_f$ (at $A_f = 0$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully)**
> 1. **Using arithmetic instead of root-sum-square for $\sigma$:** Many students write $\sigma = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$. This is a **physical nonsense** for two independent surfaces. The composite roughness is $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$. **Loss: 2–3 marks** if done incorrectly.
> 2. **Forgetting units in $\lambda$ computation:** Always quote $\lambda$ as **dimensionless**, but explicitly show $h_{min}$ and $\sigma$ in the **same units** (e.g., both in µm). Mismatched units will lose a mark.
> 3. **Confusing boundary and full-film friction coefficients:** $\mu_b$ is typically **one to two orders of magnitude higher** than $\mu_f$. Mixing these up produces a friction coefficient that is **physically wrong** by a factor of 10.
> 4. **Stating "mixed lubrication occurs when $\lambda = 1$" (a single point):** It is a *range* (1 ≤ λ ≤ 3). Examiners specifically deduct for the equality-only answer.
> 5. **Failing to identify the regime** after computing λ: The "**compute and classify**" step is worth **at least 1 mark** in a 7-mark sub-question. Always state: *"Since $\lambda$ lies in [range], the contact operates in the MIXED LUBRICATION regime."*
> 6. **Writing $\mu_{mixed} = \mu_b + \mu_f$ instead of the weighted form:** This ignores the load-partitioning logic and loses 3–4 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Density Revision Checklist — Mixed Lubrication**
> - **Definition:** Mixed lubrication is a regime in which the lubricant film and surface asperities **simultaneously** carry the load, with $1 \le \lambda \le 3$.
> - **Lambda Ratio:** $\lambda = h_{min} / \sigma$ is the master regime-classifier. **Memorize it cold.**
> - **Composite Roughness:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ (RSS, never arithmetic sum).
> - **Load Partition:** $W = W_a + W_f$ — this is the **governing physics** of the mixed regime.
> - **Asperity Load Fraction:** $A_f = W_a / W \in [0, 1]$; $A_f = 1$ (boundary), $A_f = 0$ (EHL).
> - **Friction Formula (HIGH YIELD):** $\mu_{mixed} = A_f \mu_b + (1 - A_f)\mu_f$.
> - **Stribeck Minimum:** Friction is at its **lowest** in the mixed regime — a direct consequence of the two competing contributions.
> - **Hamrock–Dowson EHL:** Used to estimate $h_{min}$ for point/line contacts in the **upper** mixed-regime / full-film boundary.
> - **Empirical $A_f$ (Hamrock):** $A_f = 0.99 \exp\!\left[-2.823 \lambda (\sigma/R')^{0.111}\right]$ — used in advanced numerical work.
> - **Engineering Importance:** Most real components (gears, bearings, cam-followers) operate in the mixed regime during start-up, shutdown, and under heavy load. **Designers must ensure $\lambda \ge 1$ under all operating conditions** to avoid adhesive wear and fatigue failure.
> - **Viscosity–Temperature Rule of Thumb:** As oil temperature increases, $\eta$ drops, $h_{min}$ drops, $\lambda$ drops, $A_f$ rises — pushing the system from full-film **into** the mixed or even boundary regime. **This is why oil coolers and correct viscosity grades are critical.**
> - **Additives that help in mixed regime:** EP (extreme-pressure) additives (e.g., ZDDP), friction modifiers (MoS₂, graphite), and viscosity-index improvers.
> - **Typical Friction Ranges:** Boundary μ ≈ 0.1–0.3; Mixed μ ≈ 0.02–0.08; Full-film μ ≈ 0.005–0.02.
> - **Unit Discipline:** Always keep $h_{min}$ and $\sigma$ in **the same units** before computing $\lambda$. The ratio is dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stribeck Curve and Its Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stribeck Curve and Its Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Stribeck Curve** is a fundamental graphical representation in tribology that plots the **coefficient of friction (μ)** as a function of the **Hersey Number (or Sommerfeld Parameter group)**, which combines the effects of lubricant viscosity, relative sliding speed, and applied load. The curve was first conceptualized by **Richard Stribeck (1902)** and experimentally refined by later researchers to characterize the transition between different lubrication regimes in machine elements such as journal bearings, rolling-element bearings, gears, and cam-follower systems.

$$ \text{Stribeck Curve: } \mu = f\left(\frac{\eta \cdot N}{P}\right) $$

where:
- **μ** = Coefficient of friction (dimensionless)
- **η (eta)** = Dynamic viscosity of the lubricant (Pa·s or cP)
- **N** = Rotational speed (rev/s) or relative sliding speed (m/s)
- **P** = Load per unit projected area or unit length (N/m² or N/m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Stribeck curve is the master key to understanding the transition between **Boundary Lubrication**, **Mixed Lubrication**, and **Hydrodynamic (Full-Film) Lubrication** regimes. It is a guaranteed question topic in every KTU ESE cycle for PEMET633.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **car driving on a flooded road**:
- At **low speed**, the tires physically touch the asphalt through the water film → high friction, you feel the road texture. This is **Boundary Lubrication**.
- As you **accelerate moderately**, the water partially lifts the tire → reduced friction but still some contact. This is **Mixed Lubrication**.
- At **very high speed**, the tire completely lifts off the road on a wedge of water → near-zero friction, you lose steering control. This is **Hydrodynamic Lubrication** (the "hydroplaning" effect).

In a real bearing, the lubricant film behaves exactly like this water film — and the Stribeck curve captures the entire journey from "touching" to "floating" on a single graph.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Stribeck curve is **not** a single curve but a **family of U-shaped curves** whose minimum (the optimal operating point) shifts based on lubricant viscosity, surface roughness, and load. Lowering the curve = better tribological performance.

### 1.3 The Hersey Number — The Master Variable

The horizontal axis of the Stribeck curve uses the **Hersey Number (H)**:

$$ H = \frac{\eta \cdot N}{P} \quad \text{(units: dimensionless when P is load per unit length)} $$

Alternatively, in terms of linear velocity (V = 2πrN) and pressure:

$$ \lambda_H = \frac{\eta \cdot V}{P} $$

This single dimensionless group captures the **speed-viscosity-load** relationship, making it possible to scale laboratory results to real machines.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**Viscous Speed over Load**" — the higher the viscosity or speed, or the lower the load, the more you move rightward on the Stribeck curve (toward full-film lubrication).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Lubrication Regimes (Detailed Breakdown)

The Stribeck curve is divided into **four distinct regimes**, each governed by different physical mechanisms:

#### Regime I — Boundary Lubrication
- **Hersey Number range:** Very low (H → 0)
- **Film thickness (h):** h ≪ Composite surface roughness (σ)
- **Mechanism:** Asperity-to-asperity contact dominates. The lubricant film is thinner than the surface peaks, so solid contacts carry the load. Friction is governed by **surface chemistry** and **boundary additives** (e.g., ZDDP, MoS₂, fatty acids forming adsorbed monolayers).
- **Friction behavior:** Friction is **high and relatively constant** (μ ≈ 0.1 to 0.3). It is largely **independent of viscosity** because the hydrodynamic film is non-existent.
- **Wear:** **High** — abrasive and adhesive wear dominate.

#### Regime II — Mixed Lubrication
- **Hersey Number range:** Low to moderate
- **Film thickness:** h ≈ σ (comparable to surface roughness)
- **Mechanism:** A partial hydrodynamic film forms, supporting a fraction of the load, while the remaining load is still carried by asperity contacts. Both solid friction and viscous shearing contribute.
- **Friction behavior:** Friction **decreases sharply** as Hersey number increases. The curve enters its steep descending limb.
- **Wear:** **Moderate** — fatigue and mild abrasion.

#### Regime III — Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)
- **Hersey Number range:** Moderate to high
- **Mechanism:** Predominantly in **rolling-element bearings and gears** where contact pressures are extremely high (GPa range). The pressure deforms the asperities **elastically** and the lubricant viscosity increases **dramatically** (piezo-viscous effect), creating a thin but very robust film.
- **Friction behavior:** Friction reaches its **minimum value (μ_min)** at the bottom of the U-curve. The specific film thickness ratio (λ = h/σ) is typically between 1 and 3.

#### Regime IV — Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Lubrication
- **Hersey Number range:** High
- **Film thickness:** h ≫ σ
- **Mechanism:** A complete lubricant film fully separates the two surfaces. Load is supported entirely by the **pressure generated within the fluid film** (Reynolds equation). No solid contact.
- **Friction behavior:** Friction **rises again** with Hersey number because viscous shearing of the thick film itself generates resistance.
- **Wear:** **Negligible** — pure fluid friction.

### 2.2 The Specific Film Thickness (Lambda Ratio)

A more practical engineering metric is the **specific film thickness**:

$$ \lambda = \frac{h_{\min}}{\sigma} = \frac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}} $$

where:
- **h_min** = Minimum lubricant film thickness (m)
- **σ (sigma)** = Composite surface roughness (m)
- **R_q1, R_q2** = RMS roughness of surfaces 1 and 2

| λ Range | Lubrication Regime | Expected Wear |
|---|---|---|
| λ < 1 | Boundary | Severe |
| 1 ≤ λ < 3 | Mixed | Moderate |
| 3 ≤ λ < 5 | Full Film / EHL | Minimal |
| λ ≥ 5 | Full Film (safe) | Negligible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** λ = 1 is the **lambda-one criterion** — below this value, asperity contact is guaranteed, and boundary lubrication additives become essential.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Hersey Number (rotational form) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda_H = \dfrac{\eta V}{P}$ | Hersey Number (linear form) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $h_{\min} = \dfrac{2.65 \, R_x \, U^{0.7} \, G^{0.54}}{W^{0.13}}$ | Hamrock-Dowson EHL minimum film thickness | m |
| 4 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | Specific film thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\mu_{\text{boundary}} \approx \dfrac{\tau_s}{P}$ | Boundary friction (shear strength model) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu_{\text{viscous}} \propto \dfrac{\eta V}{P} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R/h}$ | Viscous (full-film) friction component | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\mu_{\text{total}} = \mu_{\text{boundary}}(1-\phi) + \mu_{\text{fluid}} \cdot \phi$ | Composite friction (φ = film fill ratio) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Sommerfeld Number } S = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ | Journal bearing similarity parameter | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In KTU exams, students often confuse **Hersey Number (H)** with **Sommerfeld Number (S)**. They are related but **not identical** — Sommerfeld Number includes the radius-to-clearance ratio (r/c) of the bearing geometry.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why the Stribeck Curve Matters

1. **Bearing Selection:** Engineers choose lubricants (ISO VG grade) and operating speeds to land at the **bottom of the U-curve** (minimum friction) for maximum efficiency.
2. **Energy Efficiency:** In industrial machinery, friction losses account for **~20% of global energy consumption**. Operating in Regime IV saves enormous energy.
3. **Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration and wear analysis can detect when a machine has shifted from Regime IV → III → II → I, signaling **lubrication failure**.
4. **Design of Lubricants:** Additive chemistry is tuned to extend the **boundary regime performance** (left side of curve) for start-stop operations.
5. **Fretting & Starvation:** Stribeck curve analysis reveals when lubricant **starvation** shifts the curve upward.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Example:** In wind turbine gearboxes, the bearings operate at high Hersey numbers (full-film) during normal rotation. However, during low-wind idling, the curve shifts leftward into mixed/boundary lubrication — causing **white etching cracks (WECS)**, a major industry failure mode.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hersey Number from Petroff's Friction Model

**Starting Point:** Petroff's classical equation for a lightly loaded journal bearing considers the lubricant as a simple shear film with no pressure-induced load support.

The shear stress in the lubricant film is given by Newton's law of viscosity:

$$ \tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \cdot \frac{V}{h} = \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} $$

where:
- **V** = Linear velocity at the journal surface (m/s) = $2\pi r N$
- **h** = Film thickness ≈ radial clearance **c** (m)
- **N** = Rotational speed (rev/s)
- **r** = Journal radius (m)
- **η** = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

**Step 1 — Friction Force on the Journal:**

The friction force is the shear stress integrated over the projected bearing area:

$$ F_f = \tau \cdot A = \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} \cdot (2 \pi r L) = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c} $$

where **L** is the bearing length.

**Step 2 — Friction Torque:**

The friction torque about the journal axis is:

$$ T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^3 L \eta N}{c} $$

**Step 3 — Coefficient of Friction:**

The coefficient of friction is defined as friction force divided by normal load **W**:

$$ \mu = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c \cdot W} $$

**Step 4 — Rearranging to Hersey Form:**

Since projected area is $A = 2 r L$, the load per unit projected area is $P = W / (2 r L)$:

$$ \mu = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c \cdot W} = \frac{2 \pi^2 r \eta N}{c \cdot P} \cdot \frac{1}{\pi} = \frac{2 \pi r}{c} \cdot \frac{\eta N}{P} \cdot \frac{\pi}{\pi} $$

**Simplification:** Petroff's friction coefficient is proportional to the Hersey number times a geometric factor:

$$ \mu_{\text{Petroff}} = \frac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)}{P} \cdot \eta N = \frac{2 \pi^2 (r/c)}{P} \cdot H $$

**Interpretation:** The **linear proportionality** $\mu \propto \frac{\eta N}{P}$ is the **right-hand ascending limb** of the Stribeck curve (full-film / hydrodynamic region). This is why friction **rises** as Hersey number increases in the hydrodynamic regime — Petroff's model captures exactly this trend.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Petroff equation is the *theoretical foundation* of the Stribeck curve's right-hand branch. You may be asked to derive it or to comment on its limitations (it ignores pressure viscosity, cavitation, and asperity contact).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Friction Coefficient in Each Regime

| Regime | Physical Model | Governing Equation | Trend of μ vs H |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boundary (I)** | Asperity shear + boundary film | $\mu \approx \tau_s / P_{\text{contact}}$ (shear strength limited) | Nearly **constant** (μ ≈ 0.1–0.3) |
| **Mixed (II)** | Partial film + asperity | $\mu = (1-\phi)\mu_{\text{bdry}} + \phi \mu_{\text{fluid}}$ where $\phi$ is fill ratio | **Steeply decreasing** |
| **EHL (III)** | Elastic deformation + piezo-viscosity | $\mu \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ (visco-elastic) | **Minimum of U-curve** |
| **Hydrodynamic (IV)** | Full film viscous shear | $\mu = C \cdot \dfrac{\eta N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$ (Petroff form) | **Linearly increasing** |

### 3.3 Derivation of Film Thickness (Hamrock-Dowson Isothermal EHL)

For elliptical contacts (point or line), the **Hamrock-Dowson equation** gives the central/minimum film thickness:

$$ h_{\min} = 2.65 \, R_x \, U^{0.7} \, G^{0.54} \, W^{-0.13} $$

where:
- **R_x** = Effective radius in rolling direction (m) = $\left(\dfrac{1}{R_{1x}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{2x}}\right)^{-1}$
- **U** = Speed parameter = $\dfrac{\eta_0 \, U_e}{E' \, R_x}$ (dimensionless)
- **G** = Material parameter = $\alpha \, E'$ (dimensionless)
- **W** = Load parameter = $\dfrac{F}{E' \, R_x^2}$ (dimensionless)
- **U_e** = Entrainment velocity (m/s)
- **E'** = Effective elastic modulus (Pa)
- **α** = Pressure-viscosity coefficient (Pa⁻¹)
- **η₀** = Atmospheric viscosity (Pa·s)

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1:** Compute the effective radius based on the geometry of the contacting bodies.

$$ \frac{1}{R_x} = \frac{1}{R_{1x}} + \frac{1}{R_{2x}} \quad \text{(for ball-on-flat or similar)} $$
