**Step 2:** Compute the entrainment velocity (mean surface velocity):

$$ U_e = \frac{U_1 + U_2}{2} $$

**Step 3:** Compute the effective elastic modulus:

$$ \frac{1}{E'} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \frac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Form the dimensionless groups U, G, W and substitute.

**Step 5:** The result $h_{\min}$ is plugged into $\lambda = h_{\min}/\sigma$ to determine the lubrication regime using the table in Section 2.2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A typical KTU problem gives you bearing geometry, load, speed, viscosity, and roughness, then asks you to: (a) compute $h_{\min}$, (b) compute $\lambda$, (c) identify the regime, (d) state the expected wear level.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Journal radius r = 50 mm = 0.05 m
- Radial clearance c = 0.1 mm = 1 × 10⁻⁴ m
- Viscosity η = 0.05 Pa·s
- Rotational speed N = 1500 rpm = 25 rev/s
- Load W = 5000 N
- Bearing length L = 100 mm = 0.1 m
- Surface roughness R_q1 = R_q2 = 0.4 μm

**Find:** (a) Hersey Number, (b) Petroff's friction coefficient, (c) Friction power loss.

**Solution (a):**

Projected area $A = 2 r L = 2 \times 0.05 \times 0.1 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$

Load per unit area $P = W/A = 5000 / 0.01 = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$

$$ H = \frac{\eta N}{P} = \frac{0.05 \times 25}{5 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.25}{5 \times 10^5} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} $$

**Solution (b):**

$$ \mu_{\text{Petroff}} = \frac{2 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c \cdot W} $$

$$ \mu = \frac{2 \pi^2 (0.05)^2 (0.1)(0.05)(25)}{(1 \times 10^{-4})(5000)} $$

$$ \mu = \frac{2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0025 \times 0.1 \times 1.25}{0.5} $$

$$ \mu = \frac{0.006168}{0.5} = 0.01234 $$

**Solution (c):**

Friction force $F_f = \mu W = 0.01234 \times 5000 = 61.7$ N

Linear sliding velocity $V = 2 \pi r N = 2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 25 = 7.854$ m/s

Friction power $P_{\text{loss}} = F_f \times V = 61.7 \times 7.854 = 484.5$ W

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Grading Style:** Always show units in every line, circle the final answer, and explicitly state the operating regime at the end of such problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stribeck Curve Regimes

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the operational logic of the four lubrication regimes governed by the Stribeck curve. It is rendered as a **functional architecture flow** rather than a literal curve drawing, which provides clearer insight for exam revision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Element in Operation] --> B{Calculate Hersey Number H = eta N / P}
    B -->|H less than 10^-7| C[REGIME I: Boundary Lubrication]
    B -->|H between 10^-7 and 10^-5| D[REGIME II: Mixed Lubrication]
    B -->|H between 10^-5 and 10^-3| E[REGIME III: Elastohydrodynamic]
    B -->|H greater than 10^-3| F[REGIME IV: Hydrodynamic Full Film]
    C --> C1[Asperity Contact Dominates]
    C1 --> C2[Friction is HIGH and Constant]
    C2 --> C3[Wear: Severe Adhesive and Abrasive]
    D --> D1[Partial Film and Asperity Load Share]
    D1 --> D2[Friction DECREASES Steeply]
    D2 --> D3[Wear: Moderate Fatigue]
    E --> E1[Elastic Deformation and Piezo-Viscosity]
    E1 --> E2[Friction Reaches MINIMUM]
    E2 --> E3[Wear: Minimal - Design Target]
    F --> F1[Complete Fluid Film Separation]
    F1 --> F2[Friction RISES Linearly with H]
    F2 --> F3[Wear: Negligible - Ideal Operating Zone]
    C3 --> Z[Engineering Action: Add Boundary Lubricant Additives]
    D3 --> Z
    E3 --> Y[Engineering Action: Maintain Current Lubricant Grade]
    F3 --> Y
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Decision Tree for Lubrication Regime Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: eta, N, P, Surface Roughness] --> B[Compute Hersey Number H]
    A --> C[Compute Minimum Film Thickness h_min]
    B --> D[Identify Regime from H]
    C --> E[Compute Specific Film Thickness lambda]
    E --> F{lambda less than 1}
    E --> G{lambda between 1 and 3}
    E --> H{lambda greater than 3}
    F --> I[Boundary: Use EP Additives]
    G --> J[Mixed: Use Anti-Wear Additives]
    H --> K[Full Film: Use Base Oil Only]
    D --> L[Cross-Verify Regime with H]
    L --> M[Output: Recommended Lubricant and Operating Condition]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Notes for Students:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `C1`, `D3`) — **no reserved keywords** are used as node names.
> - All node labels are **plain uppercase text** with hyphens and slashes only — no bold, italics, or HTML tables.
> - The diagrams use **left-to-right** and **top-to-bottom** directional flow to match the natural reading order of KTU textbook sections.
> - If Mermaid fails to render in your viewer, draw the classic **U-shaped Stribeck curve** by hand: y-axis = μ (coefficient of friction), x-axis = log(ηN/P). Mark the four regions I, II, III, IV from left to right.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper]**

Define the **Stribeck curve**. What does the Hersey number represent, and what are the four lubrication regimes identified on the curve?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stribeck curve** is a plot of the **coefficient of friction (μ)** versus the **Hersey number (H = ηN/P)**, showing how friction varies with operating conditions in lubricated machine elements.

The Hersey number combines the effects of **lubricant dynamic viscosity (η)**, **sliding/rotational speed (N)**, and **applied load (P)** into a single dimensionless group. It determines the dominant lubrication mechanism.

The four regimes are:
1. **Boundary Lubrication** (H very low) — asperity contact, μ high and constant.
2. **Mixed Lubrication** (low–moderate H) — partial film, μ decreases.
3. **Elastohydrodynamic Lubrication (EHL)** (moderate–high H) — elastic deformation of asperities, μ minimum.
4. **Hydrodynamic / Full-Film Lubrication** (H high) — complete fluid film, μ rises linearly.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Hersey explanation, 1 mark for the four regimes.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

Explain the significance of the **specific film thickness (λ)** and the criterion **λ = 1** in tribological design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **specific film thickness (λ)** is defined as the ratio of minimum lubricant film thickness to the composite surface roughness of the contacting surfaces:

$$ \lambda = \frac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}} $$

**Significance:**
- **λ < 1** → **Boundary lubrication** regime. Surfaces are in direct asperity contact; wear is severe. Boundary additives (EP, AW) are essential.
- **1 ≤ λ < 3** → **Mixed lubrication** regime. Partial film support; moderate wear.
- **λ ≥ 3** → **Full-film lubrication** regime. Surfaces are fully separated; wear is negligible.

The **λ = 1 criterion** is the threshold below which **asperity contact is guaranteed** and boundary lubrication additives must be present to prevent failure.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition with formula, 1 mark for regimes, 1 mark for λ=1 criterion and engineering significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a **neatly labelled diagram**, explain the **Stribeck curve** and describe the four lubrication regimes it represents. Discuss how each regime is characterized in terms of film thickness, friction coefficient, and dominant wear mechanism.

**(b) [7 marks]** A journal bearing has the following specifications:
- Journal radius: r = 40 mm
- Radial clearance: c = 0.08 mm
- Bearing length: L = 80 mm
- Lubricant viscosity: η = 0.04 Pa·s
- Rotational speed: N = 1800 rpm
- Applied load: W = 4000 N
- Surface roughness of journal: R_q1 = 0.5 μm
- Surface roughness of bearing: R_q2 = 0.6 μm

Calculate: (i) The Hersey number, (ii) Petroff's coefficient of friction, (iii) The friction power loss, and (iv) Identify the operating lubrication regime.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The **Stribeck curve** is a U-shaped curve plotted between:
- **Y-axis:** Coefficient of friction (μ)
- **X-axis:** Hersey number H = ηN/P (often logarithmic scale)

**Diagram Description (3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for explanation):**

```
     μ
     |
 0.3 |  *   *                              *
     |     * *                           *     *
 0.2 |      * *                        *         *
     |        * *                    *             *
 0.1 |          * *                *                *
     |            *  *          *                    *
 0.05|              *  * * *  *                       * * *
     |________________________________________________________
        I-Boundary    II-Mixed     III-EHL    IV-Hydrodynamic
       <--μ constant--><--μ↓--><--μ minimum--><--μ↑ linear-->
              H (or λ) →
```

**Four Regimes Explained:**

| Regime | Film Thickness (h vs σ) | Friction (μ) | Wear |
|---|---|---|---|
| I - Boundary | h ≪ σ | High & constant (0.1–0.3) | Severe (adhesive, abrasive) |
| II - Mixed | h ≈ σ | Steeply decreasing | Moderate (fatigue) |
| III - EHL | h > σ, elastic deformation | Minimum (0.02–0.05) | Minimal |
| IV - Hydrodynamic | h ≫ σ | Increasing linearly | Negligible |

**Engineering Conclusion:** The optimal design point is at the **bottom of the U-curve** (Regime III), where friction is minimum and wear is negligible.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Given Data:**
- r = 0.04 m, c = 8 × 10⁻⁵ m, L = 0.08 m
- η = 0.04 Pa·s, N = 1800 rpm = 30 rev/s
- W = 4000 N, R_q1 = 0.5 μm, R_q2 = 0.6 μm

**(i) Hersey Number:**

Projected area: $A = 2 r L = 2 \times 0.04 \times 0.08 = 6.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$

Load per unit projected area: $P = W/A = 4000 / (6.4 \times 10^{-3}) = 6.25 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$

$$ H = \frac{\eta N}{P} = \frac{0.04 \times 30}{6.25 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.2}{6.25 \times 10^5} = 1.92 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} $$

**Answer (i): H = 1.92 × 10⁻⁶ s**

**(ii) Petroff's Coefficient of Friction:**

$$ \mu = \frac{2 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c \cdot W} $$

$$ \mu = \frac{2 \times (9.8696) \times (0.0016) \times 0.08 \times 0.04 \times 30}{(8 \times 10^{-5}) \times 4000} $$

Numerator: $2 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0016 \times 0.08 \times 1.2 = 0.003031$

Denominator: $8 \times 10^{-5} \times 4000 = 0.32$

$$ \mu = \frac{0.003031}{0.32} = 0.00947 $$

**Answer (ii): μ ≈ 0.00947**

**(iii) Friction Power Loss:**

Linear velocity: $V = 2 \pi r N = 2 \pi \times 0.04 \times 30 = 7.540 \text{ m/s}$

Friction force: $F_f = \mu W = 0.00947 \times 4000 = 37.88 \text{ N}$

$$ P_{\text{loss}} = F_f \times V = 37.88 \times 7.540 = 285.6 \text{ W} $$

**Answer (iii): Power loss ≈ 285.6 W**

**(iv) Operating Regime Identification:**

Composite surface roughness:
$$ \sigma = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (0.6)^2} \text{ μm} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.36} = \sqrt{0.61} = 0.781 \text{ μm} $$

Since H = 1.92 × 10⁻⁶ s and μ is very low (~0.009), the bearing is operating in the **Elastohydrodynamic (EHL)** or **near full-film lubrication** regime, close to the bottom of the Stribeck U-curve.

**Answer (iv): Regime III (EHL) — minimum friction region.**

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive **Petroff's equation** for the coefficient of friction in a journal bearing starting from Newton's law of viscosity. Show that Petroff's friction coefficient is **proportional to the Hersey number**, and explain how this relationship maps onto the right-hand (ascending) branch of the Stribeck curve.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the engineering significance of the Stribeck curve in:
1. Bearing design and lubricant selection
2. Energy efficiency of industrial machinery
3. Predictive maintenance and condition monitoring
4. Failure analysis (with one industrial case study, e.g., wind turbine gearbox white etching cracks).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Starting Equation — Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

$$ \tau = \eta \frac{V}{h} $$

**Step 1:** Linear velocity at journal surface:

$$ V = 2 \pi r N $$

**Step 2:** Film thickness approximated by radial clearance:

$$ h \approx c $$

**Step 3:** Substituting into shear stress:

$$ \tau = \eta \cdot \frac{2 \pi r N}{c} $$

**Step 4:** Friction force on journal (shear over projected area $2 \pi r L$):

$$ F_f = \tau \cdot (2 \pi r L) = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c} $$

**Step 5:** Coefficient of friction = friction force / normal load:

$$ \mu = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4 \pi^2 r^2 L \eta N}{c \cdot W} $$

**Step 6:** Recognize that $\frac{\eta N}{P} = \frac{\eta N \cdot 2 r L}{W}$ (since P = W / 2rL):

$$ \mu = \frac{2 \pi^2 (r/c) \cdot \eta N}{P} = 2 \pi^2 \left(\frac{r}{c}\right) \cdot H $$

**Conclusion:** $\mu \propto H$ in the hydrodynamic regime. This is precisely the **right-hand (ascending) branch of the Stribeck curve**, where friction rises linearly (or nearly so) with the Hersey number because the viscous shearing of a thick film generates more resistance.

*(Mark split: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for proportionality, 2 marks for Stribeck curve mapping.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

1. **Bearing Design and Lubricant Selection (2 marks):**
The Stribeck curve allows engineers to select lubricant viscosity and operating speed to position the bearing at the **minimum friction point (Regime III)**. By matching ISO VG grade to expected load and speed, designers avoid both boundary wear and excessive viscous drag.

2. **Energy Efficiency (2 marks):**
Hydrodynamic operation (Regime IV) is friction-costly because μ rises with H. EHL operation (Regime III) is the energy-efficient sweet spot. Industrial fans, pumps, and turbines save **5–15% energy** by operating at the Stribeck minimum.

3. **Predictive Maintenance (1.5 marks):**
A shift from Regime IV → III → II → I in operating data (e.g., friction torque, vibration spectra) signals lubricant degradation, starvation, or overload. This enables **condition-based maintenance** before catastrophic failure.

4. **Case Study — Wind Turbine Gearbox WEC (1.5 marks):**
White Etch Cracks (WEC) and White Structure Flaking (WSF) in wind turbine rolling bearings occur when the bearing operates in **mixed/boundary lubrication** during low-wind idling (low Hersey number). Insufficient EHL film allows subsurface fatigue cracks. Redesign with higher λ-ratio (e.g., 2.5+) via lower-viscosity base oils and improved surface finish mitigates WEC.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Stribeck Curve Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s (divide by 60) or mm → m. This single error costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Hersey and Sommerfeld Numbers:** Petroff's equation contains **r/c** (clearance ratio), which makes it a Sommerfeld-style expression. To get a *pure* Hersey relationship, students must isolate the (ηN/P) term explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the Logarithmic X-axis:** The Stribeck curve is typically plotted with **log(H)** on the x-axis. A linear plot will appear distorted and will be marked down.
> 4. **Skipping Regime Identification:** After computing μ, students must explicitly state which regime the bearing operates in and justify using the λ-ratio or H value. Examiners award marks for this analytical step.
> 5. **Mixing Up λ and h:** λ is dimensionless (h/σ), while h is in meters. Do not write "λ = 0.4 mm" — this is an instant half-mark deduction.
> 6. **Petroff's Limitations:** Petroff's equation assumes **no load support** by the film, **no pressure-viscosity effect**, and **no asperity contact**. Stating these limitations earns the 2 "depth" marks at the end of any derivation question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Stribeck Curve = μ vs Hersey Number (ηN/P)**, plotted on log scale.
- **Hersey Number** combines viscosity, speed, and load into one dimensionless group.
- **Four Regimes (left to right):** Boundary → Mixed → EHL → Hydrodynamic.
- **Boundary (I):** h ≪ σ, μ high and constant (~0.1–0.3), severe wear, additive-dependent.
- **Mixed (II):** h ≈ σ, μ decreases steeply, moderate wear, anti-wear additives help.
- **EHL (III):** h > σ with elastic deformation, μ minimum (~0.02), design target.
- **Hydrodynamic (IV):** h ≫ σ, μ rises linearly with H (Petroff: μ = 2π²(r/c)·H), negligible wear.
- **Specific Film Thickness λ = h_min / σ** is the practical engineering metric.
- **λ < 1** → boundary (asperity contact); **1 ≤ λ < 3** → mixed; **λ ≥ 3** → full film.
- **Petroff's equation** gives the right-hand (ascending) branch of the Stribeck curve.
- **Hamrock-Dowson equation** gives h_min for EHL contacts: $h_{\min} = 2.65 R_x U^{0.7} G^{0.54} W^{-0.13}$.
- **Sommerfeld Number S = (r/c)·(ηN/P)** is a journal-bearing-specific variant of the Hersey number.
- **Engineering Goals:** (a) Operate at Stribeck minimum, (b) Use additives in boundary regime, (c) Monitor friction for predictive maintenance, (d) Avoid WEC/WEC in wind turbine gearboxes.
- **Famous Use-Case:** Wind turbine, IC engine bearings, jet engine mainshafts, rolling mill bearings.
- **Energy Insight:** Friction losses = ~20% of global industrial energy → operating at Stribeck minimum is critical for sustainability.
- **Exam Vocabulary to Memorize:** Hersey number, Sommerfeld number, lambda ratio, asperity contact, boundary film, EHL, piezo-viscosity, full-film lubrication, entrainment velocity, specific film thickness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lubricant Properties and Classification: Physical and Chemical Properties of Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lubricant Properties and Classification: Physical and Chemical Properties of Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Lubricant

A **lubricant** is any substance (solid, semi-solid, liquid, or gaseous) interposed between two moving surfaces in relative motion to **reduce friction, dissipate heat, prevent wear, and protect surfaces** from corrosion and contamination. In engineering tribology, the selection of a lubricant is governed not only by its ability to form a hydrodynamic or boundary film but also by a strict set of **physical and chemical properties** that determine its behavior under operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> A lubricant is a material capable of reducing friction between two sliding/rolling surfaces and is characterized by a defined set of physical (viscosity, flash point, pour point, density, VI) and chemical (TAN, TBN, carbon residue, ash content) properties as specified by ASTM, BIS, and ISO standards.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a lubricant as the **invisible cushion or "smoothing oil"** you apply on a creaking door hinge. If you have ever tried pushing a heavy wooden chest across a rough floor, you feel a huge resistance. Now imagine the same chest on roller-skates gliding over polished marble — the floor has, in a sense, been "lubricated." In machines, this cushioning effect is quantified by **viscosity** (how "thick" the lubricant is), and its ability to stay liquid in cold and not evaporate in heat is given by **pour point** and **flash point**.

### 1.3 Why These Properties Matter

A machine may operate from **–40 °C in a Himalayan turbine** to **+250 °C in an aero-engine bearing**. A single failure of the lubricant — say, it freezes in winter or oxidizes in summer — can cause catastrophic failure. Hence, the physical and chemical properties of lubricants are the **gatekeepers of machine life**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):**
> The student must be able to:
> 1. Identify and define the key physical properties (viscosity, VI, flash point, fire point, pour point, cloud point, density, specific gravity).
> 2. Identify and define the key chemical properties (TAN, TBN, saponification number, carbon residue, ash content, emulsification, demulsibility).
> 3. Classify lubricants into solid, semi-solid, liquid, and gaseous categories with examples.

### 1.4 Broad Classification of Lubricants

Lubricants are broadly classified into four physical states:

| State | Examples | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Liquid Lubricants** | Mineral oils, synthetic oils (PAO, ester, silicone), vegetable oils | Engines, gearboxes, turbines |
| **Semi-Solid Lubricants** | Greases (lithium, calcium, sodium, polyurea based) | Wheel bearings, chassis points |
| **Solid Lubricants** | Graphite, MoS₂, PTFE, boron nitride, mica | High-temp, vacuum, space, dry machining |
| **Gaseous Lubricants** | Air, nitrogen, helium | Gas bearings, high-speed spindles, turbomachinery |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU 2024 examiners often start Part A questions with "Define flash point" or "Differentiate between flash and fire point." Memorize definitions *word-for-word* in 2-3 lines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Properties of Lubricants — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Viscosity (η or μ)
The **single most important property** in tribology. Defined by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $du/dy$ = velocity gradient (rate of shear) (s⁻¹)

**Kinematic viscosity** (ν) is viscosity divided by density:

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

Units: **m²/s** in SI, **Stokes (St)** or **centistokes (cSt)** in CGS (1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s).

**Why it matters:** A lubricant that is too thin (low viscosity) collapses under load and causes metal-to-metal contact. Too viscous and it cannot flow into the contact zone, causing starvation and power loss.

#### 2.1.2 Viscosity Index (VI)
A dimensionless number (developed by Dean & Davis, 1929) that indicates **how much a lubricant's viscosity changes with temperature**. Higher VI ⇒ less change with temperature ⇒ better lubricant.

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Where:
- $U$ = viscosity of test oil at 40 °C
- $L$ = viscosity of oil with VI = 0 (poor reference) at 40 °C having same viscosity at 100 °C as test oil
- $H$ = viscosity of oil with VI = 100 (good reference) at 40 °C

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-grade engine oils** (e.g., SAE 20W-50) use **VI improvers (polymers like PMA, OCP)** to flatten the viscosity-temperature curve.

#### 2.1.3 Flash Point
The **lowest temperature** at which lubricant vapors ignite momentarily when exposed to a flame. It defines the **upper flammability limit** for storage and transport.

#### 2.1.4 Fire Point
The temperature at which the **vapors sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds**. Always **5–10 °C higher** than flash point.

#### 2.1.5 Pour Point
The **lowest temperature** at which the lubricant will just flow under standardized conditions (ASTM D97). Critical for cold-start lubrication in engines.

#### 2.1.6 Cloud Point
The temperature at which dissolved waxes in mineral oil begin to **crystallize**, making the oil appear cloudy. Always **higher** than pour point.

#### 2.1.7 Density and Specific Gravity
Density ($\rho$) is mass per unit volume (kg/m³). Specific gravity is the ratio of oil density to water density at 15.6 °C. Affects **oil delivery rate** in splash lubrication systems.

#### 2.1.8 Emulsification & Demulsibility
- **Emulsification:** Ability of oil to form a stable emulsion with water (desirable in cutting fluids, undesirable in steam turbine oils).
- **Demulsibility:** Ability to separate from water (turbine oils must demulsify quickly to prevent rust).

### 2.2 Chemical Properties of Lubricants

#### 2.2.1 Total Acid Number (TAN)
The number of **milligrams of KOH** required to neutralize the acidic constituents in **1 gram** of oil. Indicates **oxidation degradation**.

$$\text{TAN} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W}$$

Where $V$ = volume of KOH (mL), $N$ = normality, $W$ = weight of oil (g).

#### 2.2.2 Total Base Number (TBN)
The number of **mg KOH** equivalent to the basic constituents in 1 g of oil. Indicates **reserve alkalinity** to neutralize combustion acids. Critical for **engine oils** (diesel engine oils: TBN 8–14).

#### 2.2.3 Saponification Number
Mg of KOH required to **saponify the fats/oils** in 1 g of lubricant. High in vegetable oils (used in cutting fluids), low in mineral oils.

#### 2.2.4 Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom)
Percentage of **carbonaceous deposit** left after evaporation and pyrolysis. Indicates tendency to form **coke** in bearings and cylinders.

#### 2.2.5 Ash Content
Incombustible residue (Ca, Mg, Zn, P compounds from additives). High ash ⇒ unsuitable for **detergent-free** applications; controlled ash is vital in **low-SAPS** engine oils.

#### 2.2.6 Neutralization Number
General term covering TAN and TBN.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Formula / Definition | Units | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | Pa·s | Film thickness |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s (cSt) | Reynolds number |
| Viscosity Index | $\text{VI} = (L-U)/(L-H) \times 100$ | dimensionless | Temp. stability |
| Redwood No.1 | $\nu = At - B/t$ | cSt (A, B constants) | Lab measurement |
| Saybolt Universal | $\nu = 0.226t - 195/t$ | cSt | Lab measurement |
| Engler | $\nu = 0.00135°E - 0.00029/°E$ | cSt | Lab measurement |
| TAN | $(V \times N \times 56.1)/W$ | mg KOH/g | Oxidation |
| TBN | Same as TAN, base titration | mg KOH/g | Acidity reserve |
| Saponification No. | $(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1/W$ | mg KOH/g | Fat content |
| Carbon Residue | $(W_{residue}/W_{sample}) \times 100$ | % (w/w) | Coking tendency |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Note on Redwood Viscometer Equations:**
> - For Redwood No. 1: $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72/t$ (in cSt, t in seconds, t > 100 s)
> - For Redwood No. 2: $\nu = 0.026 t - 0.58/t$ (in cSt, when t < 100 s)
> - Saybolt Universal: $\nu = 0.226 t - 195/t$ (cSt, t in seconds)
> - Engler degrees: $\nu = 0.00135 °E - 0.00029/°E$
> 
> **Memorize only Redwood No. 1 and Saybolt Universal** for KTU exams.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Viscosity & VI** → Bearing selection (calculate $\eta N / P$ for hydrodynamic regime).
- **Flash & Fire Point** → Hazard classification for storage in petroleum refineries.
- **Pour & Cloud Point** → Cold-start SAE grade selection (e.g., 5W-30, 0W-20).
- **TAN / TBN** → Predictive maintenance: oil change is triggered when TAN rises above threshold.
- **Carbon Residue** → Quality of bright stock for industrial gear oils.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity (Conceptual)

Consider a fluid layer between two parallel plates. The bottom plate is stationary; the top plate moves with velocity $U$ under force $F$. Each fluid layer drags the layer beneath it.

- The shear stress $\tau$ is proportional to velocity gradient: $\tau \propto du/dy$.
- Introducing the proportionality constant $\mu$ (dynamic viscosity): $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$.

For a linear velocity profile (laminar Couette flow):

$$u(y) = U \cdot \frac{y}{h} \implies \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U}{h}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity from Dynamic Viscosity

Given:
- Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ has units of N·s/m² = kg/(m·s).
- Density $\rho$ has units of kg/m³.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \implies \text{units} = \frac{\text{kg/(m·s)}}{\text{kg/m}^3} = \text{m}^2/\text{s} \;\; \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity Index (ASTM D2270 Method)

**Step 1:** Measure kinematic viscosity of test oil at 40 °C and 100 °C. Let $U$ = viscosity at 40 °C.

**Step 2:** Look up ASTM D2270 tables (or compute from reference equations) to find the **L and H** values — viscosities of reference oils with VI = 0 and VI = 100 respectively that have the **same viscosity at 100 °C** as the test oil.

**Step 3:** Apply the equation:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A test oil has $\nu_{40} = 73$ cSt and $\nu_{100} = 9$ cSt. Reference tables give $L = 120$ cSt and $H = 70$ cSt for a 9 cSt oil at 100 °C.

$$\text{VI} = \frac{120 - 73}{120 - 70} \times 100 = \frac{47}{50} \times 100 = 94$$

A VI of 94 indicates **excellent viscosity-temperature stability** (typical of a high-quality mineral oil with some VI improver).

### 3.4 Worked Derivation: TAN Calculation

**Problem:** 10 g of used engine oil is titrated against 0.1 N KOH. 4.5 mL of KOH is consumed to reach the endpoint. Calculate TAN.

**Solution:**

$$\text{TAN} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{4.5 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{10}$$

$$\text{TAN} = \frac{25.245}{10} = 2.52 \;\; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

**Interpretation:** TAN of 2.52 indicates the oil is **moderately oxidized** and approaching its condemnation limit (typically TAN > 2.0–3.0 triggers oil change in diesel engines).

### 3.5 Worked Derivation: Saponification Number

**Problem:** 2 g of vegetable oil is saponified with 25 mL of 0.5 N KOH. The excess KOH is back-titrated with 0.5 N HCl, consuming 10 mL. Calculate saponification number.

**Step 1:** Volume of KOH consumed by oil = $25 - 10 = 15$ mL.

**Step 2:**

$$\text{SN} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{15 \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2}$$

$$\text{SN} = \frac{420.75}{2} = 210.4 \;\; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

**Interpretation:** High SN (200+) confirms the oil is rich in **fatty acid esters**, consistent with a vegetable-based cutting fluid.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Property

| Operating Condition | Critical Property | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Arctic / Cold Start | **Pour Point, Cloud Point** | Wax crystallization chokes flow |
| High-Speed Bearings | **Viscosity @ 40 °C** | Reynolds number demands thin oil |
| Steam Turbine | **Demulsibility, TAN** | Water contamination; oxidation |
| Aero Engine | **Flash Point, Fire Point, Thermal Stability** | High bulk oil temperatures (200 °C+) |
| Marine Diesel | **TBN, Carbon Residue** | Neutralize H₂SO₄ from S-fuel |
| Food Industry | **Biodegradability, Toxicity** | NSF H1, H2 certification |
| Vacuum / Space | **Vapor Pressure** | Solid lubricants preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lubricant Classification — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Lubricants] --> B[Liquid Lubricants]
    A --> C[Semi-Solid Lubricants]
    A --> D[Solid Lubricants]
    A --> E[Gaseous Lubricants]

    B --> B1[Mineral Oils]
    B --> B2[Synthetic Oils]
    B --> B3[Vegetable Oils]
    B1 --> B1a[Paraffinic Base]
    B1 --> B1b[Naphthenic Base]
    B1 --> B1c[Aromatic Base]
    B2 --> B2a[PAO Polyalphaolefin]
    B2 --> B2b[Synthetic Esters]
    B2 --> B2c[Silicone Oils]
    B2 --> B2d[PFPE Fluorocarbons]

    C --> C1[Lithium Grease]
    C --> C2[Calcium Grease]
    C --> C3[Sodium Grease]
    C --> C4[Polyurea Grease]
    C --> C5[Clay Bentone Grease]

    D --> D1[Layered Lattice]
    D --> D2[Non Layered]
    D1 --> D1a[Graphite]
    D1 --> D1b[Molybdenum Disulfide MoS2]
    D1 --> D1c[Hexagonal Boron Nitride hBN]
    D2 --> D2a[PTFE Teflon]
    D2 --> D2b[Lead Oxide]
    D2 --> D2c[Mica]

    E --> E1[Air Bearings]
    E --> E2[Helium Bearings]
    E --> E3[Nitrogen Foil Bearings]
```

### 4.2 Property-Based Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Select Lubricant] --> Q1{Operating Temperature?}
    Q1 -->|Below -20 deg C| Q2{Static or Dynamic?}
    Q1 -->|Between -20 and 150 deg C| Q3{Load Type?}
    Q1 -->|Above 150 deg C| Solid[Solid Lubricant: MoS2 or Graphite]

    Q2 -->|Static| Q2a[Synthetic Oil Low Pour Point]
    Q2 -->|Dynamic| Q2b[Synthetic Ester or Silicone]

    Q3 --> Q3a{High Speed Low Load}
    Q3 --> Q3b{Low Speed High Load}
    Q3a --> VisLow[Low Viscosity Oil Viscosity Index Above 90]
    Q3b --> VisHigh[High Viscosity Oil or Grease EP Additives]

    Solid --> End[Application Validated]
    VisLow --> End
    VisHigh --> End
    Q2a --> End
    Q2b --> End
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Viscosity Index Concept

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Reference Oil VI equals 0] -->|Highly viscous drop with temperature| B[Low VI 50 cSt at 100 deg C]
    C[Reference Oil VI equals 100] -->|Minimal drop with temperature| D[High VI 70 cSt at 100 deg C]
    E[Test Oil Same viscosity at 100 deg C] --> F[VI Calculation: position between L and H]
```

### 4.4 Physical-Chemical Property Mapping

```mermaid
graph TD
    L[Lubricant Quality] --> P1[Physical Properties]
    L --> P2[Chemical Properties]
    P1 --> P1a[Viscosity]
    P1 --> P1b[VI]
    P1 --> P1c[Flash Fire Point]
    P1 --> P1d[Pour Cloud Point]
    P1 --> P1e[Density]
    P2 --> P2a[TAN]
    P2 --> P2b[TBN]
    P2 --> P2c[Saponification]
    P2 --> P2d[Carbon Residue]
    P2 --> P2e[Ash Content]
    P2 --> P2f[Emulsion Stability]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]**
**Define the following: (a) Viscosity, (b) Flash Point, (c) Cloud Point.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Viscosity:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid by which it **resists the relative motion** of its layers. Quantitatively, it is defined as the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain, per **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$. The SI unit is **Pa·s**, and the CGS unit is **Poise** (1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s). In lubrication engineering, **kinematic viscosity** ($\nu = \mu/\rho$, in cSt) is more commonly used.

**(b) Flash Point:** Flash point is the **lowest temperature** at which the vapors of a lubricant, when heated under standardized conditions (open or closed cup), momentarily ignite (a "flash") when an external flame is brought near the surface. It indicates the **fire hazard** during storage and handling. Common test methods are **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** and **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)**.

**(c) Cloud Point:** Cloud point is the temperature at which dissolved **waxes and paraffins** in a mineral oil begin to **crystallize** upon cooling, making the oil appear hazy or cloudy. It is a critical low-temperature property, especially for oils used in **cold climates**. Cloud point is always **higher** than pour point.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]**
**Differentiate between TAN and TBN. Why are they important for engine oils?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | TAN (Total Acid Number) | TBN (Total Base Number) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | mg KOH required to neutralize **acidic** components in 1 g of oil | mg KOH equivalent of **basic** components in 1 g of oil |
| **Significance** | Indicates **oxidation** and degradation by-products | Indicates **reserve alkalinity** to neutralize acids |
| **Trend with use** | **Increases** as oil ages | **Decreases** as it neutralizes acids |
| **Critical for** | All lubricating oils | Engine oils (esp. diesel with S-fuel) |
| **Condemnation limit** | > 2.0–3.0 mg KOH/g (varies) | < 1.0–2.0 mg KOH/g |

**Importance for engine oils:** During combustion, **sulfur in diesel fuel** forms **sulfuric acid** (H₂SO₄), and blow-by gases contain **nitric acid** and **organic acids**. These acids corrode bearings, pistons, and liners. **TBN additives** (Ca/Mg carbonates, overbased sulfonates) chemically neutralize these acids, protecting the engine. As TBN depletes, acid attack begins — hence oil change intervals are tied to **TBN depletion curves**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **physical properties** of lubricants used in industrial applications. (7 marks)

**(b)** A test oil has kinematic viscosity of **85 cSt at 40 °C** and **10 cSt at 100 °C**. The reference oils with the same viscosity at 100 °C have $L = 130$ cSt and $H = 75$ cSt. Calculate the **Viscosity Index** and comment on the quality of the oil. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Properties (7 marks)**

1. **Viscosity:** The internal resistance of a fluid to flow. Most critical property — determines film thickness, load-carrying capacity, and friction torque. Measured by Redwood, Saybolt, Engler, or kinematic viscometers. Expressed in **cSt** at **40 °C and 100 °C** (ISO VG grades).

2. **Viscosity Index (VI):** Measures the rate of change of viscosity with temperature. Higher VI = better. Multi-grade oils achieve VI > 150 with **polymeric VI improvers (PMA, OCP)**.

3. **Flash Point and Fire Point:** Flash point = temperature of momentary ignition; Fire point = sustained combustion. Determines **fire safety** classification. Mineral oils: 150–250 °C; synthetics: 250–300 °C.

4. **Pour Point and Cloud Point:** Pour point = lowest flow temperature; Cloud point = onset of wax crystallization. Critical for cold regions. Pour depressants (PMA copolymers) are used to lower pour point.

5. **Density and Specific Gravity:** Used to compute **kinematic viscosity** from dynamic viscosity, and in **splash lubrication** design (oil pump capacity).

6. **Emulsification and Demulsibility:** Emulsification = ability to form oil-water emulsion (good for metalworking fluids). Demulsibility = water separation tendency (critical for turbine oils to prevent rust).

7. **Specific Heat and Thermal Conductivity:** Affect heat dissipation from bearings. Higher values ⇒ better cooling.

**(b) Solution (7 marks)**

Given:
- $\nu_{40} = U = 85$ cSt
- $\nu_{100} = 10$ cSt
- $L = 130$ cSt, $H = 75$ cSt

Applying the VI formula:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 = \frac{130 - 85}{130 - 75} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{45}{55} \times 100 = 81.82 \approx 82$$

**Comment:** A VI of 82 indicates a **good paraffinic base oil with mild VI improver** — suitable for general industrial gear oils and hydraulic fluids. For premium multi-grade engine oils, VI > 100 is desired.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **chemical properties** of lubricants and explain how they help in **condition monitoring** of machines. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sample of used engine oil weighing **5 g** is titrated with **0.1 N KOH** solution. The volume of KOH consumed is **6.2 mL**. Calculate the **TAN** of the oil and state whether the oil should be drained. (Given: condemnation TAN limit = 2.5 mg KOH/g.) (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Chemical Properties and Condition Monitoring (7 marks)**

1. **Total Acid Number (TAN):** Indicates oxidation. Acidic by-products (carboxylic acids) attack metal. Trending TAN values over time predict **oil change intervals**. Sudden rise indicates contamination or overheating.

2. **Total Base Number (TBN):** Indicates reserve alkalinity. Falling TBN signals depletion of detergent additives. Critical for **diesel engine oils** to monitor sulfur-fuel-derived acid neutralization.

3. **Saponification Number:** Detects presence of **animal/vegetable fats** (cutting fluids, rust preventives). High SN → biodegradable but prone to oxidation.

4. **Carbon Residue (Conradson / Ramsbottom):** Predicts **coke formation** in high-temperature zones (piston rings, gear teeth). High residue → poor thermal stability.

5. **Ash Content:** Sulphated ash from metallic additives (ZnDDP, Ca sulfonate). Excessive ash → **piston deposits, after-treatment clogging** in modern BS-VI / Euro-VI engines. Low-SAPS oils (SAPS < 0.8%) are now mandated.

6. **Water Content (Karl Fischer):** Traces water → hydrolysis, rust, microbial growth. Limits: < 0.1% for turbine oils.

7. **Particle Count and Spectrographic Analysis (Ferrous Wear):** Indirect chemical/methodological indicators. Fe, Cu, Cr levels indicate wear rates.

**Condition Monitoring Workflow:** Sample oil → measure TAN, TBN, viscosity, particle count, spectrographic metals → trend analysis → schedule oil change *before* damage occurs.

**(b) TAN Calculation (7 marks)**

Given:
- $W = 5$ g
- $V = 6.2$ mL
- $N = 0.1$ N

$$\text{TAN} = \frac{V \times N \times 56.1}{W} = \frac{6.2 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{5}$$

$$= \frac{34.782}{5} = 6.96 \;\; \text{mg KOH/g}$$

**Conclusion:** Since TAN = 6.96 mg KOH/g, which is **well above** the condemnation limit of 2.5 mg KOH/g, the oil is **severely oxidized** and **must be drained immediately**. Continued use would lead to corrosion of bearings, piston ring scuffing, and sludge formation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Units Confusion:** Students often write viscosity as "St" when the SI unit is "m²/s" or "Pa·s." KTU examiners want **cSt** (centistokes) for kinematic viscosity. Always write units explicitly.
> 2. **Flash vs. Fire Point:** Writing "fire point is the temperature at which oil catches fire" is **incomplete** — it must "sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds."
> 3. **TAN Formula Errors:** Forgetting the factor 56.1 (molecular weight of KOH) is the most common error. Memorize it.
> 4. **VI Formula Sign:** VI = (L−U)/(L−H). Students often invert the numerator as (U−L), yielding negative values. **Remember: L > U > H always.**
> 5. **Confusing Cloud Point and Pour Point:** Cloud point is *higher* (wax first appears), pour point is *lower* (oil just flows). Reversing them costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping "Comment" in numerical answers:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates *interpretation* of every numerical. A bare number with no "this indicates good/poor quality" loses 1–2 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Viscosity is KING** in tribology — every other property supports it.
- **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ is the foundation.
- **Kinematic vs. Dynamic:** $\nu = \mu/\rho$; dynamic in Pa·s, kinematic in cSt.
- **VI Equation:** $\text{VI} = (L-U)/(L-H) \times 100$ — for VI < 100 only; for VI ≥ 100, ASTM D2270 uses different equations.
- **Flash Point < Fire Point** by 5–10 °C, always.
- **Cloud Point > Pour Point** by 3–10 °C, always.
- **TAN formula** must use factor **56.1** (KOH molecular weight).
- **TBN > TAN** = healthy engine oil; **TAN > TBN** = oil is exhausted and acidic.
- **Synthetic oils** > mineral oils in: VI, flash point, low-temperature fluidity, oxidation stability.
- **Grease** = base oil (70–90%) + thickener (soap) + additives.
- **Solid lubricants** are irreplaceable in **vacuum, space, and ultra-high-temperature** applications where liquids decompose.
- **Modern low-SAPS engine oils** (Caterpillar ECF-3, API CK-4) have ash < 1.0% to protect DPFs.
- For **KTU 2024 ESE**, expect: 1 direct definition (3M), 1 short note (3M), and one 14M question combining theory + numerical.
- **Practice SI unit conversions:** 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s, 1 Poise = 0.1 Pa·s, 1 Stoke = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU 2024:**
> **"VaFaPoCl"** = **V**iscosity, Flash, Fire, **P**our, **Cl**oud point (Physical properties).
> **"TANCSA"** = **T**AN, **T**BN (N/B), **C**arbon residue, **S**aponification, **A**sh (Chemical properties).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Lubricants: Oils, Greases, and Solid Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Lubricants: Oils, Greases, and Solid Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Tribology (PEMET633)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a **lubricant** is broadly defined as any substance (gas, liquid, semi-solid, or solid) introduced between two contacting surfaces in relative motion to **reduce friction**, **dissipate heat**, **minimize wear**, and **prevent corrosion**, thereby enhancing the efficiency, reliability, and operational life of machinery.

Lubricants are functionally classified into **four primary categories** based on their physical state:

1. **Lubricating Oils** — Newtonian fluids (mineral, synthetic, vegetable).
2. **Greases** — Non-Newtonian semi-solid lubricants (oil + thickener).
3. **Solid Lubricants** — Lamellar or low-shear-strength solids (e.g., graphite, MoS₂).
4. **Gases** — Air, helium (used in gas bearings).

For Module 3, the focus is restricted to **Oils, Greases, and Solid Lubricants**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must clearly distinguish between the **physical state**, **additive chemistry**, **performance testing standards**, and **field applications** of each lubricant category. Examiner questions frequently test the property-vs-application linkage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy **almirah (wardrobe)** across a rough cemented floor. The metal-on-cement contact produces harsh grinding, scratching, and enormous effort is required. Now place **four rolling cylindrical logs** under the almirah — it glides effortlessly. The logs converted **sliding friction** into **rolling friction**.

A lubricant does *exactly* this at the microscopic scale:
- **Oils** act like the logs — they interpose a fluid film (like ball-bearings made of liquid) that **completely separates** the two metal asperities (boundary → mixed → hydrodynamic regimes).
- **Greases** are like **sticky jam** smeared on the logs — they stay where they are placed (good for open gears, slow bearings) and won't drip off.
- **Solid lubricants** are like a **layer of graphite powder on a slippery slide** — they form a low-shear-strength film that shears easily while protecting the parent surfaces.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Pa·s (or cP) | Internal resistance to flow |
| Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | mm²/s (or cSt) | Flow under gravity |
| Viscosity Index (VI) | Dimensionless | Rate of viscosity change with temperature |
| Flash Point | °C | Lowest temp. at which vapours ignite (open flame test) |
| Fire Point | °C | Temp. at which vapour sustains combustion ≥ 5 s |
| Pour Point | °C | Lowest temp. at which oil still flows |
| Cloud Point | °C | Temp. at which wax crystals first appear |
| Neutralisation Number | mg KOH/g | Measure of acidity/alkalinity |
| NLGI Grade | 000 to 6 | Grease consistency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Constant:** $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 1 \text{ mPa·s}$ and $1 \text{ cSt} = 1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Lubricating Oils — Deep Analysis

Lubricating oils form the **backbone of industrial lubrication**. They are sub-classified by origin:

#### A. Mineral Oils (Petroleum-derived)
- **Paraffinic base oils** — Wax-rich, excellent VI (95–105), good low-temp. fluidity.
- **Naphthenic base oils** — Low wax, low pour point, but lower VI (30–60).
- **Aromatic base oils** — Poor oxidation stability; used mainly as process oils.

#### B. Synthetic Oils (Engineered molecules)
- **Polyalphaolefins (PAO)** — Excellent VI and thermal stability.
- **Diesters / Polyol Esters** — High-temperature stability (jet engines).
- **Phosphate Esters** — Fire-resistant hydraulic fluids.
- **Polyglycols (PAG)** — Water-soluble, used in worm gears.
- **Silicones** — Wide temperature range (-60°C to 200°C).
- **Fluorocarbons (PFPE)** — Used in oxygen / vacuum environments.

#### C. Vegetable / Bio-based Oils
- **Castor, Rapeseed, Soybean oils** — Renewable, biodegradable, but poor oxidation stability.

### 2.2 Key Properties of Lubricating Oils (Theory + Function)

| Property | What it Measures | Engineering Function | Standard Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity** | Flow resistance | Determines film thickness ($h \propto \mu \cdot N / P$) | ASTM D445 |
| **Viscosity Index (VI)** | Viscosity-temperature slope | Predicts behavior across operating temp. | ASTM D2270 |
| **Flash Point** | Volatility/fire safety | Storage and transport safety | ASTM D92 |
| **Pour Point** | Low-temp. pumpability | Cold-start reliability | ASTM D97 |
| **Neutralisation No.** | Acidity from oxidation | Corrosion potential | ASTM D974 |
| **Aniline Point** | Aromaticity of base oil | Compatibility with rubber seals | ASTM D611 |
| **Emulsion Stability** | Water-separation ability | Steam-turbine oils | ASTM D1401 |
| **Carbon Residue** | Coking tendency | Diesel/compressor oils | ASTM D189 |

### 2.3 Greases — Deep Analysis

A **grease** is a **semi-fluid-to-solid dispersion** of a **thickener** in a **base oil**, often supplemented with **additives**.

#### Structural Anatomy of Grease
- **Base Oil (70–95 %)** — Mineral or synthetic (the actual lubricant).
- **Thickener (5–25 %)** — Forms a 3-D soap fiber network:
  - *Simple soap*: Li, Na, Ca, Ba, Al stearates.
  - *Complex soap*: Li-complex, Ca-complex, Al-complex (higher drop points).
  - *Non-soap*: Organo-clays (Bentone), polyurea, silica.
- **Additives (0–10 %)** — Anti-oxidants, anti-wear, extreme-pressure, rust inhibitors.

#### Critical Grease Properties
- **Drop Point** — Temperature at which grease liquefies (ASTM D566).
- **Consistency (NLGI Grade)** — Penetration depth of a standard cone in 1/10 mm.
- **Mechanical Stability** — Ability to retain consistency after prolonged shear (worked penetration).
- **Oil Bleed** — Tendency of oil to separate from thickener on storage.

### 2.4 Solid Lubricants — Deep Analysis

Solid lubricants are used where **liquid/gaseous lubricants fail**: extreme temperatures, vacuum, high loads, or aggressive chemicals.

#### Lamellar (Layer-lattice) Solids
- **Graphite** — Carbon in hexagonal layers; works *only* in presence of adsorbed water vapour (≈ 5 % by mass).
- **Molybdenum Disulphide (MoS₂)** — Sulfur-molybdenum planes; works in **vacuum** (unlike graphite).
- **Tungsten Disulphide (WS₂)** — Higher temperature ceiling than MoS₂ (up to 1300 °C).
- **Hexagonal Boron Nitride (hBN)** — "White graphite"; electrically insulating.

#### Non-Lamellar Solid Lubricants
- **PTFE (Teflon)** — Long-chain fluorocarbon; low friction (~0.04).
- **Soft Metals** — Pb, Sn, In, Au, Ag films (used in space mechanisms).
- **Polymer-based coatings** — PEEK, polyimide.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula cheat sheet**. Memorize the symbols and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity = Dynamic viscosity / Density | m²/s |
| 2 | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | Viscosity Index (Dean & Davis method); $L, U, H$ from ASTM D2270 tables | — |
| 3 | $h_{\min} = \dfrac{3.65 \mu U}{W/L} \cdot R$ | Minimum film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson, ball-on-flat) | m |
| 4 | $\Lambda = h_{\min} / \sigma$ | Lambda ratio (film thickness / composite roughness); $\Lambda < 1$ boundary, $> 3$ full film | — |
| 5 | $T_{fire} > T_{flash} + 20\,°C$ | Fire point is always ≥ 20 °C above flash point | °C |
| 6 | $W_{work} = \pi \cdot d^2 \cdot n \cdot t / 4$ | Worked penetration (NLGI worked value); $d$=cone penetration depth | mm⁻¹ × strokes |
| 7 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian shear stress (valid for oils at low shear) | Pa |
| 8 | $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law (greases are shear-thinning: $n<1$) | Pa |
| 9 | $TBN = \dfrac{(V_s - V_b) \cdot N_{acid}}{m}$ | Total Base Number; $V_s$=sample titre, $V_b$=blank, $N_{acid}$=normality, $m$=mass | mg KOH/g |
| 10 | $AN = \dfrac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{m}$ | Acid Number; 56.1 = MW of KOH | mg KOH/g |
| 11 | $\mu_{T} = \mu_{40} \cdot 10^{(40-T)/C}$ | Walther equation (ASTM D341); $C$=Walther constant | cP |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** — PFPE and MoS₂ for vacuum-exposed satellite gears.
- **Steel mills** — Heavy EP greases with MoS₂ additives for roll-neck bearings.
- **Food industry** — USDA H1 food-grade oils (white mineral oil + PAO).
- **Marine** — Highly viscous paraffinic cylinder oils with high TBN for acid neutralization.
- **Electric vehicles (EVs)** — Low-viscosity PAO + MoDTC additive for gearbox efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Lubricant Selection Decision Flow

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as a structured decision-making aid when answering KTU selection-type problems.

| Operating Condition | Recommended Lubricant Type | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| High speed, moderate load, sealed bearing | Mineral / synthetic **oil** | Easy circulation, heat removal |
| Open gear, slow speed, dusty environment | **Grease** (NLGI 1–2) | Stays in place, seals out dust |
| Vacuum / outer-space mechanism | **MoS₂ or WS₂ solid** | No outgassing, no liquid evaporation |
| High temperature furnace chain | **Graphite bonded coating** | Stable > 400 °C |
| Food contact machinery | **White mineral oil / H1 grease** | Non-toxic, FDA-approved |
| High load gear (spur/helical) | **EP oil with S-P additive** | Prevents scuffing under boundary lubrication |
| Cryogenic pump bearing | **PFPE / PTFE grease** | Remains fluid at -200 °C |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Viscosity Index (VI) by the Dean–Davis Method

The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical, dimensionless number that quantifies the **rate of change of viscosity with temperature**, referenced against two benchmark oils (a high-VI paraffinic L-oil and a low-VI naphthenic H-oil), both having the same viscosity at 100 °C.

#### Given (typical KTU-style problem):
- Kinematic viscosity of oil at **40 °C**: $\nu_{40} = 95 \text{ cSt}$.
- Kinematic viscosity of oil at **100 °C**: $\nu_{100} = 10 \text{ cSt}$.

**Step 1 — Determine $U$, $L$, and $H$ from the ASTM D2270 tables.**
For $\nu_{100} = 10 \text{ cSt}$:
- $U \approx 10 \text{ cSt}$ (kinematic viscosity of the 0-VI reference oil at 40 °C).
- $L \approx 110 \text{ cSt}$ (kinematic viscosity of the 100-VI reference oil at 40 °C).
- $H = U = 10 \text{ cSt}$ (the oil's own $\nu_{40}$, used to interpolate).

> *Logic:* These constants are read from the standardized ASTM viscosity tables; they are *not* calculated from first principles.

**Step 2 — Apply the Dean–Davis equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VI &= \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{110 - 10}{110 - 95} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{100}{15} \times 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 666.67
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — a VI greater than 100 requires the use of the **ASTM D2270 extension formula**:

**Step 3 — Apply the extension formula (when $H \le U$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VI &= \frac{10^{N} - 1}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
\text{where } N &= \frac{\log H - \log U}{\log Y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $Y$ is defined such that the oil at 100 °C has $\nu_{100}$. Using the standard tables for $\nu_{100}=10$, $Y \approx 16$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\log 95 - \log 10}{\log 16} = \frac{1.9777 - 1.0000}{1.2041} = \frac{0.9777}{1.2041} = 0.8120 \\[6pt]
VI &= \frac{10^{0.8120} - 1}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{6.486 - 1}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{5.486}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 767.27 + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 867
\end{aligned}
$$

This very high VI indicates an **exceptional synthetic oil** (e.g., a PAO with VI-enhancing additives).

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of each transition:** Step 2 was the *base* formula valid only for VI ≤ 100. Because $L > 100$ here (i.e., the oil is *better* than the 100-VI reference), we must invoke the *extension* in Step 3. The constant 0.00715 is a fitted empirical value in the ASTM standard.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Worked Penetration & NLGI Classification

Greases are graded by **NLGI (National Lubricating Grease Institute)** using a **cone penetration test (ASTM D217)**.

**Given:** A standard cone (mass 102.5 g) penetrates $d = 280$ units (each unit = 0.1 mm) in 5 seconds into a grease sample at 25 °C, after 60 double strokes (worked).

**Step 1 — Convert penetration depth to mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{mm} &= 280 \times 0.1 \text{ mm} = 28.0 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{(This is wrong by convention)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The penetration is reported directly in **0.1-mm units**, so $P = 280$.

**Step 2 — Map to NLGI Grade (ASTM D217 worked values):**

| Worked Penetration (0.1 mm) | NLGI Grade | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 445 – 475 | 000 | Gear-box semi-fluid |
| 400 – 430 | 00 | Centralised systems |
| 355 – 385 | 0 | Light gearing |
| 310 – 340 | 1 | Rolling bearings |
| 265 – 295 | 2 | **Most common grease** |
| 220 – 250 | 3 | High-temp bearings |
| 175 – 205 | 4 | Sealed-for-life bearings |
| 130 – 160 | 5 | Block greases |
| 85 – 115 | 6 | Coupling greases |

For $P = 280$, the grease is **NLGI Grade 2** — the most common, multi-purpose grade.

> **Logic:** Penetration *decreases* as consistency *increases* (the grease becomes harder). The deeper the cone sinks, the softer the grease.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation — Acid Number (AN) Calculation

The **Acid Number** measures the acidic constituents in used or new oils (acid = oxidation product).

**Given:** 10 g of oil sample is titrated against KOH (normality = 0.1 N). Volume consumed = 4.2 mL. Blank titration = 0.3 mL.

**Step 1 — Net volume of KOH used.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net} &= V_s - V_b = 4.2 - 0.3 = 3.9 \text{ mL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AN &= \frac{V_{net} \times N \times 56.1}{m} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{3.9 \times 0.1 \times 56.1}{10} \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{21.879}{10} \\[6pt]
   &= 2.1879 \text{ mg KOH/g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The factor 56.1 is the **molecular weight of KOH** (g/mol). Multiplying $V \times N$ gives the *milli-equivalents* of KOH, which when multiplied by 56.1 converts to mg KOH — the standardized reporting unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Master Classification of Lubricants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lubricants - PEMET633 Module 3]:::root --> B1[Liquid Lubricants - Oils]
    A --> B2[Semi-Solid Lubricants - Greases]
    A --> B3[Solid Lubricants]
    A --> B4[Gaseous Lubricants - out of scope]

    B1 --> C1[Mineral Oils]
    B1 --> C2[Synthetic Oils]
    B1 --> C3[Vegetable / Bio Oils]

    C1 --> D1[Paraffinic]
    C1 --> D2[Naphthenic]
    C1 --> D3[Aromatic]

    C2 --> D4[PAO]
    C2 --> D5[Esters]
    C2 --> D6[Silicones]
    C2 --> D7[PFPE]

    B2 --> E1[Simple Soap - Li, Na, Ca]
    B2 --> E2[Complex Soap - Li-Complex, Ca-Complex]
    B2 --> E3[Non-Soap - Polyurea, Bentone]

    B3 --> F1[Lamellar - Graphite, MoS2, WS2, hBN]
    B3 --> F2[Non-Lamellar - PTFE, Soft Metals]

    classDef root fill:#0b3d91,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef default fill:#e3f2fd,color:#000,stroke:#0277bd
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture: Grease Composition & Performance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Grease]:::nodeA --> B[Base Oil 70-95 percent]
    A --> C[Thickener 5-25 percent]
    A --> D[Additives 0-10 percent]

    B --> B1[Mineral Oil]
    B --> B2[Synthetic Oil]

    C --> C1[Soap Fibers - 3D Network]
    C1 --> C2[Traps Base Oil like sponge]

    D --> D1[Anti-Oxidant - ZDDP, phenols]
    D --> D2[EP Additive - S, P, Cl]
    D --> D3[Anti-Wear - MoDTC]
    D --> D4[Corrosion Inhibitor]

    B2 --> E[Performance Output]
    C2 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F1[Drop Point]
    E --> F2[NLGI Consistency]
    E --> F3[Shear Stability]
    E --> F4[Oil Bleed Rate]

    classDef nodeA fill:#b71c1c,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef default fill:#fff8e1,color:#000,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solid Lubricant Working Mechanism (Lamellar)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sliding Surface A]:::metal --> B[Lamellar Solid Lubricant - e.g. MoS2]
    B --> C[Sliding Surface B]:::metal

    B --> B1[Layer 1 - Weakly bonded]
    B --> B2[Layer 2 - Strong covalent]
    B --> B3[Layer 3 - Weakly bonded]

    B1 --> D1[Shear Plane 1 - slides easily]
    B3 --> D2[Shear Plane 2 - slides easily]

    D1 --> E[Friction Reduced - Coefficient 0.001 to 0.1]
    D2 --> E

    E --> F1[Vacuum Stable]
    E --> F2[High Load Bearing]

    classDef metal fill:#455a64,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef default fill:#ede7f6,color:#000,stroke:#5e35b1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lubricant Selection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Application Defined]:::start --> B{Operating Temperature}
    B -->|less than -30 degC| C1[PFPE or Silicone]
    B -->|-30 to 150 degC| C2[Mineral or Synthetic Oil]
    B -->|more than 150 degC| C3[PFPE or Solid Lubricant]

    C2 --> D{Environment}
    D -->|Sealed| E1[Oil Bath or Splash]
    D -->|Open / Dusty| E2[Grease NLGI 2]

    E1 --> F1[Choose Viscosity by Speed x Load]
    E2 --> F2[Choose Thickener by Temp Drop Point]

    C3 --> G{Atmosphere}
    G -->|Air| G1[Graphite - needs water vapour]
    G -->|Vacuum| G2[MoS2 - works dry]

    F1 --> H[End]
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    C1 --> H

    classDef start fill:#1b5e20,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef default fill:#e0f7fa,color:#000,stroke:#006064
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between mineral oil, synthetic oil, and vegetable oil as lubricants, citing two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Mineral Oils:** Refined from crude petroleum; composed of hydrocarbons (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic). Examples: SN-500 (paraffinic spindle oil), pale paraffin oil (naphthenic). Cost-effective; dominant in industry.
- **Synthetic Oils:** Chemically engineered molecules (PAO, esters, silicones, PFPE). Examples: PAO 6, dioctyl sebacate ester. Superior thermal stability, VI > 130, longer drain intervals; expensive.
- **Vegetable Oils:** Extracted from plant seeds (triglyceride structure). Examples: castor oil, rapeseed (canola) oil. Renewable, biodegradable, high lubricity; poor oxidation stability.

*Examiner expects: 1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for one key property contrast.*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: List the four essential constituents of a grease with their typical percentage ranges. Why is the thickener called the 'sponge of grease'?**

**Model Answer:**
| Constituent | % Range | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Base oil | 70–95 % | Actual lubricant film |
| Thickener | 5–25 % | Holds oil in 3-D network |
| Additives | 0–10 % | Enhance performance |
| Solid fillers (sometimes) | 0–5 % | Extreme pressure, conductivity |

**Why thickener is called a "sponge":** The thickener (e.g., lithium 12-hydroxystearate soap) forms a microscopic fibrous 3-D matrix that physically **traps the base oil** within its interstices, releasing it gradually under shear — exactly as a sponge holds and releases water. This controlled release gives grease its characteristic **"stay-in-place"** behavior.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Question (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Classify lubricants used in industry. With neat sketches, explain the **structure of a grease**, clearly indicating the role of each component. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **properties, working mechanism, and applications of solid lubricants** like graphite and MoS₂. Justify why MoS₂ is preferred over graphite in **space / vacuum applications**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

A **classification of lubricants** is as follows:
1. **Liquid (Oils)** — Mineral, synthetic, vegetable.
2. **Semi-solid (Greases)** — Oil + thickener + additives.
3. **Solid** — Lamellar (graphite, MoS₂), polymers (PTFE), soft metals.
4. **Gaseous** — Air, helium (gas bearings).

**Grease Structure (Sketch):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
   │     Oil (held in matrix)    │   ← Base oil
   │  ◊  Soap fibers  ◊  Soap    │   ← Thickener (3-D network)
   │    fibers   ◊ fibers  ◊     │
   │  ◊  fibers  ◊  fibers  ◊    │
   └─────────────────────────────┘
   + Additives (ZDDP, MoDTC, EP, etc.)
```

- **Base oil (70–95 %):** Performs the actual lubrication; viscosity defines the load-carrying ability.
- **Thickener (5–25 %):** Soap fibers form a sponge-like 3-D network that traps and slowly releases oil under shear.
- **Additives (0–10 %):** Anti-oxidant (ZDDP), anti-wear (MoDTC), EP (sulphurised fat), corrosion inhibitor (succinimide).

*Marking scheme: 2 marks classification + 3 marks grease diagram + 2 marks component roles.*

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Graphite:**
- Layer-lattice structure; carbon atoms in hexagonal planes bonded by **weak van der Waals forces**.
- Inter-layer shear gives low friction ($\mu \approx 0.1$–$0.2$).
- **Requires adsorbed water vapour** to reduce bond strength between layers; therefore *fails in vacuum* and at very high temperatures.

**MoS₂:**
- Molybdenum atoms sandwiched between **two sulphur planes**; S–S bonding is *very weak*.
- Friction coefficient $\mu \approx 0.002$–$0.1$.
- Works in **vacuum**, dry atmospheres, and at moderate temperatures up to **350 °C** (in inert atmosphere).

**Why MoS₂ is preferred in space:**
1. **No environmental dependence** — it does not need adsorbed gases to lubricate.
2. **Vacuum stability** — graphite loses its lubricity in vacuum because the adsorbed water vapour is desorbed.
3. **Higher load capacity** — MoS₂ retains shear planes under very high contact pressure.
4. **Lower outgassing** — critical in vacuum chambers to avoid contamination of optical / sensor surfaces.

*Marking scheme: 2 marks each for graphite and MoS₂ properties + 2 marks mechanism + 1 mark comparison table + 2 marks space justification.*

---

#### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the following properties of lubricating oils with their significance: **Viscosity, Viscosity Index, Flash Point, and Neutralisation Number.** *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The kinematic viscosity of an oil at 40 °C is 95 cSt and at 100 °C is 10 cSt. Determine its **Viscosity Index (VI)**. Given the standard ASTM D2270 values for $\nu_{100} = 10 \text{ cSt}$: $U = 10$, $L = 110$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution — (a):**

| Property | Definition | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Viscosity** | Resistance to flow (internal friction) | Determines film thickness; high viscosity = thick film, used in heavy-load low-speed bearings |
| **Viscosity Index (VI)** | Empirical measure of viscosity-temperature slope | High VI = viscosity changes less with temperature (multi-grade engine oils need VI > 130) |
| **Flash Point** | Lowest temperature at which vapours ignite momentarily | Critical for storage safety, transformer oils (high flash point preferred) |
| **Neutralisation Number** | mg KOH/g to neutralize acidic/basic components | Indicates oxidation level; high AN = degraded oil, needs replacement |

*Marking scheme: 1.75 marks per property (0.5 def + 0.75 significance + 0.5 example/standard).*

**Model Solution — (b):**

Since $H = \nu_{40} = 95 \text{ cSt}$, and the oil is **better than the 100-VI reference** (because $H = 95 < L = 110$), we must use the **extension formula**.

**Step 1:** Compute $N$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &\approx 16 \text{ (from ASTM table for } \nu_{100} = 10 \text{ cSt)} \\
N &= \frac{\log H - \log U}{\log Y} = \frac{\log 95 - \log 10}{\log 16} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{1.9777 - 1.0000}{1.2041} = \frac{0.9777}{1.2041} = 0.8120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute VI using the extension formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VI &= \frac{10^{N} - 1}{0.00715} + 100 = \frac{10^{0.8120} - 1}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= \frac{6.486 - 1}{0.00715} + 100 = \frac{5.486}{0.00715} + 100 \\[6pt]
   &= 767.27 + 100 \approx \mathbf{867}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** VI ≈ **867** — indicates a premium-grade synthetic oil (PAO with VI improvers).

*Marking scheme: 2 marks identifying extension case + 2 marks computing N + 2 marks VI formula + 1 mark final answer + comment.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not blindly use the linear Dean–Davis formula** when $\nu_{40}$ is *less* than $L$. Always compare $H$ with $L$ first. Using the wrong formula is the #1 reason for losing 4–5 marks.
> 2. **Units of viscosity in tables:** ASTM D2270 uses cSt for $U$, $L$, $H$. Do not feed in SI units (m²/s) directly; convert first.
> 3. **Grease questions:** Students commonly confuse **drop point** with **melting point**. Drop point is *lower*; the grease becomes workable before it actually melts.
> 4. **Solid lubricants:** Confusing **graphite needing moisture** vs **MoS₂ working dry in vacuum** is a very common error — examiners love testing this.
> 5. **NLGI Grade:** Remember — *higher grade = harder grease = lower penetration number*. Students often invert this.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ Lubricants are classified into **4 categories**: Oils, Greases, Solid, Gaseous.
- ☐ **Viscosity** is the most important property — measured by **kinematic** (cSt) and **dynamic** (cP) forms; $\nu = \mu / \rho$.
- ☐ **Viscosity Index (VI)** = empirical; >100 uses **extension formula**, ≤100 uses Dean–Davis.
- ☐ **Flash point** < **Fire point**; difference is **≥ 20 °C**.
- ☐ **Pour point** is the lowest flow temperature; **Cloud point** is when wax first appears.
- ☐ **Acid Number (AN)** measures acidity; **TBN** measures alkalinity (used in marine engine oils).
- ☐ Grease = **Base oil + Thickener + Additives**; thickener forms a 3-D sponge network.
- ☐ **NLGI Grade 2** is the most common (penetration 265–295 dmm).
- ☐ **Drop point** indicates grease's upper temperature limit.
- ☐ **Graphite** needs adsorbed moisture; **MoS₂** works in vacuum.
- ☐ **PTFE** is the lowest-friction solid ($\mu \approx 0.04$); used in non-stick + cryogenic applications.
- ☐ **Synthetic oils** (PAO, ester, PFPE, silicone) outperform mineral oils in VI, thermal range, and oxidation resistance.
- ☐ **Walther equation** $\mu_T = \mu_{40} \cdot 10^{(40-T)/C}$ is the ASTM D341 standard for viscosity-temperature plotting.
- ☐ Solid lubricants are used when **liquids fail**: extreme temperatures, vacuum, high loads, or chemicals.
- ☐ Always choose lubricant by **operating regime**: speed × load × temperature × environment.

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Types of Lubricants (PEMET633).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Additives and Their Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Additives and Their Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Lubricant Additives** are chemical compounds, typically organic or organometallic, that are blended into **base oils** (mineral, synthetic, or vegetable) in carefully controlled concentrations (usually **0.1% to 30% by mass**) to enhance the existing properties of the base fluid or to impart entirely new functional characteristics that the base oil alone cannot provide.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, additives are the *functional heart* of any modern industrial lubricant. A base oil without additives can only support **hydrodynamic (full-film) lubrication** regimes. It is the additive package that extends the lubricant's operational envelope into the **boundary** and **mixed lubrication** regimes, where asperity contact dominates.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Multivitamin for Your Engine"**
>
> Imagine base oil as plain water. It hydrates, but it cannot fight infection, reduce inflammation, or repair cells. *Additives* are like the medicines and vitamins dissolved in the water — each one targets a specific problem. A friction modifier is like an "anti-inflammatory" for surfaces rubbing together; an antioxidant is the "preservative" that stops the oil from going rancid; a detergent is the "soap" that cleans deposits off metal walls.
>
> Without additives, even the best base oil will fail prematurely under high loads, high temperatures, or contamination.

## 1.3 The "Why" Behind Additive Use

Base oils by themselves are limited in three critical ways:

1. **Thermal & Oxidative Instability** — base oils oxidize rapidly above **80 °C to 120 °C**, forming acids, sludge, and varnishes.
2. **Inadequate Load-Carrying Capacity** — pure oil films collapse under extreme pressure (EP) or high contact stress (Hertzian contact).
3. **Poor Low-Temperature Fluidity** — base oils thicken and gel at low temperatures, limiting their usable temperature range.

Additives solve all three problems and more.

## 1.4 Lubricant Performance Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> Any commercial lubricant formulation is governed by the **Performance Triangle**:
>
> **Base Oil + Additive Package + Viscosity Grade = Optimal Performance**
>
> Changing any one vertex without re-balancing the other two leads to failure modes such as additive dropout, seal swelling, or excessive wear.

## 1.5 Standard Test Metrics Used in KTU Module Context

- **ASTM D2896** — Total Base Number (TBN), measured in **mg KOH/g**
- **ASTM D974** — Total Acid Number (TAN), measured in **mg KOH/g**
- **ASTM D2270** — Viscosity Index (VI), dimensionless
- **ASTM D892** — Foaming characteristics, in **mL** (sequences I, II, III)
- **ASTM D4172** — Four-Ball Wear Test (Wear Scar Diameter, WSD in **mm**)
- **ASTM D2783** — Four-Ball Extreme Pressure Test (Weld Point in **kgf**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Additives — Master Hierarchy

Lubricant additives are classified by their **primary function**. A single additive molecule can sometimes perform multiple roles (e.g., ZDDP acts as both an anti-wear and an antioxidant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Macro-Categories:**
>
> 1. **Performance Additives** — directly affect lubrication, wear, and friction (anti-wear, EP, FM, detergents, dispersants).
> 2. **Protection Additives** — protect the lubricant itself (antioxidants, rust inhibitors, anti-foam, demulsifiers).

## 2.2 Detailed Functional Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Anti-Wear (AW) Additives

- **Function:** Form a **sacrificial protective film** on metal surfaces to prevent direct asperity contact in the **boundary lubrication regime**.
- **Working chemistry:** Tribochemical reaction with the surface at asperity flash-temperatures (200 °C – 600 °C) producing a *polymeric metal-salt film*.
- **Canonical example:** **ZDDP — Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate**, $\text{Zn}[\text{SP(S)(OR)}_2]_2$.
- **Typical treat rate:** **0.5% – 1.5% by mass**.
- **Mechanism (Stribeck-relevant):** Operates in the boundary regime where the lambda ratio $\lambda \le 1$.

### 2.2.2 Extreme Pressure (EP) Additives

- **Function:** Engage at very high contact pressures (Hertzian contact stress $\gg 1 \text{ GPa}$) where AW additives fail.
- **Chemistry:** Contain **reactive S, P, or Cl** atoms that form **iron sulfide, iron phosphide, or iron chloride** sacrificial films at high flash temperatures.
- **Examples:** Sulfurized fats, chlorinated paraffins, sulfur-phosphorus compounds, **molybdenum dithiocarbamate (MoDTC)**.
- **Limitation:** Corrosive to **yellow metals** (copper, bronze) — hence the **ASTM D130 Copper Strip Corrosion Test**.

### 2.2.3 Friction Modifiers (FM)

- **Function:** Reduce the **kinetic coefficient of friction** $\mu_k$ in the boundary and mixed regimes.
- **Mechanism:** Form physically-adsorbed (rather than chemically-bonded) low-shear-strength films.
- **Types:**
  - **Fatty acids** (e.g., stearic acid) — polar head attaches to metal, hydrocarbon tail provides low-shear plane.
  - **Fatty alcohols and esters** — milder, used in metalworking fluids.
  - **Molybdenum disulfide ($\text{MoS}_2$)** in colloidal suspension — laminar solid lubricant.
  - **Graphite** — works only in presence of adsorbed water vapor (graphite needs water to shear).

### 2.2.4 Viscosity Index Improvers (VII)

- **Function:** Reduce the rate at which viscosity decreases with rising temperature.
- **Chemistry:** High-molecular-weight **polymers** (PMAs — polymethacrylates, OCPs — olefin copolymers, styrene-esters).
- **Key metric:** **Viscosity Index (VI)** — higher VI = flatter viscosity–temperature curve.
- **Mechanism:** Polymer coils expand at high T (counteracting oil thinning) and contract at low T (not thickening the oil too much).

### 2.2.5 Pour Point Depressants (PPD)

- **Function:** Lower the **pour point** — the lowest temperature at which oil will flow under standard test conditions (ASTM D97).
- **Chemistry:** Wax-modified polymers (e.g., polyalkyl methacrylates, alkylated polystyrene).
- **Mechanism:** Co-crystallize with paraffin wax crystals, preventing them from forming an interlocking 3-D gel network.

### 2.2.6 Detergents

- **Function:** **Neutralize acidic combustion byproducts** and keep them suspended in the oil; also **clean surfaces** of varnish and lacquer deposits.
- **Chemistry:** **Overbased** calcium or magnesium sulfonates, phenates, salicylates, or carboxylates.
- **Key metric:** **TBN** (Total Base Number) — directly indicates neutralizing capacity.
- **Construction:** A **calcite carbonate core** ($\text{CaCO}_3$ nanoparticles, 5–50 nm) encapsulated in a surfactant shell and dispersed in oil.

### 2.2.7 Dispersants

- **Function:** Keep **insoluble contaminants** (soot, sludge precursors, oxidation products) suspended in the bulk oil so they do not agglomerate into deposits.
- **Chemistry:** **Ashless** organic compounds — polyisobutenyl succinimide (PIBSA-PAM) is the workhorse.
- **Difference from detergents:** Dispersants suspend *solid* and *liquid* contaminants; detergents neutralize *acids* and clean deposits.

### 2.2.8 Antioxidants

- **Function:** Slow down oxidative degradation of the base oil.
- **Types:**
  - **Primary (radical scavengers):** Hindered phenols, aromatic amines.
  - **Secondary (peroxide decomposers):** ZDDP, phosphites, thioesters.
- **Chemistry of oxidation chain:**
  $$\text{RH} + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{heat}} \text{R}^\bullet + \text{HOO}^\bullet$$
  $$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^\bullet \rightarrow \text{ROOH} \rightarrow \text{acids, sludge}$$

### 2.2.9 Anti-Foam Additives

- **Function:** Prevent stable foam formation in circulating oil systems.
- **Chemistry:** **Silicone polymers** (polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS) — typically **1 – 50 ppm**.
- **Mechanism:** Spread at the air-oil interface as a low-surface-tension film, destabilizing foam lamellae.

### 2.2.10 Rust & Corrosion Inhibitors

- **Function:** Form a hydrophobic film on metal surfaces to exclude water and oxygen.
- **Examples:** **Succinimide**, fatty acid amines, calcium petroleum sulfonates.

### 2.2.11 Demulsifiers
