- **Function:** Promote rapid **separation of water from oil** in systems where water ingress is common (e.g., marine, steam-turbine lubricants).
- **Chemistry:** Ethoxylated phenol-formaldehyde resins, polyalkoxylated polyamines.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Module 3 — Additives**

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{\min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | $\lambda$ = lambda ratio, $h_{\min}$ = minimum film thickness, $R_q$ = RMS roughness | dimensionless | Regime selection (boundary, mixed, full-film) |
| 2 | $\mu = \mu_b + \mu_v + \mu_c$ | Boundary, viscous, and cohesive friction components | dimensionless | Friction decomposition |
| 3 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{3F}{2\pi a b}$ | Max Hertzian contact pressure | Pa | EP additive trigger threshold |
| 4 | $h_{min} = 2.65 R_x U^{0.7} G^{0.54} W^{-0.13}$ | Hamrock-Dowson min film thickness | m | EHL film prediction |
| 5 | $\text{TBN} = \dfrac{(V \cdot N \cdot 56.1)}{m}$ | $V$ = titrant vol, $N$ = normality, $m$ = sample mass | mg KOH/g | Detergent neutralizing capacity |
| 6 | $\text{VI} = \dfrac{10^N - 1}{0.7}$ (approx.) | Viscosity Index | dimensionless | VII effectiveness |
| 7 | $\Delta \mu = \mu_{base} - \mu_{add}$ | Friction reduction due to additive | dimensionless | FM performance metric |
| 8 | $k = A e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius — oxidation rate | varies | Antioxidant thermal stability |
| 9 | $t_{f} = \dfrac{C_{ox}}{k_{ox}}$ | Time-to-failure (oxidation-limited) | hours | Service life prediction |
| 10 | $\gamma_{oil/air} \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{foaming} \uparrow$ | Surface tension vs. foaming tendency | N/m | Anti-foam logic |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in derivations below use $\vert \cdot \vert$ only — never `| |` inside a markdown row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive engine oils:** ZDDP + dispersant + VII + detergent package (API SP, ILSAC GF-6 specs).
- **Industrial gear oils:** EP-sulfur-phosphorus + AW + anti-foam (AGMA, ISO 12925).
- **Metalworking fluids:** Fatty-alcohol FMs + chlorinated-paraffin EP + biocides + rust inhibitors.
- **Wind-turbine gearboxes:** Synthetic PAO base + MoDTC + antioxidants — operates from −30 °C to 120 °C.
- **Aviation gas-turbine oils:** Ester base + TCP (tricresyl phosphate) anti-wear — non-Zn, ashless.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Linking Additive Treat Rate to TBN

**Problem setup:** A finished engine oil is blended by adding an overbased calcium sulfonate detergent (TBN = **400 mg KOH/g**) to a base oil (TBN ≈ 0) at a treat rate of $m_{add}$ grams per 100 g of base oil. Derive the **final oil TBN** and compute it for a typical 2.5% treat rate.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

By definition of TBN:

$$\text{TBN} = \dfrac{\text{mg KOH equivalent to neutralize 1 g of sample}}{\text{1 g of sample}}$$

Let us define:
- $m_b$ = mass of base oil (g)
- $m_a$ = mass of additive (g)
- $\text{TBN}_a$ = additive TBN (mg KOH/g)
- $\text{TBN}_b$ = base oil TBN (mg KOH/g) — typically 0
- $\text{TBN}_{mix}$ = mixed oil TBN (mg KOH/g)

The **total KOH-equivalent content** in the blend is the mass-weighted sum:

$$(\text{Total KOH-eq}) = \text{TBN}_a \cdot m_a + \text{TBN}_b \cdot m_b$$

The **total mass of the sample** is:

$$m_{total} = m_a + m_b$$

Hence the **mixture TBN** is:

$$\text{TBN}_{mix} = \dfrac{\text{TBN}_a \cdot m_a + \text{TBN}_b \cdot m_b}{m_a + m_b}$$

Since $\text{TBN}_b \approx 0$ for a virgin base oil:

$$\text{TBN}_{mix} \approx \dfrac{\text{TBN}_a \cdot m_a}{m_a + m_b}$$

**Numerical substitution:** Take $\text{TBN}_a = 400$ mg KOH/g, $m_a = 2.5$ g, $m_b = 97.5$ g:

$$\text{TBN}_{mix} = \dfrac{400 \times 2.5}{2.5 + 97.5} = \dfrac{1000}{100.0} = 10.0 \;\text{mg KOH/g}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 2.5% addition of a 400-TBN detergent yields a finished oil TBN of **10 mg KOH/g** — a typical value for a heavy-duty diesel engine oil (API CK-4 class).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Effect of VII on Kinematic Viscosity

**Setup:** A polymethacrylate VII has intrinsic viscosity $[\eta]$. Derive how the **kinematic viscosity** of the finished oil depends on the polymer concentration $c$ (in g/dL) and temperature.

### Derivation

The **Huggins equation** gives the relative viscosity increment due to a dissolved polymer:

$$\dfrac{\eta - \eta_0}{\eta_0 \cdot c} = [\eta] + k_H [\eta]^2 \cdot c$$

where:
- $\eta_0$ = base oil kinematic viscosity (cSt)
- $\eta$ = blend kinematic viscosity (cSt)
- $c$ = polymer concentration (g/dL)
- $k_H$ = Huggins constant (≈ 0.3 – 0.4 for flexible polymers in oil)
- $[\eta]$ = intrinsic viscosity (dL/g)

**At very low $c$** (the limit relevant to VII, typically 1–5% mass), the quadratic term is small and we can linearize:

$$\dfrac{\eta - \eta_0}{\eta_0 \cdot c} \approx [\eta]$$

Solving for $\eta$:

$$\eta \approx \eta_0 \left( 1 + [\eta] \cdot c \right)$$

**Temperature dependence:** The VII polymer expands as $T$ rises, increasing its effective hydrodynamic volume. The empirical **Walters / polymer-coil model** states:

$$[\eta]_T = [\eta]_{T_{ref}} \cdot \exp\!\left[ \beta \left( \dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} \right) \right]$$

where $\beta > 0$ is the polymer–oil interaction parameter.

**Substituting back:**

$$\eta(T) \approx \eta_0(T)\left(1 + c \cdot [\eta]_{T_{ref}} \cdot e^{\beta\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)}\right)$$

This is the core reason why a high-VI oil is *flatter* on the $\eta$ vs. $T$ curve: as $T$ increases, $\eta_0(T)$ falls but $[\eta]_T$ rises, partially compensating.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Boundary-Film Friction with Friction Modifier

**Setup:** Derive the friction coefficient in the **boundary lubrication regime** when a fatty-acid FM forms a monolayer film of thickness $t$ and shear strength $\tau_f$ on a sliding contact under normal load $W$ and contact area $A_c$.

### Derivation

In boundary lubrication, the load is supported by the asperity contacts and by the FM film, with the film shearing at its own yield strength $\tau_f$. The frictional force is:

$$F_f = \tau_f \cdot A_c$$

The contact pressure in asperity contact is approximately:

$$p = \dfrac{W}{A_c}$$

Dividing:

$$\mu_b = \dfrac{F_f}{W} = \dfrac{\tau_f \cdot A_c}{W} = \dfrac{\tau_f}{p}$$

Thus:

$$\mu_b = \dfrac{\tau_f}{p}$$

Now, when **no FM is present**, the surface shear strength is that of the metal-metal junction, $\tau_m$ (typically 50–500 MPa). When an FM monolayer is present:

$$\tau_f \ll \tau_m$$

The friction reduction is therefore:

$$\Delta \mu = \mu_{base} - \mu_{FM} = \dfrac{\tau_m - \tau_f}{p}$$

**Numerical example:** For $p = 500$ MPa, $\tau_m = 100$ MPa, $\tau_f = 10$ MPa:

$$\mu_{base} = \dfrac{100}{500} = 0.20$$

$$\mu_{FM} = \dfrac{10}{500} = 0.02$$

$$\Delta \mu = 0.18 \;\; (\text{a 90% reduction})$$

This is the **quantitative justification** for using FMs in low-viscosity engine oils (e.g., 0W-20 specifications).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Oxidation Induction Time

**Setup:** A lubricant is held at constant elevated temperature $T$. The peroxide number $P$ (in meq/kg) grows as an **autocatalytic chain** of rate constant $k_{ox}$. Derive the **time $t_f$** at which the peroxide level reaches a failure threshold $P_f$.

### Derivation

For an autocatalytic reaction (chain-branching), the empirical rate equation is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dt} = k_{ox} \cdot P \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{P}{P_{max}} \right)$$

where $P_{max}$ is the saturation level. This is a **logistic differential equation**.

Separating variables:

$$\int \dfrac{dP}{P\left(1 - P/P_{max}\right)} = \int k_{ox} \, dt$$

Using partial fractions:

$$\dfrac{1}{P\left(1 - P/P_{max}\right)} = \dfrac{1}{P} + \dfrac{1}{P_{max} - P}$$

Integrating:

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{P}{P_{max} - P} \right) = k_{ox} \cdot t + C_0$$

At $t = 0$, $P = P_0$ (initial peroxide level), so:

$$C_0 = \ln \left( \dfrac{P_0}{P_{max} - P_0} \right)$$

Therefore:

$$\ln \left( \dfrac{P \cdot (P_{max} - P_0)}{P_0 \cdot (P_{max} - P)} \right) = k_{ox} \cdot t$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \dfrac{1}{k_{ox}} \ln \left[ \dfrac{P (P_{max} - P_0)}{P_0 (P_{max} - P)} \right]$$

The **time to reach failure threshold** $P = P_f$:

$$\boxed{\;t_f = \dfrac{1}{k_{ox}} \ln \left[ \dfrac{P_f (P_{max} - P_0)}{P_0 (P_{max} - P_f)} \right]\;}$$

**Effect of antioxidant:** The antioxidant **scavenges radicals**, effectively reducing $k_{ox}$ via the Arrhenius relationship:

$$k_{ox, \text{with AO}} = k_{ox, 0} \cdot \exp\!\left( - \dfrac{E_a}{R T} \right) \cdot \left( 1 - \alpha \cdot c_{AO} \right)$$

where $\alpha$ is an antioxidant-specific activity factor and $c_{AO}$ is its concentration.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Additive Categories

| Additive Type | Regime of Action | Surface / Bulk Effect | Typical Concentration | Failure Mode if Misused |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Anti-wear (ZDDP) | Boundary | Surface film (Fe-polyphosphate) | 0.5 – 1.5% | Catalyst poisoning, ash deposits |
| Extreme Pressure (S–P) | Boundary / EP | Sacrificial FeS / FeP film | 1.0 – 5.0% | Copper corrosion, odor |
| Friction Modifier (FM) | Boundary / Mixed | Physically-adsorbed monolayer | 0.1 – 2.0% | Stripped off at high T |
| Viscosity Index Improver (VII) | All regimes | Bulk — polymeric coil expansion | 1.0 – 10% | Shear degradation (permanent viscosity loss) |
| Pour Point Depressant (PPD) | Low T bulk | Co-crystallizes with wax | 0.1 – 1.0% | Insufficient if wax type changes |
| Detergent | Bulk + surface | Neutralizes acids, cleans | 1.0 – 6.0% | Ash, base-depletion at high T |
| Dispersant | Bulk | Suspends soot/sludge | 2.0 – 8.0% | Foaming at high treat |
| Antioxidant | Bulk | Radical scavenger / peroxide decomposer | 0.1 – 2.0% | Oxidation-induced sludge |
| Anti-foam (PDMS) | Bulk + interface | Reduces surface tension | 1 – 50 ppm | Air entrainment if overdosed |
| Rust Inhibitor | Surface | Hydrophobic film | 0.05 – 1.0% | Stripped by water washout |
| Demulsifier | Bulk | Breaks W/O emulsion | 0.01 – 0.5% | Inverted emulsion if overdosed |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Boundary vs. Full-Film Selection

**Given:** A steel shaft ($R_{q1} = 0.4 \;\mu\text{m}$) runs in a steel sleeve ($R_{q2} = 0.5 \;\mu\text{m}$) with $h_{min} = 0.7 \;\mu\text{m}$ computed by Hamrock-Dowson.

**Compute** $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}} = \dfrac{0.7}{\sqrt{0.4^2 + 0.5^2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{0.7}{\sqrt{0.16 + 0.25}} = \dfrac{0.7}{\sqrt{0.41}} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.6403} \approx 1.09$$

Since $\lambda \approx 1$, we are on the boundary between **mixed** ($1 \le \lambda \le 3$) and **boundary** ($\lambda < 1$) lubrication — this is precisely the regime where **AW additives (ZDDP)** are essential.

> [!NOTE]
> **General regime map:**
> - $\lambda < 1$ — Boundary (additive-dominated)
> - $1 \le \lambda \le 3$ — Mixed (additive + EHD)
> - $\lambda > 3$ — Full-film (additives not load-carrying)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Additive Functional Architecture in a Lubricant System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **interactions between additive categories** within a formulated lubricant, as well as the **inputs (base oil + additives)** and **outputs (lubricant performance characteristics)**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Base Oil Mineral or Synthetic] --> B1[Formulated Lubricant Blend]
    A2[Additive Package] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Anti-Wear AW]
    B1 --> C2[Extreme Pressure EP]
    B1 --> C3[Friction Modifier FM]
    B1 --> C4[Viscosity Index Improver VII]
    B1 --> C5[Pour Point Depressant PPD]
    B1 --> C6[Detergent]
    B1 --> C7[Dispersant]
    B1 --> C8[Antioxidant AO]
    B1 --> C9[Anti-Foam]
    B1 --> C10[Rust Inhibitor]
    B1 --> C11[Demulsifier]
    C1 --> D1[Surface Film Formation]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Boundary Lubrication]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D3[Bulk Viscosity Stability]
    C5 --> D3
    C6 --> D4[Acid Neutralization and Cleaning]
    C7 --> D5[Contaminant Suspension]
    C8 --> D6[Oxidation Resistance]
    C9 --> D7[Foam Suppression]
    C10 --> D8[Corrosion Protection]
    C11 --> D9[Water Separation]
    D1 --> E1[Final Tribological Performance]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    D7 --> E1
    D8 --> E1
    D9 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Engine Gears Bearings Turbines Compressors]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Flow — ZDDP Anti-Wear Tribochemical Reaction

The following diagram illustrates the **mechanism of ZDDP film formation** on a ferrous (Fe) surface under boundary lubrication. The additive decomposes tribochemically and forms a **polyphosphate glassy film**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Steel Surface Fe] --> S2[Local Flash Temperature from Asperity Contact]
    S2 --> S3[ZDDP Decomposition]
    S3 --> S4[Polyphosphate Glassy Film Zn Fe Polyphosphate]
    S4 --> S5[Load Bearing Sacrificial Layer]
    S5 --> S6[Reduced Asperity Welding and Adhesive Wear]
    S6 --> S7[Lower Friction and Wear Rate]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Selection Logic for Additive Type vs. Lubrication Regime

This block diagram is a **decision logic** mapping lubrication regime (Stribeck curve position) to the correct additive class.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Operating Condition Detected] --> R1{Compute Lambda Ratio}
    R1 --> R2{Lambda less than 1}
    R1 --> R3{Lambda between 1 and 3}
    R1 --> R4{Lambda greater than 3}
    R2 --> R5[Select AW and FM Additives]
    R3 --> R6[Select AW plus Mild VII]
    R4 --> R7[Select VII plus Antioxidant plus Anti-Foam]
    R5 --> R8[Boundary Film Formation]
    R6 --> R9[Mixed Film and EHD Support]
    R7 --> R10[Full Film Viscous Support]
    R8 --> R11[Lubricant Performance Verified]
    R9 --> R11
    R10 --> R11
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Detergent Micelle Architecture

Overbased detergent molecules form **inverse micelles** in oil, with a **calcium carbonate core** stabilized by a surfactant shell.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Oil Matrix] --> N2[Surfactant Shell Sulfonate Phenate Salicylate]
    N2 --> N3[Calcium Carbonate Core Calcite 5 to 50 nm]
    N3 --> N4[Reserve Alkalinity TBN 100 to 400 mg KOH per g]
    N4 --> N5[Acid Neutralization Site in Combustion]
    N5 --> N6[Converted to Sulfate Ash Waste]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — Oxidation Inhibition Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Base Oil RH] --> O2[Heat O2]
    O2 --> O3[Radical R]
    O3 --> O4[Peroxide ROO]
    O4 --> O5[Acids Sludge Varnish]
    O5 --> O6[Lubricant Failure]
    AO1[Antioxidant Hindered Phenol] --> AO2[Scavenge Radical R]
    AO2 --> O3
    AO3[Antioxidant ZDDP Secondary] --> AO4[Decompose Peroxide ROO]
    AO4 --> O4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "lubricant additive" and explain why base oils alone are not sufficient for modern industrial lubricants. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **lubricant additive** is a chemical substance blended into a base oil in small concentrations (typically 0.1% – 30% by mass) to enhance existing properties or impart new functional capabilities, such as anti-wear, extreme-pressure resistance, oxidation inhibition, and contamination control.

> Base oils alone are insufficient because: **(i)** they fail to provide adequate load-carrying capacity in the **boundary lubrication regime** where asperity contact occurs; **(ii)** they oxidize rapidly above 80 °C – 120 °C, producing acids, sludge, and varnish; **(iii)** they have limited viscosity-temperature behavior and pour-point characteristics; and **(iv)** they cannot suspend contaminants or neutralize combustion byproducts.

> **Two examples:** ZDDP (anti-wear + antioxidant) and polyisobutenyl succinimide (dispersant).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of an extreme pressure (EP) additive? How does it differ chemically from an anti-wear (AW) additive?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of EP additives:** They form a **sacrificial, low-shear-strength chemical reaction film** (e.g., iron sulfide or iron phosphide) on rubbing metal surfaces under very high contact pressures, preventing welding and seizure of asperities.

> **Difference from AW additives:** AW additives (like ZDDP) react tribochemically at moderate flash temperatures (200 °C – 600 °C) to form a polyphosphate glassy film. EP additives contain **highly reactive sulfur, phosphorus, or chlorine** that react at higher flash temperatures to form sacrificial **inorganic metal-salt films**. EP additives are more aggressive and corrosive, and are not used where yellow metals (Cu, bronze) are present.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "anti-wear" and "extreme pressure" — many students mark them as synonyms and lose **3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Always state the treat rate** (typical % by mass) when you name an additive — examiners expect it.
> 3. When asked about ZDDP, mention **all three** of its functions: anti-wear, antioxidant (secondary), and corrosion inhibitor. Partial answers lose **2 marks**.
> 4. In derivation questions, **show the substitution of numerical values** and **box the final answer**. Unboxed answers are penalized **1 mark** in the "presentation" category.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **tribochemical mechanism of ZDDP as an anti-wear additive**. Discuss the chemical structure, the role of zinc, phosphorus, and sulfur in the molecule, and the conditions under which the protective film forms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A heavy-duty diesel engine oil is blended by adding an overbased calcium sulfonate detergent (TBN = 380 mg KOH/g) at a treat rate of **2.8% by mass** to a paraffinic base oil (TBN = 0). Compute the **final TBN of the blended oil**. Comment on whether this TBN is suitable for an **API CK-4** specification engine running on high-sulfur diesel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ZDDP — Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate** has the general formula:

$$\text{Zn[S-P(=S)(OR)_2]_2}$$

where R is typically an alkyl group (C4 – C12).

**Mechanism of film formation:**

1. **Adsorption:** ZDDP molecules physisorb on the freshly-exposed Fe surface as asperity contact exposes fresh metal.
2. **Tribochemical decomposition:** Local flash temperatures of **200 °C – 600 °C** at asperity contacts break the O–Zn and S–P bonds.
3. **Reaction with substrate:** The decomposition products react with iron to form a **polyphosphate glassy film** of approximate composition $\text{ZnFe-polyphosphate}$ (a mixture of $\text{FePO}_4$, $\text{Zn}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2$, and sulfide linkages).
4. **Film growth:** The film grows to a thickness of **50 – 150 nm**; it is sacrificial — it wears and re-forms continuously.
5. **Load bearing:** The film has a melting point exceeding **700 °C** and a low shear strength, so it carries the load without allowing metal-to-metal welding.

**Role of elements:**
- **Zn** — coordinates with the phosphate group; aids film formation.
- **P** — backbone of the polyphosphate glass; provides hardness.
- **S** — aids adhesion to Fe; provides anti-oxidant (secondary) action by decomposing peroxides.

**Film formation conditions:** Active only in the **boundary lubrication regime** ($\lambda < 1$) under sliding/rolling contact with appreciable asperity flash heating.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Using the derivation from Section 3.1:

$$\text{TBN}_{mix} = \dfrac{\text{TBN}_a \cdot m_a + \text{TBN}_b \cdot m_b}{m_a + m_b}$$

With $\text{TBN}_a = 380$ mg KOH/g, $\text{TBN}_b = 0$, $m_a = 2.8$ g, $m_b = 97.2$ g:

$$\text{TBN}_{mix} = \dfrac{380 \times 2.8 + 0}{2.8 + 97.2} = \dfrac{1064}{100.0} = 10.64 \;\text{mg KOH/g}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TBN}_{mix} \approx 10.6 \;\text{mg KOH/g}}$$

**Suitability for API CK-4 with high-sulfur fuel:**

API CK-4 specifies a minimum **TBN of 8 – 10 mg KOH/g** for typical high-sulfur (≥ 500 ppm) diesel operation. A TBN of 10.6 is **suitable** — it provides a good safety margin for acid neutralization. However, for very-high-sulfur fuels (> 2000 ppm) found in marine or certain developing-market diesel, a higher TBN (12 – 15) would be preferred, achievable by increasing the treat rate to about 3.5% – 4.0%.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Classify the major **types of lubricant additives** and explain the mechanism of action of **at least four** types in detail, with one industrial application example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel journal bearing operates at a contact pressure of **600 MPa**. Without friction modifier, the surface shear strength of the metal-metal junction is **120 MPa**. With a fatty-acid friction modifier forming a monolayer of shear strength **8 MPa**, compute the **friction coefficient in both cases** and the **percentage reduction in friction**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The classification and four selected types are as follows:

**1. Anti-Wear Additives (e.g., ZDDP)**
- **Mechanism:** Tribochemical film formation on Fe surfaces.
- **Application:** Engine oils (API SP, ILSAC GF-6).

**2. Extreme-Pressure Additives (e.g., Sulfurized Isobutylene)**
- **Mechanism:** Sacrificial FeS / FeP film formation at high flash temperatures.
- **Application:** Industrial gear oils, metal-cutting fluids.

**3. Viscosity Index Improvers (e.g., Polymethacrylate)**
- **Mechanism:** Polymer coils expand at high T, compensating for base-oil thinning.
- **Application:** Multi-grade engine oils (e.g., 5W-30, 0W-20).

**4. Detergents (e.g., Overbased Calcium Sulfonate)**
- **Mechanism:** Neutralize acidic combustion byproducts; suspend deposit precursors in colloidal micelles.
- **Application:** Heavy-duty diesel engine oils (API CK-4).

(Other categories may include dispersants, antioxidants, anti-foam, rust inhibitors, and PPDs — credit for any well-explained four.)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Using the derivation from Section 3.3:

**Without FM:**

$$\mu_{base} = \dfrac{\tau_m}{p} = \dfrac{120}{600} = 0.20$$

**With FM:**

$$\mu_{FM} = \dfrac{\tau_f}{p} = \dfrac{8}{600} \approx 0.0133$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\Delta \mu \, \% = \dfrac{\mu_{base} - \mu_{FM}}{\mu_{base}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.20 - 0.0133}{0.20} \times 100$$

$$= \dfrac{0.1867}{0.20} \times 100 \approx 93.3\%$$

$$\boxed{\mu_{base} = 0.20, \quad \mu_{FM} = 0.0133, \quad \Delta \mu \approx 93.3\%}$$

**Engineering insight:** A 93% friction reduction is realistic for a well-formulated FM in a journal bearing — this translates to substantial energy savings and temperature reduction, extending bearing life.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 3 — Additives and Their Functions**

- [ ] An **additive** is a performance-enhancing chemical blended into base oil, typically at **0.1% – 30%** concentration.
- [ ] **Anti-wear (ZDDP)** forms a **polyphosphate glassy film**; active in boundary regime at **200 – 600 °C** flash T.
- [ ] **EP additives** form **FeS / FeP films** at very high contact pressures — corrosive to **yellow metals**.
- [ ] **Friction modifiers** form **physically-adsorbed monolayers** — reduce $\mu$ by up to **90%**.
- [ ] **VII (polymethacrylate, OCP)** improves VI by polymer coil expansion with temperature.
- [ ] **PPD** co-crystallizes with **paraffin wax** to prevent gel network at low T.
- [ ] **Detergents** are **overbased** (TBN 100 – 400); neutralize acids via $\text{CaCO}_3$ reserve alkalinity.
- [ ] **Dispersants** are **ashless** (PIBSA-PAM); suspend soot, sludge, varnish precursors.
- [ ] **Antioxidants** are of two types: **primary** (radical scavengers — hindered phenols) and **secondary** (peroxide decomposers — ZDDP, phosphites).
- [ ] **Anti-foam** uses **PDMS silicone** at 1 – 50 ppm.
- [ ] **Rust inhibitors** form **hydrophobic films** — succinimide, fatty amines.
- [ ] **Demulsifiers** break **water-in-oil emulsions** — critical for steam-turbine and marine oils.
- [ ] The **lambda ratio** $\lambda$ dictates which additives are needed: $\lambda < 1 \Rightarrow$ boundary (AW, EP, FM); $\lambda > 3 \Rightarrow$ full-film (VII, AO, AF).
- [ ] **TBN equation:** $\text{TBN}_{mix} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot \text{TBN}_i}{\sum m_i}$.
- [ ] **Boundary friction:** $\mu_b = \tau_f / p$ — a 10× reduction in $\tau_f$ yields a 10× reduction in $\mu$.
- [ ] **Oxidation kinetics** follow a **logistic model**; antioxidants reduce $k_{ox}$ but cannot eliminate it.
- [ ] **ZDDP** is the single most multifunctional additive — anti-wear, secondary antioxidant, mild corrosion inhibitor.
- [ ] **Always quote the treat rate** (mass %) and the **regime of action** in your KTU answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Criteria for Selecting Lubricants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Criteria for Selecting Lubricants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lubricant Selection Criteria** is defined as the systematic, engineering-based process of evaluating operating parameters, environmental conditions, machine design constraints, and tribological performance requirements to identify the *optimum lubricant grade and chemistry* that ensures adequate film formation, minimum wear, controlled friction, heat dissipation, and acceptable service life for a given tribo-system.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET633 – Industrial Tribology, Module 3)**, the selection of a lubricant is **not** an arbitrary choice. It is a **decision matrix** that integrates:
1. **Operating Regime** (boundary, mixed, elastohydrodynamic, hydrodynamic).
2. **Physical-Chemical Stability** (oxidation, thermal degradation, contamination resistance).
3. **Machine & Component Geometry** (load, speed, surface finish, clearance).
4. **Service Environment** (temperature, dust, humidity, food-grade, vacuum).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Selecting a lubricant is identical to selecting the **correct cooking oil** for a recipe. 
> - *Deep-frying at 200 °C* → you need a **high smoke-point oil** (peanut/avocado) — analogous to a lubricant with **high flash & fire point**.
> - *Cold-storage salad dressing* → you need an oil that **does not solidify** — analogous to a lubricant with a **low pour point**.
> - *Stir-frying a delicate fish* → you need a **neutral-flavour oil** — analogous to a lubricant that does **not react** with seals or gaskets.
> - *Heavy dough kneading* → you need **high-viscosity oil** — analogous to a lubricant for **slow, heavily loaded gears**.

Just as a chef matches oil properties to the cooking conditions, a tribologist matches lubricant properties to the **tribo-system operating conditions**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Industry Standard)

The following **physical constants & standard metrics** govern lubricant selection globally:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu$ | $1$ – $1000$ mPa·s | ISO 3104 |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | $\nu$ | $2$ – $1500$ mm²/s | ISO 3104 |
| **Viscosity Index** | VI | $0$ – $200$ (mineral), $>200$ (synthetic) | ISO 2909 |
| **Flash Point** | $T_{fp}$ | $150$ – $320$ °C | ISO 2592 |
| **Pour Point** | $T_{pp}$ | $-60$ to $+10$ °C | ISO 3016 |
| **Density (15 °C)** | $\rho$ | $850$ – $990$ kg/m³ | ISO 12185 |

## 1.4 Why Selection is Critical — The Engineering Consequence

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A *wrong lubricant* does not fail immediately. It fails **catastrophically after 2000–8000 hours** of operation, causing:
> - **Premature bearing fatigue** (spalling, brinelling).
> - **Scuffing & welding** of gear flanks.
> - **Increased parasitic friction** (energy loss, heat).
> - **Seal hardening & leakage** (compatibility failure).
> - **Coking & varnish deposition** in hydraulic valves (oxidation failure).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Lubricant Selection

The selection of a lubricant in any **KTU 2024 Scheme exam answer** must be presented as a five-pillar decision framework:

### Pillar 1 — Operating Condition Parameters
- **Load ($W$ or $P$)**: Determines required **film strength** & extreme-pressure (EP) additives.
- **Speed ($N$ or $v$)**: Determines required **viscosity** via the dimensionless $pv$ factor.
- **Temperature ($T$)**: Determines **viscosity-temperature stability** (VI) & pour point.
- **Environment ($E$)**: Humidity, dust, vacuum, radiation, chemical exposure.

### Pillar 2 — Machine Design Geometry
- **Bearing type**: Journal, ball, roller, thrust, tapered.
- **Surface roughness ($R_a$)**: Stribeck curve placement.
- **Clearance ($c$)**: Sommerfeld number dependency.
- **Seal material**: Fluoroelastomer (FKM), nitrile (NBR) compatibility.

### Pillar 3 — Lubricant Physical Properties
- **Viscosity at operating temperature** (most critical).
- **Viscosity Index (VI)** — high VI = stable film across temperature swings.
- **Flash & fire points** — thermal safety.
- **Pour point** — cold-start protection.
- **Neutralization number (TAN/TBN)** — acid control.

### Pillar 4 — Chemical Stability & Compatibility
- **Oxidation stability** (RBOT, TOST tests).
- **Hydrolytic stability** (water contamination resistance).
- **Demulsibility** (water-separation ability).
- **Additive package**: Anti-wear (AW), extreme-pressure (EP), anti-foam, rust inhibitor.

### Pillar 5 — Service Life & Maintenance Economics
- **Lubricant life expectancy** (in hours).
- **Relubrication interval** (calendar vs. operating hours).
- **Disposal classification** (mineral vs. biodegradable).

## 2.2 The Step-by-Step Logical Selection Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> The **standard KTU-recommended algorithm** for selecting a lubricant is:

1. **Step 1** → Identify the *required viscosity at operating temperature* $\nu_{40}$ using the **speed factor** $K_s$.
2. **Step 2** → Determine the *required additive package* (AW / EP / anti-oxidant) from the *load factor* $K_l$.
3. **Step 3** → Check *temperature extremes* to set minimum flash point & maximum pour point.
4. **Step 4** → Verify *compatibility* with seals, paints, and existing residual lubricants.
5. **Step 5** → Match with an **ISO VG grade** from ISO 3448 standard.
6. **Step 6** → Validate *service life & cost* against maintenance schedule.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Selection Equations

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nu_{40} = K_s \cdot K_l$ | Required kinematic viscosity at 40 °C | mm²/s |
| 2 | $K_s = \frac{1}{N}$ (rolling) | Speed factor (rolling-element bearings) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K_s = \frac{10^7}{N}$ (journal) | Speed factor (journal bearings) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $K_l = 10^{0.0046P-1.5}$ | Load factor | $P$ in MPa |
| 5 | $\nu_T = \nu_{40} \cdot 10^{\alpha(40-T)}$ | ASTM D341 viscosity-temperature | $\alpha$ = VI-dependent |
| 6 | $S = \frac{r}{c}\cdot\frac{\mu N}{P}$ | Sommerfeld number (hydrodynamic) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{min} = 2.65 \cdot R_q \cdot \Lambda$ | Minimum film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson) | µm |
| 8 | $\Lambda = \frac{h_{min}}{\sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}}$ | Lambda ratio — film-to-roughness | dimensionless |
| 9 | $h_c = 0.11 \cdot \nu^{0.68} \cdot R^{0.43} \cdot W^{-0.066}$ | Central EHL film thickness (EHL) | m |
| 10 | $P_f = \frac{W \cdot v}{A}$ | Specific loading / pv-factor | MPa·m/s |
| 11 | $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ | Newtonian shear stress (Newton's law) | Pa |
| 12 | $F_f = \tau \cdot A_c$ | Friction force in lubricated contact | N |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in formulas use $\vert x \vert$ or vertical notation to keep markdown tables valid.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace turbines** → synthetic PAO + ester blends with VI $>150$, flash $>240$ °C.
- **Wind turbine gearboxes** → ISO VG 320 mineral + EP additives, pour point $<-30$ °C.
- **Food industry** → NSF H1 certified PAO, white mineral oils, H1 additive package.
- **Marine stern tubes** → biodegradable EAL (Environmentally Acceptable Lubricant) per EPA 2013 VGP.
- **Hydraulic systems** → ISO VG 32–68 with high VI and demulsibility for servo valves.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Viscosity-Temperature Relationship (Walther Equation)

The **ASTM D341 / Walther equation** relates kinematic viscosity at two temperatures:

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \cdot \log T$$

**Step-by-step derivation from two known points ($\nu_1$ at $T_1$, $\nu_2$ at $T_2$):**

$$\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{\log \log(\nu_1 + 0.7) - \log \log(\nu_2 + 0.7)}{\log T_2 - \log T_1} \\[6pt]
A &= \log \log(\nu_1 + 0.7) + B \cdot \log T_1 \\[6pt]
\nu_T &= 10^{10^{A - B \log T}} - 0.7
\end{aligned}$$

> **Explanation:** The double-log transform linearizes the otherwise non-linear viscosity-temperature curve, enabling engineers to **predict viscosity at any operating temperature** from two lab measurements at 40 °C and 100 °C.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Viscosity at Operating Temperature

Given a **journal bearing** with $D = 100$ mm, $N = 1500$ rpm, $P = 2$ MPa, find required $\nu_{40}$.

**Step 1 — Speed factor for journal bearing:**

$$K_s = \frac{10^7}{N} = \frac{10^7}{1500} = 6667$$

**Step 2 — Load factor:**

$$K_l = 10^{0.0046 \cdot P - 1.5} = 10^{0.0046 \cdot 2 - 1.5} = 10^{-1.4908} = 0.0323$$

**Step 3 — Required viscosity at 40 °C:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{40} &= K_s \cdot K_l \\[4pt]
&= 6667 \cdot 0.0323 \\[4pt]
&= 215.3 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Round to nearest ISO VG grade:**

> From **ISO 3448 standard**, the closest ISO VG grade is **ISO VG 220** (range 198–242 mm²/s at 40 °C).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Film Thickness (Hamrock-Dowson EHL)

For an **elliptical contact** (ball bearing):

$$h_{min} = 2.65 \cdot R_q \cdot \Lambda \quad \text{where} \quad R_q = \sqrt{R_{q1}^2 + R_{q2}^2}$$

**Rewriting in terms of operating parameters:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{min} &= 2.65 \cdot R_q \cdot \Lambda \\[4pt]
\Lambda &= \frac{h_{min}}{R_q} \\[4pt]
h_c &= 3.63 \cdot R_x \cdot U^{0.68} \cdot G^{0.49} \cdot W^{-0.073} \quad \text{(central EHL)} \\[4pt]
U &= \frac{\eta_0 \cdot u_e}{E' \cdot R_x} \quad ; \quad G = \alpha \cdot E' \quad ; \quad W = \frac{w}{E' \cdot R_x^2}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Where:** $R_x$ = effective radius (m), $U$ = speed parameter, $G$ = material parameter, $W$ = load parameter, $\eta_0$ = atmospheric viscosity, $u_e$ = entraining velocity, $E'$ = effective elastic modulus, $\alpha$ = pressure-viscosity coefficient.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Lambda Ratio Selection Check

**Given:** Ball bearing, $R_q = 0.4$ µm, ISO VG 68 oil at 80 °C, $U = 0.5 \times 10^{-10}$, $W = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, $R_x = 5$ mm.

**Step 1 — Calculate central EHL film thickness:**

$$h_c = 3.63 \cdot R_x \cdot U^{0.68} \cdot G^{0.49} \cdot W^{-0.073}$$

Assume $G = 5000$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U^{0.68} &= (0.5 \times 10^{-10})^{0.68} = 1.86 \times 10^{-7} \\[4pt]
G^{0.49} &= 5000^{0.49} = 56.6 \\[4pt]
W^{-0.073} &= (2 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.073} = 2.41 \\[4pt]
h_c &= 3.63 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 1.86 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 56.6 \cdot 2.41 \\[4pt]
h_c &= 4.61 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.461 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Lambda ratio:**

$$\Lambda = \frac{h_c}{R_q} = \frac{0.461}{0.4} = 1.15$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $\Lambda < 1$ → **Boundary lubrication** (high wear, NOT acceptable).
> - $1 < \Lambda < 3$ → **Mixed lubrication** (acceptable with AW/EP additives).
> - $\Lambda > 3$ → **Full-film EHL** (ideal, no wear).
>
> **Conclusion:** A higher viscosity grade (e.g., ISO VG 100) is required to achieve $\Lambda > 3$.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Property vs. Application

| Application | Load | Speed | Temp | Recommended Viscosity | Additive Package | VI Required |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electric motor bearings** | Low | High ($>3000$ rpm) | 60–90 °C | ISO VG 32–46 | AO + AW | $>95$ |
| **Wind turbine main gearbox** | Very high | Low ($<100$ rpm) | 60–80 °C | ISO VG 320–460 | EP + AW + Rust | $>150$ |
| **Hydraulic servo systems** | Medium | Medium | 50–70 °C | ISO VG 32–46 | AO + AW + Antifoam | $>180$ |
| **Paper machine dryer bearings** | Medium | High | 100–150 °C | ISO VG 100–220 | EP + AO + Detergent | $>95$ |
| **Marine stern tube (EAL)** | High | Very low | 10–50 °C | ISO VG 68–100 | AW + Biodegradable | $>180$ |
| **Precision machine tool spindles** | Light | Very high ($>20000$ rpm) | 50–80 °C | ISO VG 10–22 | AO + AW + Antifoam | $>200$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Lubricant Selection Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Tribo-System] --> B[Determine Operating Parameters]
    B --> B1[Load: P in MPa]
    B --> B2[Speed: N in rpm]
    B --> B3[Temperature: T in deg C]
    B1 --> C[Compute Speed Factor Ks]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Compute Load Factor Kl]
    D --> E[Calculate Required nu at 40 deg C]
    E --> F[Match ISO VG Grade per ISO 3448]
    F --> G[Check Pour Point vs Min T]
    F --> H[Check Flash Point vs Max T]
    G --> I{All Checks Pass?}
    H --> I
    I -->|Yes| J[Select Additive Package]
    I -->|No| K[Re-evaluate Viscosity or Base Stock]
    K --> C
    J --> L[AW: Anti Wear]
    J --> M[EP: Extreme Pressure]
    J --> N[AO: Anti Oxidant]
    J --> O[Rust and Foam Inhibitors]
    L --> P[Validate with Lambda Ratio Test]
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Lambda greater than 1?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[APPROVED LUBRICANT]
    Q -->|No| S[Increase Viscosity or Surface Finish]
    S --> P
```

## 4.2 Property Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Base Stock] --> P2[Mineral]
    P1 --> P3[Synthetic]
    P3 --> P4[PAO]
    P3 --> P5[ Ester]
    P3 --> P6[PAG]
    P3 --> P7[Silicone]
    P1 --> P8[Bio-based]
    P2 --> A1[Additive Package]
    P3 --> A1
    P4 --> A1
    P5 --> A1
    P6 --> A1
    P7 --> A1
    P8 --> A1
    A1 --> AD1[Anti Wear AW]
    A1 --> AD2[Extreme Pressure EP]
    A1 --> AD3[Anti Oxidant AO]
    A1 --> AD4[Anti Foam AF]
    A1 --> AD5[Rust Inhibitor RI]
    A1 --> AD6[Detergent and Dispersant]
    AD1 --> Q[Final Lubricant Grade]
    AD2 --> Q
    AD3 --> Q
    AD4 --> Q
    AD5 --> Q
    AD6 --> Q
```

## 4.3 Tribo-System Selection Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Input: Application Type] --> T2{Bearing Type?}
    T2 -->|Journal| J[Journal Bearing Selection]
    T2 -->|Ball| B[Rolling Element Selection]
    T2 -->|Gear| G[Gear Lubrication]
    J --> J1[Check Sommerfeld Number S]
    J1 --> J2[Verify Film Thickness h]
    B --> B1[Check Lambda Ratio]
    B1 --> B2[Verify EHL Film]
    G --> G1[Check FZG Scuffing Load]
    G1 --> G2[Verify AGMA Grade]
    J2 --> X[Final Recommendation]
    B2 --> X
    G2 --> X
    X --> Y[ISO VG Grade Output]
    X --> Z[Additive Package Output]
    X --> W[Relubrication Interval Output]
```

## 4.4 Lubrication Regime Map (Stribeck Curve Conceptualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Boundary Lubrication] --> R1A[High Friction, High Wear]
    R1A --> R1B[Lambda less than 1]
    R2[Mixed Lubrication] --> R2A[Moderate Friction, AW/EP Needed]
    R2A --> R2B[Lambda between 1 and 3]
    R3[Full Film EHL] --> R3A[Low Friction, Minimal Wear]
    R3A --> R3B[Lambda greater than 3]
    R1B --> R4[Selection Goal: Move Right]
    R2B --> R4
    R3B --> R4
    R4 --> R5[Increase Viscosity or Reduce Roughness]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List the primary criteria for selecting a lubricant for an industrial gear box."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The primary criteria for selecting a lubricant for an industrial gearbox are:
1. **Viscosity at operating temperature** — determined by the speed factor $K_s$ and load factor $K_l$.
2. **Viscosity Index (VI)** — ensures stable film thickness across the operating temperature range.
3. **Load-carrying capacity** — Extreme Pressure (EP) additives required for high contact pressures (typically $P > 1$ GPa in gear contacts).
4. **Flash point & pour point** — safety margins for maximum and minimum service temperatures.
5. **Compatibility with seals** — typically FKM or NBR elastomer compatibility.
6. **Foaming resistance & demulsibility** — for reliable hydraulic action and water separation.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define Viscosity Index (VI) and state its significance in lubricant selection."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical, dimensionless number (per **ISO 2909**) that indicates the rate of change of a lubricant's viscosity with temperature, relative to a reference oil (VI = 100, Pennsylvania crude) and a low-VI reference (VI = 0, Gulf Coast crude).

**Significance in lubricant selection:**
- **High VI ($>150$)** → lubricant retains viscosity at high temperature (resists thinning) and remains fluid at low temperature (resists thickening) → wider operating temperature envelope.
- **Low VI ($<50$)** → unsuitable for variable-temperature service (e.g., outdoor machinery, aerospace).
- For applications with $\Delta T > 50$ °C, a **high-VI oil or synthetic (PAO/ester)** is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *Explain in detail the **five major criteria** that govern the selection of a lubricant for a given tribo-system. (7 marks)

**(b)** *A journal bearing has the following specifications: Diameter $D = 80$ mm, Length $L = 120$ mm, Speed $N = 1800$ rpm, Load $W = 25$ kN, Operating temperature $T = 75$ °C. Determine the required ISO VG grade of the lubricant. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

The **five major criteria** for lubricant selection are:

1. **Viscosity Requirement (Most Critical)** — Determined by the speed factor ($K_s = 10^7/N$ for journal bearings) and load factor ($K_l = 10^{0.0046P-1.5}$). The product $K_s \cdot K_l$ gives the required kinematic viscosity at 40 °C, which is matched to the nearest ISO VG grade per **ISO 3448**.

2. **Operating Temperature Range** — Sets the constraints on **pour point** (must be at least 5–10 °C below minimum operating temperature) and **flash point** (must be 50–100 °C above maximum operating temperature for safety).

3. **Additive Package** — Determined by load severity: **Anti-Wear (AW)** for moderate loads, **Extreme-Pressure (EP)** for high loads with sliding contact, **Anti-Oxidant (AO)** for high-temperature service, and **Rust/Foam inhibitors** for humid/contaminated environments.

4. **Compatibility** — Chemical compatibility with **seals (FKM/NBR)**, **paints**, **other lubricants** (residual from previous fill), and **metals** (copper, silver alloy bearings).

5. **Service Life & Environmental Compliance** — Defined by **TOST (Turbine Oil Stability Test)** oxidation life, **biodegradability** (EPA VGP for marine), **food-grade certification (NSF H1)** for food machinery.

> **[7 marks breakdown: 1.4 marks × 5 criteria, plus diagram = 7]**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute bearing pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.120 \cdot 0.080} \\[4pt]
P &= 2.604 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Speed factor for journal bearing:**

$$K_s = \frac{10^7}{N} = \frac{10^7}{1800} = 5555.6$$

**Step 3 — Load factor:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_l &= 10^{0.0046 \cdot P - 1.5} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{0.0046 \cdot 2.604 - 1.5} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{0.01198 - 1.5} \\[4pt]
&= 10^{-1.4880} = 0.0325
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Required viscosity at 40 °C:**

$$\nu_{40} = K_s \cdot K_l = 5555.6 \cdot 0.0325 = 180.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Round to nearest ISO VG grade per ISO 3448:**

> Closest grade: **ISO VG 150** (range: 135–165 mm²/s) or **ISO VG 220** (range: 198–242 mm²/s). The calculated value 180.6 falls **between**; in practice, the higher **ISO VG 220** is selected for safety margin at higher operating temperature (75 °C), where viscosity drops by 50–60%.

**Step 6 — Viscosity correction for 75 °C operating temperature (assume VI = 95):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu_{75} &\approx \nu_{40} \cdot \left(\frac{40}{75}\right)^{0.7} \\[4pt]
&\approx 180.6 \cdot (0.533)^{0.7} \\[4pt]
&\approx 180.6 \cdot 0.633 \approx 114.3 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Recommended: ISO VG 220 with VI $>95$ and AW additives.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a)** *Discuss the role of **lubricant additives** in the selection criteria. Explain the functions of **AW, EP, AO, and anti-foam** additives with one example each. (7 marks)

**(b)** *A rolling-element bearing operates at $N = 3000$ rpm, under radial load $W = 8$ kN, with effective radius $R_x = 6$ mm, surface roughness $R_q = 0.5$ µm, and entraining velocity $u_e = 5$ m/s. Using the Hamrock-Dowson equation, check whether ISO VG 100 oil (at 70 °C, $\eta_0 = 18$ mPa·s) provides adequate lubrication. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

Lubricant additives are **chemical compounds blended with the base stock** (typically 0.1–25% by mass) to enhance properties that the base oil cannot provide alone. Their role in **selection criteria** is to extend the lubricant's performance envelope beyond the base oil's natural capabilities.

| Additive | Function | Typical Chemistry | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anti-Wear (AW)** | Forms a sacrificial phosphate/borate film on metal surfaces under mixed lubrication | **ZDDP** (Zinc Dialkyldithiophosphate) | Engine cam-follower, hydraulic pump vane |
| **Extreme-Pressure (EP)** | Reacts with metal at high temperature to form iron-sulfide/iron-chloride low-shear film | **Sulfurised olefins, chlorinated paraffins** | Hypoid gears, warm rolling mills |
| **Anti-Oxidant (AO)** | Scavenges peroxides & free radicals, extends service life | **Hindered phenols, aromatic amines** | Turbine oil, transformer oil |
| **Anti-Foam** | Reduces surface tension of air bubbles, prevents foam-induced cavitation | **Silicone polymers (PDMS), polyacrylates** | Hydraulic reservoirs, high-speed gearboxes |

> **Selection rule:** EP additives are required when **contact pressure > 1 GPa AND sliding component present**; AW additives are sufficient for **$< 1$ GPa with rolling contact**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $N = 3000$ rpm, $W = 8$ kN, $R_x = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m, $R_q = 0.5$ µm, $u_e = 5$ m/s, $\eta_0 = 18$ mPa·s $= 0.018$ Pa·s, $T = 70$ °C, $E' = 2.3 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Compute dimensionless parameters:**

$$\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\eta_0 \cdot u_e}{E' \cdot R_x} = \frac{0.018 \cdot 5}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \cdot 0.006} = 6.52 \times 10^{-11} \\[4pt]
G &= \alpha \cdot E' = 1.5 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 2.3 \times 10^{11} = 3450 \\[4pt]
W_d &= \frac{w}{E' \cdot R_x^2} = \frac{8000}{2.3 \times 10^{11} \cdot (0.006)^2} = 9.66 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hamrock-Dowson central film equation:**

$$h_c = 2.69 \cdot R_x \cdot U^{0.67} \cdot G^{0.53} \cdot W_d^{-0.067}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
U^{0.67} &= (6.52 \times 10^{-11})^{0.67} = 5.28 \times 10^{-7} \\[4pt]
G^{0.53} &= 3450^{0.53} = 56.5 \\[4pt]
W_d^{-0.067} &= (9.66 \times 10^{-4})^{-0.067} = 2.40 \\[4pt]
h_c &= 2.69 \cdot 0.006 \cdot 5.28 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 56.5 \cdot 2.40 \\[4pt]
h_c &= 1.15 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.115 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Lambda ratio:**

$$\Lambda = \frac{h_c}{R_q} = \frac{0.115}{0.5} = 0.23$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often **forget to take the square root of $R_q$ for combined roughness** or **use the wrong viscosity units (cP vs Pa·s)**.
>
> **Conversion check:** $1$ mPa·s $= 10^{-3}$ Pa·s ✓

| Lambda Value | Regime | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|
| $\Lambda = 0.23$ | **Boundary** | NOT acceptable. **ISO VG 100 oil is insufficient.** |

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**
- Increase viscosity to **ISO VG 220 or higher**, OR
- Improve surface finish to $R_q < 0.2$ µm, OR
- Add **EP/AW additive package** to handle boundary contact.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Criteria for Selecting Lubricants" questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting viscosity-temperature correction** — selecting ISO VG based only on 40 °C without checking viscosity at *actual operating temperature* (often 60–100 °C) → film thickness collapse in exams.
> 2. **Ignoring the dimensionless $\Lambda$ ratio** — examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for verifying that $\Lambda > 1$ (or $>3$ for full film).
> 3. **Confusing AW and EP additives** — AW is for mild wear, EP is for high-pressure sliding contacts; mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of base stock** (mineral vs synthetic) — always state the *reason* (cost, VI, biodegradability, food-grade).
> 5. **Skipping the units check** — $1$ cP $= 1$ mPa·s; $1$ mm²/s $= 1$ cSt; mis-conversion is a 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Omitting the pour-point margin** — pour point must be 5–10 °C **below** minimum operating temperature, not equal to it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Viscosity at operating temperature is the #1 selection parameter** — never select lubricant based on 40 °C viscosity alone.
- ⭐ **Speed factor** $K_s = 10^7/N$ for journal; $K_s = 1/N$ for rolling-element.
- ⭐ **Load factor** $K_l = 10^{0.0046P - 1.5}$ where $P$ is in MPa.
- ⭐ **ISO 3448** defines ISO VG grades: 2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 22, 32, 46, 68, 100, 150, 220, 320, 460, 680, 1000, 1500.
- ⭐ **Lambda ratio $\Lambda > 3$** → full-film EHL; **$1 < \Lambda < 3$** → mixed (additives needed); **$\Lambda < 1$** → boundary (high wear).
- ⭐ **Sommerfeld number** $S = (r/c)(\mu N/P)$ governs hydrodynamic journal bearing performance.
- ⭐ **Additives**: AW (ZDDP) for mild wear; EP (S/Cl/P) for high-pressure sliding; AO (phenols/amines) for oxidation; Anti-foam (silicone) for cavitation prevention.
- ⭐ **Pour point** must be 5–10 °C below minimum service temperature; **Flash point** must be 50–100 °C above maximum.
- ⭐ **Base stock hierarchy (cost vs. performance):** Mineral → Hydrocracked → PAO → Ester → PAG → Silicone.
- ⭐ **Hamrock-Dowson** is the *standard* EHL film thickness equation for KTU exams.
- ⭐ **Relubrication interval** depends on: temperature, contamination, load, and oil type (synthetic lasts 3–5× longer than mineral).
- ⭐ **Viscosity Index (VI)**: $>150$ is considered high-VI (suitable for variable-temperature service).
- ⭐ **For biodegradable applications** → use **EAL** (Environmentally Acceptable Lubricant) per EPA 2013 VGP — vegetable oils, synthetic esters, PAG.
- ⭐ **For food-grade** → use **NSF H1 registered** oils; non-toxic, odorless, tasteless.
- ⭐ **Final selection workflow**: Identify parameters → Compute $K_s$, $K_l$ → Calculate $\nu_{40}$ → Match ISO VG → Verify $\Lambda$ ratio → Select additive package → Check compatibility → Approve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Treatments and Coatings: Heat Treatments, Surface Hardening, and Nitriding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Surface Treatments and Coatings: Heat Treatments, Surface Hardening, and Nitriding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Surface Treatment & Coating — The Tribological Perspective

**Surface treatment** refers to a family of metallurgical, thermochemical, and physical processes deliberately applied to the outermost layer of a component to engineer its microstructure, residual stress state, hardness profile, and chemical composition — without altering the bulk core properties. In tribology, this is the *first line of defense* against the three classical failure modes: **adhesive wear, abrasive wear, and contact fatigue**.

**Surface coating**, by contrast, is the deliberate deposition (or growth) of an entirely *foreign* material layer — metallic, ceramic, polymeric, or composite — onto the substrate to create a functional tribo-surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Surface engineering encompasses all processes that alter the near-surface chemistry, microstructure, or topography of a component to reduce wear, lower friction, and inhibit corrosion — thereby extending service life under tribological contact."

## 1.2 The Engineering Intuition — Why Treat the Surface?

A load-bearing component in a tribosystem is rarely *fully stressed*. In rolling/sliding contact, the **Hertzian contact stress** decays exponentially with depth, and over **90% of the contact load** is carried by the top **0.1–1.0 mm** of material.

**Conceptual Analogy (The "Armored Knight" Analogy):**
Imagine a medieval knight. His skeleton (the bulk core) gives him strength and rigidity, but it is the **armor plate on the outside** that survives battle. In a gear, cam, or bearing race:
- **Core** = tough, ductile, fatigue-resistant (shock absorber)
- **Surface** = hard, wear-resistant, low-friction (armor)

A through-hardened component is like a knight in *solid steel plate* — heavy, brittle, and prone to catastrophic fracture. A **case-hardened or coated component** is like a knight in *light, flexible chainmail with hardened plate* — tough inside, hard outside, optimized for the contact load distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Philosophy:** Bulk properties handle *static and bulk-dynamic loads*; surface properties handle *contact loads*. The art of surface engineering is to **decouple** these two design requirements.

## 1.3 Classification of Surface Engineering Processes (KTU Module 4)

The KTU 2024 scheme categorizes the techniques under three primary umbrellas:

| Category | Core Mechanism | Representative Processes |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional Heat Treatments** | Phase transformation via heating/cooling | Annealing, Normalizing, Hardening, Tempering |
| **Surface Hardening** | Localized phase transformation | Induction, Flame, Laser, Electron-beam hardening |
| **Thermochemical Diffusion Treatments** | Diffusion of foreign atoms | Carburizing, **Nitriding**, Carbonitriding, Boriding |
| **Surface Coatings** | Deposition of foreign material | PVD, CVD, Thermal Spray, DLC |

**Critical Tribological Constants to Remember:**

- **Hardness (H):** measured in **HV (Vickers)** or **HRC (Rockwell C)** — units must be quoted.
- **Effective case depth:** depth where hardness falls to **550 HV** (per **ISO 18203**).
- **Total case depth:** depth of measurable microstructural change.
- **Nitriding temperature:** typically **500–550 °C** — *below* the Fe–N eutectoid (591 °C), preserving core properties.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question specifies "improved wear resistance *without distortion*," the intended answer is almost always **nitriding** — because it is a *sub-eutectoid* diffusion process with minimal dimensional change.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conventional Heat Treatments — The Phase-Transformation Foundation

Heat treatments manipulate the **Fe–Fe₃C (Iron–Iron Carbide) phase diagram**. For a hypoeutectoid steel (e.g., AISI 1045, 4140), the relevant phase boundaries are:

- **A₁ (Eutectoid temperature):** **727 °C** — start of austenite (γ-FCC) formation from pearlite.
- **A₃:** upper critical temperature — austenite finish on heating for hypoeutectoid steels.
- **A_cm:** upper critical temperature for hypereutectoid steels.
- **M_s:** **Martensite-start temperature** (typically **200–300 °C** for medium-carbon steels).
- **M_f:** **Martensite-finish temperature** (often near or below room temperature).

### 2.1.1 The Four Classical Heat Treatments

**(a) Annealing**
- Heat to **A₃ + 30–50 °C**, hold, then **furnace cool**.
- Result: coarse pearlite + ferrite, **low hardness (≈ 150–200 HB)**, **maximum ductility**, **residual stresses eliminated**.
- *Tribological use:* Pre-machining stress relief, dimensional stabilization.

**(b) Normalizing**
- Heat to **A₃ + 30–50 °C**, hold, then **air cool**.
- Result: **finer pearlite + ferrite**, **moderate hardness (≈ 200–250 HB)**, refined grain.
- *Tribological use:* Refines forging/casting grain structure prior to machining.

**(c) Quenching (Hardening)**
- Heat to **A₃ + 30–50 °C**, hold for full austenitization, then rapidly cool in **water, oil, or polymer (PAG) quenchant**.
- Result: **Martensite** (BCT structure) — extremely hard (up to **65 HRC**) but **brittle**.
- *Tribological use:* Through-hardening for cutting tools, dies, bearings — but always followed by tempering.

**(d) Tempering**
- Reheat quenched steel to **150–650 °C**, hold, then cool.
- Result: Precipitation of **fine carbides (ε-carbide → cementite)** from martensite — **toughness restored, hardness slightly reduced**.
- *Tribological use:* Critical for any hardened tribo-component to prevent brittle spalling under Hertzian contact.

## 2.2 Surface Hardening — Localized Transformation

Surface hardening heats **only the outer layer** above A₃, then quenches. The bulk core remains tough.

### 2.2.1 Induction Hardening
- **Frequency range:** 1 kHz to 1 MHz (audio, medium, high frequency).
- **Skin depth (δ):** the depth to which 86.5% of eddy-current energy is deposited.

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \cdot \mu \cdot f}} \quad [\text{m}]
$$

where $\rho$ = electrical resistivity [Ω·m], $\mu$ = magnetic permeability [H/m], $f$ = frequency [Hz].

- **Higher frequency → shallower case** (e.g., 1 MHz → ≈ 0.5 mm; 10 kHz → ≈ 5 mm).
- *Tribological application:* Gear teeth, crankshaft journals, cam lobes.

### 2.2.2 Flame Hardening
- Oxy-acetylene or oxy-propane flame rapidly heats surface; quench follows.
- **Hardness depth:** 1–6 mm; **hardness:** 55–62 HRC.
- *Tribological application:* Large gears, mill rolls, rail tracks.

### 2.2.3 Laser / Electron Beam Hardening
- **Scanning speed (v)**, **beam power (P)**, and **absorptivity (A)** determine case depth:

$$
d_{\text{case}} \approx \frac{A \cdot P}{v \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot (T_A - T_0)} \quad [\text{m}]
$$

where $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat, $T_A$ = austenitization temperature, $T_0$ = initial temperature.

- **Self-quenching** by the cold substrate — no external quenchant needed.
- *Tribological application:* Selective hardening of valve seats, gear flanks with minimal distortion.

## 2.3 Nitriding — The Tribologist's Gold Standard for Distortion-Free Hardening

Nitriding is a **thermochemical diffusion process** in which **atomic nitrogen** diffuses into the steel surface at **500–550 °C** (sub-eutectoid) to form:
1. A thin, ultra-hard **compound layer (white layer)** of **ε-Fe₂₃N and γ′-Fe₄N** at the top.
2. A deeper **diffusion zone** of **α-Fe with coherent nitride precipitates (CrN, AlN, MoN)** that block dislocation motion.

### 2.3.1 Why Nitriding Dominates Tribology
- **No phase transformation** of the core → **near-zero distortion** (≈ 0.005 mm/mm).
- **Surface hardness:** 800–1200 HV (vs 60–65 HRC ≈ 800–900 HV for through-hardened steel).
- **Retained hardness at elevated temperature** (up to 500 °C) — ideal for **hot-working dies, gears in oil-lubricated service**.
- **High compressive residual stress** at the surface → **exceptional fatigue life**.

### 2.3.2 The Three Industrial Nitriding Routes

| Method | Medium | Case Depth | Surface Hardness | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas Nitriding** | NH₃ gas (dissociates → N + H₂) | 0.2–0.8 mm | 800–1100 HV | Most common, large furnaces |
| **Plasma (Ion) Nitriding** | Glow-discharge N₂/H₂ plasma | 0.1–0.6 mm | 900–1200 HV | Lowest distortion, fastest, mask-free control |
| **Salt-Bath Nitriding** | Cyanide–cyanate melt (e.g., Tenifer®) | 0.005–0.05 mm | 1000–1200 HV | Thin, very hard layer; environmental concerns |

### 2.3.3 Fick's Laws Governing the Nitriding Depth

The nitrogen concentration profile follows **Fick's second law of diffusion**:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}
$$

For a semi-infinite solid with constant surface concentration $C_s$, the solution is:

$$
\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)
$$

The **effective case depth** (where N concentration falls to a critical value) scales as:

$$
x \;\propto\; \sqrt{D \cdot t}
$$

The **diffusion coefficient** follows an **Arrhenius relation**:

$$
D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

where $D_0$ ≈ **0.0047 cm²/s** for N in α-Fe (low-alloy steel regime), $Q$ ≈ **77 kJ/mol** (activation energy), $R$ = **8.314 J/mol·K**, $T$ = absolute temperature [K].

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Doubling the *time* increases case depth by only **√2 ≈ 1.414×**, but raising the *temperature* from 500 °C to 550 °C (just +50 °C) roughly **doubles** the diffusion coefficient — temperature is far more effective than time.

## 2.4 Surface Coatings — The Modern Tribological Arsenal

| Coating Type | Process | Hardness | Friction Coef. (μ) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TiN** (Titanium Nitride) | PVD (arc/sputter) | 2300 HV | 0.4–0.5 | Cutting tools, drills |
| **CrN** (Chromium Nitride) | PVD | 1800 HV | 0.3–0.4 | Plastic molds, dies |
| **DLC** (Diamond-Like Carbon) | PECVD, PLD | 1500–9000 HV | **0.05–0.15** | Engine components, bearings |
| **WC–Co** (Tungsten Carbide) | Thermal Spray (HVOF) | 1100–1400 HV | 0.3–0.4 | Landing gear, hydraulic rods |
| **Al₂O₃** (Alumina) | Plasma Spray | 900–1100 HV | 0.6–0.8 | Thermal barrier top coats |

**Archard's Wear Equation** governs the lifetime of any coated or hardened surface:

$$
V = \frac{k \cdot W \cdot L}{H}
$$

where $V$ = wear volume, $k$ = dimensionless **wear coefficient**, $W$ = normal load, $L$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness of the *softer* contacting surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness doubles → wear volume halves.** This is the fundamental justification for the entire surface-engineering industry.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/(\pi\mu f)}$ | Skin depth of induction heating | m | Induction case depth |
| $x \propto \sqrt{Dt}$ | Diffusion depth | m | Nitriding, carburizing |
| $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ | Arrhenius diffusion | m²/s | Temperature effect |
| $V = kWL/H$ | Archard wear | m³ | All wear calculations |
| $M_s \approx 500 - 350 \cdot \%C$ | Martensite start (approx.) | °C | Quench design |
| $H_{\text{eff}} = H_{\text{sub}} + H_{\text{coat}}$ | Composite hardness (rule of mixtures) | HV | Coated systems |
| $\sigma_{\text{res}} \approx -E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Residual stress from quench | Pa | Distortion analysis |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fick's Second-Law Solution for Nitriding Case Depth

**Starting Point:** Fick's second law of diffusion in one dimension, with diffusivity $D$ assumed independent of concentration.

$$
\frac{\partial C(x,t)}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C(x,t)}{\partial x^2}
$$

**Initial Condition (IC):** Before nitriding, nitrogen concentration is uniform at the bulk value $C_0$:

$$
C(x,0) = C_0 \quad \text{for all } x \geq 0
$$

**Boundary Condition (BC):** The gas/plasma maintains a constant surface concentration $C_s$ at $x = 0$ throughout the process (assuming the surface reaction is fast relative to bulk diffusion):

$$
C(0,t) = C_s \quad \text{for all } t \geq 0
$$

**Semi-infinite Domain:** The substrate is treated as semi-infinite, since the case depth is negligible compared to component thickness:

$$
\lim_{x \to \infty} C(x,t) = C_0
$$

**Step 1 — Non-Dimensionalization:**
Define the dimensionless concentration and similarity variable:

$$
\theta = \frac{C - C_0}{C_s - C_0}, \qquad \eta = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Chain Rule:**

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial \eta}{\partial t} = -\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial x} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{Dt}} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Fick's Law:**

$$
-\frac{\eta}{2t} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = D \cdot \frac{1}{4Dt} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}
$$

The $t$ cancels:

$$
-\frac{\eta}{2} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into Standard Form:**

$$
\frac{d^2\theta}{d\eta^2} + 2\eta \cdot \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Twice:**

Let $p = d\theta/d\eta$:

$$
\frac{dp}{d\eta} + 2\eta p = 0
$$

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dp}{p} = -2\eta \, d\eta
$$

Integrating:

$$
\ln p = -\eta^2 + A_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p = A \cdot e^{-\eta^2}
$$

Integrating again:

$$
\theta(\eta) = A \int_0^\eta e^{-u^2} du + B
$$

**Step 6 — Apply BC at $\eta = 0$** (surface, $C = C_s$ → $\theta = 1$):

$$
\theta(0) = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = 1
$$

**Step 7 — Apply BC at $\eta \to \infty$** (bulk, $C = C_0$ → $\theta = 0$):

$$
0 = A \int_0^{\infty} e^{-u^2} du + 1 = A \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} + 1
$$

$$
A = -\frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final Solution:**

$$
\theta = 1 - \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^{\eta} e^{-u^2} du = 1 - \text{erf}(\eta)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{C(x,t) - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = 1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)\;}
$$

**Step 9 — Case Depth at Threshold Concentration $C_{\text{th}}$:**

Setting $C(x,t) = C_{\text{th}}$ and rearranging:

$$
\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right) = 1 - \frac{C_{\text{th}} - C_0}{C_s - C_0} = \text{erf}(\eta_{\text{th}})
$$

Therefore:

$$
x_{\text{case}} = 2\,\eta_{\text{th}}\,\sqrt{Dt}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The case depth is **linearly proportional to $\sqrt{t}$** and **linearly proportional to $\sqrt{D}$** — confirming the qualitative insight from §2.3.3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time Required to Achieve a Specified Nitriding Case Depth

**Given:** AISI 4140 steel, gas nitriding at $T = 520\,^\circ\text{C} = 793\,\text{K}$. The nitrogen diffusivity in low-alloy steel at this temperature is to be calculated. Required case depth at the 550 HV contour: $x = 0.5\,\text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$. Threshold similarity variable $\eta_{\text{th}} \approx 0.55$ (from concentration ratio analysis).

**Step 1 — Compute Diffusion Coefficient:**

$$
D = D_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q}{RT}\right)
$$

$$
D = 0.0047 \;\text{cm}^2/\text{s} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{77{,}000\;\text{J/mol}}{8.314 \times 793}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{77{,}000}{6593.0} \approx 11.68
$$

$$
\exp(-11.68) \approx 8.39 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
D = 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \times 8.39 \times 10^{-6} = 3.94 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{cm}^2/\text{s}
$$

Converting to SI:

$$
D = 3.94 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Case-Depth Equation:**

$$
x_{\text{case}} = 2\,\eta_{\text{th}}\,\sqrt{D\,t}
$$

Solving for $t$:

$$
t = \left(\frac{x_{\text{case}}}{2\,\eta_{\text{th}}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$
t = \left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.55}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{3.94 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.1}\right)^2 = (4.545 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.066 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
t = \frac{2.066 \times 10^{-7}}{3.94 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 5.24 \times 10^{4}\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hours:**

$$
t = \frac{5.24 \times 10^{4}}{3600} \approx 14.6\;\text{hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;t \approx 14.6\;\text{hours} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{an overnight nitriding cycle is sufficient.}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Effect of Temperature on Case Depth (Comparative)

To show the dramatic effect of temperature, recompute $D$ at $T = 550\,^\circ\text{C} = 823\,\text{K}$:

$$
\frac{Q}{RT} = \frac{77{,}000}{8.314 \times 823} = \frac{77{,}000}{6842.4} \approx 11.25
$$

$$
\exp(-11.25) \approx 1.32 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
D = 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.32 \times 10^{-5} = 6.20 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{cm}^2/\text{s} = 6.20 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}
$$

**Ratio of diffusivities:**

$$
\frac{D_{550}}{D_{520}} = \frac{6.20}{3.94} \approx 1.57
$$

**Time required at 550 °C for the same 0.5 mm case:**

$$
t = \frac{2.066 \times 10^{-7}}{6.20 \times 10^{-12}} = 3.33 \times 10^{4}\;\text{s} \approx 9.25\;\text{hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{A } 30\,^\circ\text{C} \text{ temperature rise cuts nitriding time by } \approx 36\%.\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What (Decision Flowchart Logic)

| **Component Requirement** | **Recommended Treatment** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| High wear + tight dimensional tolerance | **Nitriding** | No phase change → minimal distortion |
| High wear + high impact load | **Induction hardening + tempering** | Hard surface, tough core, deeper case |
| Hot-work die (≈ 500 °C service) | **Nitriding (plasma)** | Hardness retained at temperature |
| Cutting tool (high-speed steel) | **PVD TiN/AlTiN coating + nitriding** | Combined thermal + tribological barrier |
| Large gear, low cost | **Flame hardening** | Cheap, deep case, accepts some distortion |
| Plastic injection mold | **Plasma nitriding + CrN PVD** | Release, corrosion, wear combined |
| Bearing race, fatigue critical | **Through-hardening + tempering** | Uniform hardness, maximum fatigue life |
