# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Why is nitriding preferred over carburizing for precision gears and worms?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Nitriding is preferred for precision gears and worms because of three reasons:

1. **No phase transformation of the core:** Nitriding occurs at **500–550 °C**, which is below the Fe–N eutectoid temperature (591 °C) and the Fe–C eutectoid (727 °C). The core does not undergo austenitization, hence **no dimensional distortion** occurs (typically < 0.005 mm/mm). Precision gears require tight AGMA quality grades (Q6–Q8), which carburizing — operating at 850–950 °C — cannot deliver without post-process grinding.

2. **Higher retained hardness at elevated temperature:** The surface hardness of **800–1200 HV** is retained up to **500 °C**, making nitrided gears suitable for hot-running gearboxes, while carburized surfaces lose hardness above **200 °C** due to carbide coarsening.

3. **Compressive residual stresses:** Nitrogen in solid solution and as fine nitrides (CrN, AlN) imparts a **compressive residual stress** of 400–800 MPa, dramatically improving **rolling contact fatigue life** — the dominant failure mode for gear teeth under Hertzian loading.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line answer tip:** "Sub-eutectoid temperature, no phase change, no distortion, retained hot hardness."

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define 'effective case depth' and 'total case depth' in nitriding. What is the standard hardness threshold for effective case depth as per ISO 18203?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Effective case depth:** The perpendicular distance from the surface to the point where the **Vickers hardness** falls below a defined threshold, measured under a standard load (typically HV 0.3 or HV 1). For nitriding, **ISO 18203** specifies the threshold as **550 HV** (a hardness contour approach) or alternatively, a hardness equal to **core hardness + 100 HV**, depending on the agreement between supplier and customer.

- **Total case depth:** The perpendicular distance from the surface to the point where the **microstructure** no longer shows any evidence of nitriding — i.e., the boundary between the diffusion zone and the unaffected core (typically identified by chemical etching with **3% Nital** and microscopic examination at 100–200×).

- The **550 HV threshold** is the internationally standardized criterion that allows cross-comparison between different nitriding service providers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Choice A** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]
**(a)** With the aid of the **Fe–Fe₃C phase diagram** and a labeled **TTT diagram**, explain the four conventional heat treatment processes (annealing, normalizing, quenching, tempering). For each, specify the **austenitization temperature, cooling medium, and resulting microstructure**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **4140 steel** gear is to be induction-hardened. The skin depth of the induction heating is to be **2.5 mm**. The relative magnetic permeability is **μᵣ = 80**, and the resistivity is **ρ = 2.2 × 10⁻⁷ Ω·m**. Calculate the **required frequency** of the induction coil. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B1-A**

**(i) Annealing:**
- **Austenitization:** A₃ + 30–50 °C (≈ 840 °C for 4140 steel).
- **Cooling:** Furnace cool (≈ 30 °C/h).
- **Microstructure:** Coarse pearlite + proeutectoid ferrite.
- **Properties:** Hardness ≈ 180 HB, ductility maximum, residual stress eliminated.
- **Tribological use:** Pre-machining conditioning, stress relief.

**(ii) Normalizing:**
- **Austenitization:** A₃ + 30–50 °C.
- **Cooling:** Air cool (still air).
- **Microstructure:** Fine pearlite + ferrite.
- **Properties:** Hardness ≈ 220 HB, uniform grain structure.
- **Tribological use:** Grain refinement after forging.

**(iii) Quenching (Hardening):**
- **Austenitization:** A₃ + 30–50 °C; full austenitization ensured by holding.
- **Cooling:** Water (≥ 600 °C/s), oil (≈ 150 °C/s), or 10% PAG polymer.
- **Microstructure:** **Martensite (BCT)** — supersaturated C in Fe, highly distorted lattice.
- **Properties:** Hardness up to **65 HRC**, brittle, high residual stress.
- **Tribological use:** Cutting tools, dies, through-hardened bearings.

**(iv) Tempering:**
- **Temperature:** 150–650 °C, held 1–2 h.
- **Cooling:** Air cool (or water cool for temper-embrittlement-prone steels).
- **Microstructure:** **Tempered martensite** — fine ε-carbide or cementite precipitates in α-Fe matrix.
- **Properties:** Hardness reduced to 40–55 HRC, toughness restored.
- **Tribological use:** Mandatory follow-up to quenching for all dynamically loaded components.

> The **TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) curve** for 4140 steel shows the **C-curve nose** at ≈ 540 °C with an incubation time of ≈ 1 s. To avoid the nose during quenching, the cooling rate must exceed the **critical cooling rate** (≈ 200 °C/s) to ensure the austenite bypasses the pearlite/bainite nose and transforms to martensite at M_s ≈ 300 °C.

---

#### **Model Solution to B1-B**

**Given:**
- Skin depth: $\delta = 2.5\;\text{mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}$
- Relative permeability: $\mu_r = 80$
- Resistivity: $\rho = 2.2 \times 10^{-7}\;\Omega\cdot\text{m}$
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\;\text{H/m}$

**Absolute permeability:**

$$
\mu = \mu_r \cdot \mu_0 = 80 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.005 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{H/m}
$$

**Skin-depth formula:**

$$
\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}
$$

**Square both sides and solve for $f$:**

$$
\delta^2 = \frac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}
$$

$$
f = \frac{\rho}{\pi \mu \delta^2}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
f = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-7}}{\pi \times 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

**Denominator step-by-step:**

$$
\pi \times 1.005 \times 10^{-4} = 3.157 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
3.157 \times 10^{-4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.973 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
f = \frac{2.2 \times 10^{-7}}{1.973 \times 10^{-9}} = 111.5\;\text{Hz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f \approx 111\;\text{Hz}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** This is a **low-frequency induction heating** (in the audio-frequency range, typically supplied by motor-generator sets or modern IGBT inverters), giving a deep case suitable for **large module gears and shaft journals**.

---

### **Question B2 — Choice B (Alternative to B1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain the **gas nitriding process** with a labeled schematic. Discuss the **metallurgy of the compound and diffusion zones**, and explain why **plasma nitriding is preferred for stainless steel components**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **EN41B steel** (Nitriding steel: ≈ 1.5% Cr, 1.0% Al, 0.3% Mo) gear requires a **nitriding case depth of 0.6 mm at the 550 HV contour** at a process temperature of **500 °C**. The nitrogen diffusivity in this steel at 500 °C is $D = 1.85 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$, and the similarity variable at the threshold is $\eta_{\text{th}} = 0.52$. Calculate the **required nitriding time** in hours. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B2-A**

**Gas Nitriding Process Schematic (text-rendered):**

```
  [NH3 cylinder] --> [Flow meter] --> [Furnace at 500-550 C]
                                            |
                                [Steel component on tray]
                                            |
                              [Exhaust NH3 --> H2 + N]
                                            |
                              [Bubbler / dissociator check]
```

**Mechanism:**
1. **Ammonia dissociation** at the hot steel surface:

$$
2\text{NH}_3 \;\xrightarrow{500-550\,^\circ\text{C}}\; 2\text{N (atomic)} + 3\text{H}_2
$$

2. **Adsorption** of atomic N on the surface; diffusion inward along concentration gradient.

**Metallurgy — Two-Zone Structure:**

- **Compound (White) Layer** — top 5–30 μm: A mixture of $\gamma'\text{-Fe}_4\text{N}$ and $\varepsilon\text{-Fe}_{2-3}\text{N}$. Extremely hard (up to 1200 HV) but **brittle**. Often removed by post-nitride grinding or controlled to be < 10 μm.
- **Diffusion Zone** — below compound layer, 0.2–0.8 mm: $\alpha$-Fe matrix with **fine coherent nitride precipitates** of chromium (CrN), aluminum (AlN), and molybdenum (Mo₂N). These precipitates are the *real reason* for the high hardness — they pin dislocations, blocking plastic flow. The hardness profile **decays smoothly** from 1100 HV at the surface to core hardness.

**Why Plasma Nitriding is Preferred for Stainless Steel:**

Stainless steel (e.g., AISI 304, 316) owes its corrosion resistance to a **passive Cr₂O₃ layer**. Conventional gas nitriding fails on stainless steel because:
- The Cr₂O₃ layer **blocks ammonia dissociation** at the surface — nitrogen cannot penetrate.
- Result: poor, uneven, or no nitriding.

**Plasma (ion) nitriding** overcomes this because:
- The **glow discharge** physically **sputters the oxide layer** (cathodic bombardment with H⁺ ions, a process called *sputter cleaning*).
- Once the oxide is removed, the active plasma releases atomic N directly into the surface.
- The result is a uniform, high-quality nitrided layer on austenitic stainless steel — the basis of the famous **"S-phase"** (expanded austenite, also called **γ_N**), which has **> 1500 HV** surface hardness and **maintains corrosion resistance**.

---

#### **Model Solution to B2-B**

**Given:**
- Required case depth: $x = 0.6\;\text{mm} = 6 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}$
- Diffusivity at 500 °C: $D = 1.85 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- Threshold similarity variable: $\eta_{\text{th}} = 0.52$

**Governing equation:**

$$
x = 2\,\eta_{\text{th}}\,\sqrt{D\,t}
$$

**Step 1 — Solve for $t$:**

$$
t = \left(\frac{x}{2\,\eta_{\text{th}}}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\frac{x}{2\eta_{\text{th}}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{2 \times 0.52} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.04} = 5.769 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Square the result:**

$$
(5.769 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 3.328 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $D$:**

$$
t = \frac{3.328 \times 10^{-7}}{1.85 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.799 \times 10^{5}\;\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hours:**

$$
t = \frac{1.799 \times 10^{5}}{3600} = 49.97\;\text{h}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;t \approx 50\;\text{hours}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students often forget to convert $x$ from mm to m. A 0.6 mm entry without conversion gives a 1000×-wrong answer. Always write **all units in SI** before substituting.
> 2. **Dimensional trap:** $D$ in the Fick's law solution must be in **m²/s**, not cm²/s. A common slip is using the cm²/s value from a reference table.
> 3. **Threshold trap:** The similarity variable $\eta_{\text{th}}$ depends on the chosen concentration ratio. KTU often gives this value directly in the question — using $\eta = 1$ (a popular textbook shortcut) is **wrong** unless explicitly justified.
> 4. **Quench confusion:** A common error in part (a) is recommending **water quenching for nitrided steel** — but nitriding is *not* followed by quenching. Furnace cooling to room temperature is the standard practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Surface Treatments and Coatings: KTU 2024 Rapid Recap**

**A. Conventional Heat Treatments (must-know):**
- A₁ = **727 °C** (eutectoid), A₃ = upper critical, M_s ≈ 200–300 °C (medium C steel).
- Anneal = furnace cool (soft), Normalize = air cool (medium), Quench = water/oil (hard/brittle), Temper = reheat 150–650 °C (tough).

**B. Surface Hardening (must-know formulas):**
- Skin depth: $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/(\pi\mu f)}$ — higher $f$ → shallower case.
- Laser case depth: $d \propto P/(v \rho c_p \Delta T)$.

**C. Nitriding (highest weightage in KTU):**
- Temperature: **500–550 °C** (sub-eutectoid, below 591 °C).
- Atmosphere: NH₃ (gas), N₂/H₂ (plasma), cyanide–cyanate (salt bath).
- Diffusion equation: $C(x,t) - C_0 = (C_s - C_0)[1 - \text{erf}(x/2\sqrt{Dt})]$.
- Case depth: $x = 2\eta\sqrt{Dt}$ where $\eta$ is the threshold similarity.
- Diffusion Arrhenius: $D = D_0 \exp(-Q/RT)$ — **temperature-sensitive**.
- Surface hardness: **800–1200 HV**; threshold for case depth: **550 HV** (ISO 18203).
- **No quench, no distortion, retains hardness at 500 °C** — *the four key selling points.*

**D. Coatings (one-line each):**
- **TiN** — gold-colored, 2300 HV, cutting tools.
- **CrN** — silver-gray, 1800 HV, plastic molds (better release).
- **DLC** — black, 1500–9000 HV, μ ≈ 0.05–0.15, engine components.
- **HVOF WC–Co** — 1100–1400 HV, hydraulic rods, landing gear.
- **Al₂O₃** — plasma sprayed, thermal barrier top coat.

**E. Universal Wear Equation (always appears in KTU):**
- **Archard:** $V = kWL/H$ — wear inversely proportional to hardness.

**F. Common KTU Question Triggers:**
- "Low distortion" → **Nitriding**.
- "Hot work die" → **Plasma nitriding (Al-containing steel).**
- "Cutting tool" → **PVD TiN/AlTiN coating.**
- "Deep case + cheap" → **Flame or induction hardening.**
- "Stainless steel + nitriding" → **Plasma nitriding (sputter cleaning of Cr₂O₃).**

**G. Numerical-Heavy Topics to Practice:**
- Induction heating skin depth (frequency calculation).
- Fick's law case depth (time calculation).
- Arrhenius temperature effect on $D$.
- Archard wear volume computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coatings: PVD, Thermal Spray Coatings, and Electroplating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coatings: PVD, Thermal Spray Coatings, and Electroplating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Surface Coatings in Tribology

In tribology, surface coatings are engineered thin or thick films applied to the **substrate** (base material) to alter its surface properties without changing the bulk characteristics. They serve three primary tribological purposes: **wear reduction, friction control, and corrosion protection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET633 Module 4):**
> Surface coatings are classified as *thin films* (typically $< 10 \mu m$, e.g., PVD) and *thick films* (typically $> 50 \mu m$, e.g., thermal spray). The selection depends on the tribological load, contact pressure, sliding speed, and operating environment.

## 1.2 Three Major Coating Technologies

| Coating Type | Governing Principle | Typical Thickness |
|--------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition) | Physical vapor transport under vacuum | $1 - 10 \mu m$ |
| Thermal Spray | Melting + kinetic projection | $50 - 500 \mu m$ |
| Electroplating | Electrochemical reduction | $5 - 50 \mu m$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shield Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:**
> Imagine a sword (the substrate) that needs to be protected from rust and wear:
> - **PVD** is like **atomically spraying a fine mist of titanium** that bonds with the sword's surface at the molecular level — extremely thin, hard, and uniform.
> - **Thermal Spray** is like **shooting molten metal droplets at high speed** that splatter and solidify on the sword — thicker, more rugged, but slightly rougher.
> - **Electroplating** is like **dipping the sword into a charged chemical bath** where metal ions "magically" settle on the sword's surface — uniform, controllable thickness, limited to conductive metals.

## 1.4 Why Coatings Matter in Tribology

- **Hardness enhancement** (e.g., TiN coating raises surface hardness from ~3 GPa to ~25 GPa).
- **Low friction coefficient** (e.g., DLC coatings can reduce $\mu$ from $0.5$ to $< 0.1$).
- **Chemical inertness** (e.g., Cr plating resists acidic corrosion).
- **Dimensional restoration** of worn components (thermal spray is heavily used in **remanufacturing**).
- **Operating temperature** of coating: often rated up to **800 °C** for ceramics like $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

## 1.5 PVD — Physical Vapor Deposition

**Formal Definition:**
PVD is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition process in which a target material is converted into vapor by physical means (evaporation or sputtering) and condensed onto a substrate to form a bonded, dense coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Range:** Chamber pressure of **$10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa**; substrate temperature **$200 - 500\ ^\circ\text{C}$**.

## 1.6 Thermal Spray Coatings

**Formal Definition:**
Thermal spray is a process in which a coating material (in powder, wire, or rod form) is melted or semi-molten by a heat source and propelled onto a substrate, where it rapidly solidifies to form a lamellar (layered) coating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Feature:** The temperature of the heat source can exceed **$10{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$** (in plasma spray), enabling deposition of high-melting-point ceramics.

## 1.7 Electroplating

**Formal Definition:**
Electroplating is an electrochemical deposition process in which positively charged metal ions (cations) from an electrolyte solution are reduced at the cathode (substrate) under an applied DC current, forming a metallic coating governed by **Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Faraday's Constant $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$ is the cornerstone constant for all electroplating calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PVD — Operational Theory

The PVD process follows three universal stages:

1. **Vapor Generation:** The target material is converted to vapor via:
   - **Thermal Evaporation** (resistance, electron beam, or arc heating)
   - **Sputtering** (kinetic energy transfer from inert gas ions, typically $\text{Ar}^+$)
2. **Vapor Transport:** Vapor travels through a high-vacuum chamber with minimal collision (mean free path is large).
3. **Condensation & Film Growth:** Atoms deposit on the substrate, nucleate, and grow into a thin film.

### 2.1.1 Sputtering Yield

$$Y = \frac{\text{Number of atoms ejected}}{\text{Number of incident ions}}$$

- Typical $Y$ values: $\text{Cu with Ar}^+ \approx 2.3$, $\text{Ag with Ar}^+ \approx 3.1$.
- $Y$ depends on **incident ion energy, target mass, and binding energy**.

### 2.1.2 PVD Variants (KTU Focus)

| Variant | Vapor Source | Substrate Bias | Tribological Use |
|---------|------------|----------------|------------------|
| Thermal Evaporation | Resistive/EB heating | None | Decorative |
| Sputtering | $\text{Ar}^+$ ion bombardment | Optional | Tool coatings |
| Cathodic Arc | Vacuum arc discharge | High negative bias | Hard wear coatings (TiN, CrN) |
| Ion Plating | Evap + simultaneous ion bombardment | Negative bias | Excellent adhesion |

## 2.2 Thermal Spray — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Process Stages

1. **Material Feed:** Powder (most common), wire, or rod.
2. **Melting/Softening:** Heat source (flame, arc, plasma, detonation).
3. **Acceleration:** Propellant gas jets the particle toward substrate.
4. **Impact & Solidification:** Splat formation on substrate.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Spray Variants

| Method | Heat Source Temperature | Particle Velocity | Porosity | Bond Strength |
|--------|--------------------------|--------------------|----------|---------------|
| Flame Spray (Powder) | $3{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $30 - 100\ \text{m/s}$ | $10 - 20\%$ | $7 - 20\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Arc Spray | $4{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $50 - 150\ \text{m/s}$ | $5 - 15\%$ | $10 - 30\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Plasma Spray | $12{,}000 - 16{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $100 - 300\ \text{m/s}$ | $2 - 5\%$ | $30 - 70\ \text{MPa}$ |
| HVOF (High Velocity Oxy-Fuel) | $2{,}800\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $600 - 1{,}000\ \text{m/s}$ | $< 1\%$ | $70 - 100\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Detonation Gun | $4{,}500\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $> 1{,}000\ \text{m/s}$ | $< 1\%$ | $80 - 120\ \text{MPa}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *Higher velocity $\rightarrow$ Lower porosity $\rightarrow$ Higher bond strength $\rightarrow$ Better wear resistance.* This is why **HVOF** dominates wear-critical tribological applications like aircraft landing gear and hydraulic rods.

### 2.2.3 Lamellar Microstructure

Each molten droplet "splats" on the substrate and cools at rates of $10^6 - 10^8\ \text{K/s}$, producing layered splats with intersplat pores and oxide stringers.

## 2.3 Electroplating — Electrochemical Theory

### 2.3.1 Cell Components

- **Anode:** Coating metal (e.g., Cr, Ni, Zn) — dissolves into electrolyte.
- **Cathode:** Substrate to be coated.
- **Electrolyte:** Aqueous solution of metal salt (e.g., $\text{NiSO}_4$ for nickel plating).
- **Power Source:** DC rectifier providing $3 - 12\ \text{V}$ and $10 - 500\ \text{A/m}^2$.

### 2.3.2 Faraday's Laws (HIGH-YIELD FOR KTU)

**Faraday's First Law:**
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Faraday's Second Law:**
$$\frac{m_1}{m_2} = \frac{E_1}{E_2} = \frac{M_1 / n_1}{M_2 / n_2}$$

where $E = M/n$ is the **equivalent weight**.

**Current Efficiency (Coulombic Efficiency):**
$$\eta = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.3.3 Coating Thickness Equation

$$h = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{\rho \cdot A \cdot n \cdot F}$$

where $h$ = thickness, $\rho$ = density of deposited metal, $A$ = plated area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation allows process engineers to compute *how long to plate* to achieve a desired thickness — a routine shop-floor calculation in surface finishing industries.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Y = \text{atoms ejected}/\text{incident ions}$ | Sputtering yield (PVD) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $d_{\text{coating}} = m / (\rho A)$ | Coating thickness | m |
| 3 | $m = (I \cdot t \cdot M) / (n \cdot F)$ | Faraday's First Law (mass deposited) | kg |
| 4 | $m_1 / m_2 = E_1 / E_2$ | Faraday's Second Law | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta = (m_{\text{act}}/m_{\text{th}}) \times 100$ | Current efficiency | % |
| 6 | $h = I t M / (\rho A n F)$ | Plating thickness | m |
| 7 | $\text{Bond Strength} \propto v_{\text{particle}}^{n}$ | Thermal spray empirical | MPa |
| 8 | $v_{\text{particle}} \propto \sqrt{T_{\text{heat source}}}$ | Thermal spray velocity | m/s |
| 9 | $\text{Porosity} \propto 1/v_{\text{particle}}$ | Thermal spray porosity | % |
| 10 | $F = 96{,}485$ | Faraday's constant | C mol$^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Isolation Rule:** In the formulas above, vertical bars were strictly avoided. For absolute values, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to prevent breaking the markdown table.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis

### Step 1 — Start with Charge-Conservation Principle

The total electric charge passed through the electrolyte is:
$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where $I$ is the current (amperes) and $t$ is the time (seconds).

### Step 2 — Relate Charge to Moles of Electrons

One mole of electrons carries Faraday's constant $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$. Therefore, the number of moles of electrons transferred is:
$$n_{e^-} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{I \cdot t}{F}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Reaction Stoichiometry

For the general reduction half-reaction:
$$\text{M}^{n+} + n e^- \rightarrow \text{M}$$

$n$ moles of electrons deposit $1$ mole of metal M. So the number of moles of metal deposited is:
$$n_{\text{M}} = \frac{n_{e^-}}{n} = \frac{I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Convert Moles of Metal to Mass

Using molar mass $M$ (kg/mol):
$$m = n_{\text{M}} \cdot M = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **complete, textbook derivation** of Faraday's First Law. It is often tested for $2$ to $3$ marks in KTU exams as a "state and explain" derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Plating Thickness Equation

### Step 1 — Start with Faraday's Mass Expression

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 2 — Express Mass in Terms of Geometry

If the deposited metal forms a uniform film of thickness $h$ over area $A$, with density $\rho$:
$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot h = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $h$

$$h = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **frequently used working equation** in industrial electroplating. It is one of the most heavily tested derivations in the KTU Tribology module.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Three Coating Technologies

| Parameter | PVD | Thermal Spray | Electroplating |
|-----------|-----|---------------|----------------|
| **Principle** | Vapor-phase condensation | Molten droplet impact | Electrochemical reduction |
| **Environment** | High vacuum | Open air / inert gas | Aqueous electrolyte |
| **Temperature** | $200 - 500\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Substrate $< 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (cold process) | $40 - 80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (bath) |
| **Coating Thickness** | $1 - 10\ \mu m$ | $50 - 500\ \mu m$ | $5 - 50\ \mu m$ |
| **Adhesion** | Excellent (diffusion bond) | Good (mechanical + metallurgical) | Moderate (metallurgical) |
| **Porosity** | Near-zero | $1 - 20\%$ | Low (depends on bath) |
| **Materials** | Metals, ceramics, compounds | Ceramics, cermets, polymers | Mainly pure metals |
| **Substrate Restriction** | None | None | Must be conductive |
| **Typical Tribological Use** | Cutting tools (TiN, CrN) | Turbine blades, hydraulic shafts | Corrosion + wear (Cr, Ni) |
| **Cost** | High (vacuum) | Medium | Low |
| **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | None | None | **Yes (critical risk)** |

## 3.4 Analytical Insight: Why HVOF is Preferred for Tribological Coatings

From the empirical bonding relation:
$$\sigma_{\text{bond}} \approx k \cdot v_{\text{particle}}^{1.5}$$

- For **flame spray**, $v \approx 50\ \text{m/s} \Rightarrow \sigma_{\text{bond}} \approx 7\ \text{MPa}$.
- For **HVOF**, $v \approx 800\ \text{m/s} \Rightarrow \sigma_{\text{bond}} \approx 90\ \text{MPa}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** This $\sim 13\times$ improvement in bond strength is the direct cause of HVOF's dominance in tribology — components like **aircraft landing gear, oil & gas drill pipes, and hydraulic rams** rely on HVOF-applied WC-CoCr coatings to survive millions of wear cycles.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Electroplating — KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A steel shaft of area $A = 0.02\ \text{m}^2$ is to be electroplated with chromium. A current of $I = 40\ \text{A}$ is passed for $t = 3{,}600\ \text{s}$. Given: $M_{\text{Cr}} = 0.052\ \text{kg/mol}$, $n = 3$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$, $\rho_{\text{Cr}} = 7{,}190\ \text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate (a) mass of Cr deposited, (b) thickness of coating, (c) time required to achieve $50\ \mu m$ thickness at the same current.

### Solution

**(a) Mass Deposited:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F} = \frac{40 \times 3{,}600 \times 0.052}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m = \frac{7{,}488}{289{,}455} = 0.02586\ \text{kg} \approx 25.86\ \text{g}$$

**(b) Thickness:**

$$h = \frac{m}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{0.02586}{7{,}190 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.02586}{143.8}$$

$$h = 1.798 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} \approx 179.8\ \mu m$$

**(c) Time for $50\ \mu m$:**

$$h = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} \Rightarrow t = \frac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}{I \cdot M}$$

$$t = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 3 \times 96{,}485 \times 7{,}190 \times 0.02}{40 \times 0.052}$$

$$t = \frac{2{,}080.2}{2.08} = 1{,}000\ \text{s} \approx 16.67\ \text{min}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PVD Sputtering Process — Block Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Argon Gas Inlet] --> B[Vacuum Chamber 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ Pa]
    C[DC Power Supply] --> D[Cathode Target e.g. Ti]
    E[Substrate Holder with Bias Voltage] --> B
    B --> F[Plasma Glow Discharge]
    F --> G[Ar+ Ions Bombard Target]
    G --> H[Target Atoms Ejected Sputtering]
    H --> I[Vapor Transport through Vacuum]
    I --> J[Condensation on Substrate]
    J --> K[Thin Film Growth e.g. TiN]
    K --> L[Tribological Coating 1 to 10 μm]
```

## 4.2 Thermal Spray Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Powder Feeder] --> H1[Heat Source]
    H1 --> H2[Plasma Flame / HVOF Jet / Arc]
    H2 --> M1[Particle Melting]
    G1[Propellant Gas] --> H2
    M1 --> A1[Particle Acceleration]
    A1 --> I1[Impact on Substrate]
    I1 --> S1[Particle Splatting and Solidification]
    S1 --> L1[Lamellar Coating Buildup 50 to 500 μm]
    S1 --> P2[Porosity and Oxide Stringers]
    L1 --> C1[Tribological Application e.g. Turbine Blade]
```

## 4.3 Electroplating Cell — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU[DC Rectifier] --> A1[Anode Coating Metal e.g. Cr]
    PSU --> C1[Cathode Substrate e.g. Steel Shaft]
    A1 --> B1[Electrolyte Bath CrO3 + H2SO4]
    C1 --> B1
    B1 --> M1[Metal Cations Cr3+ in Solution]
    M1 --> R1[Reduction at Cathode M to M⁰]
    R1 --> D1[Deposition of Cr Layer]
    A1 --> O1[Oxidation at Anode M⁰ to M3+]
    O1 --> M1
    D1 --> F1[Final Cr Coating 5 to 50 μm]
    PSU --> P1[Current Density 100 to 500 A/m2]
    PSU --> V1[Voltage 3 to 12 V]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Coating Thickness Spectrum — Block Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Electroplating 5 to 50 μm] --> T2[PVD 1 to 10 μm]
    T2 --> T3[Thermal Spray 50 to 500 μm]
    T3 --> T4[Thick Weld Overlay > 1 mm]
    T1 -.-> APP1[Decorative Corrosion]
    T2 -.-> APP2[Cutting Tools Wear]
    T3 -.-> APP3[Turbines Hydraulic Shafts]
    T4 -.-> APP4[Remanufacturing Mining]
```

## 4.5 Process Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start Selection] --> Q1{Substrate Conductive?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Thickness < 10 μm?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R1[Choose PVD]
    Q2 -- No --> R3[Choose Thermal Spray]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{Corrosive Environment?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R2[Choose Electroplating Cr or Ni]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Extreme Wear Loading?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Choose HVOF WC CoCr]
    Q4 -- No --> R5[Choose PVD TiN or CrN]
    R1 --> END[Process Locked]
    R2 --> END
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
    R5 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation Note:** All Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs** (no reserved keywords) and **plain text labels** (no markdown formatting inside labels) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
**Q:** List any **three** differences between PVD and Electroplating coating processes.

**Model Answer:**

| # | PVD | Electroplating |
|---|-----|----------------|
| 1 | Vacuum-based process ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa) | Aqueous electrolyte bath |
| 2 | Coating formed by vapor condensation | Coating formed by electrochemical reduction |
| 3 | Can deposit metals, ceramics, and compounds | Mainly pure metals |
| 4 | Coating thickness $1 - 10\ \mu m$ | Coating thickness $5 - 50\ \mu m$ |
| 5 | No hydrogen embrittlement | Risk of hydrogen embrittlement |

*(Any three points — 3 marks)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
**Q:** What is **HVOF** coating? State its main advantage over conventional flame spray in tribological applications.

**Model Answer:**
HVOF (**High Velocity Oxy-Fuel**) is a thermal spray process in which a fuel (kerosene, hydrogen, propylene) is combusted with oxygen at high pressure, producing a supersonic gas jet ($600 - 1{,}000\ \text{m/s}$) that propels semi-molten particles onto the substrate.

**Main advantage:** Very high particle velocity results in:
- Porosity $< 1\%$ (vs. $10 - 20\%$ in flame spray)
- Bond strength $70 - 100\ \text{MPa}$ (vs. $7 - 20\ \text{MPa}$ in flame spray)
- Excellent wear resistance — ideal for landing gear, hydraulic rods, drill pipes. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (PVD + Electroplating)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **Physical Vapor Deposition (PVD)** process with a neat block diagram. Discuss the **sputtering** mechanism in detail and list its advantages for tribological applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** from fundamentals. A component of surface area $0.05\ \text{m}^2$ is to be electroplated with nickel. Calculate the time required to deposit a $30\ \mu m$ thick coating using a current of $25\ \text{A}$. Given: $M_{\text{Ni}} = 0.0587\ \text{kg/mol}$, $n = 2$, $\rho_{\text{Ni}} = 8{,}900\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C/mol}$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a)

1. **Definition (2 marks):** PVD is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition technique in which a target material is physically converted to vapor phase and condensed onto a substrate.
2. **Process Stages (2 marks):**
   - Vapor generation (sputtering/evaporation)
   - Vapor transport in vacuum
   - Condensation and film growth on substrate
3. **Sputtering Mechanism (2 marks):** Inert gas (Ar) is ionized in vacuum chamber; $\text{Ar}^+$ ions are accelerated toward the negatively biased target (cathode). On impact, atoms from the target are ejected due to momentum transfer. The ejected atoms travel through vacuum and deposit on the substrate.
4. **Advantages (1 mark):** Excellent adhesion, dense coatings, no hydrogen embrittlement, suitable for cutting tools (TiN, CrN coatings).

### Model Solution for (b)

**Derivation (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation]
$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

$$h = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A} \Rightarrow t = \frac{h \cdot n \cdot F \cdot \rho \cdot A}{I \cdot M}$$

$$t = \frac{30 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8{,}900 \times 0.05}{25 \times 0.0587}$$

Numerator: $30 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 8{,}900 \times 0.05$

$$= 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 96{,}485 \times 445$$

$$= 30 \times 10^{-6} \times 8.587 \times 10^{10}$$

$$= 2.576 \times 10^{6}$$

Denominator: $25 \times 0.0587 = 1.4675$

$$t = \frac{2.576 \times 10^{6}}{1.4675} = 1.756 \times 10^{6}\ \text{s} \approx 487.7\ \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — KTU Valuation Pattern:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ — losing **1 mark** for dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing n with Molar Mass:** Students mistakenly use $M$ (molar mass) in place of $n$ (valency) in Faraday's equation — losing **1 to 2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping Derivation Steps:** For 7-mark derivation questions, listing only the final formula without showing $\,Q = It\,$ → $\,n_{e^-} = Q/F\,$ → $\,n_M = n_{e^-}/n\,$ → $\,m = n_M \cdot M\,$ will cost **3 marks**.
> 4. **No Block Diagram for PVD:** If a diagram is asked in part (a) and is missing, **2 marks** are deducted.

### Question B — Choice 2 (Thermal Spray + Comparative)

**(a)** Explain the **Thermal Spray Coating** process. Compare **HVOF** and **Plasma Spray** methods in terms of temperature, particle velocity, porosity, and bond strength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare PVD, Thermal Spray, and Electroplating as **tribological coatings** based on **at least eight** engineering parameters. Justify the use of HVOF for hydraulic shaft applications. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a)

1. **Process Explanation (3 marks):** Thermal spray involves:
   - Feeding material (powder/wire) into a heat source
   - Melting the material to molten/semi-molten state
   - Propelling it using gas jets
   - Impact and rapid solidification on substrate to form a lamellar coating.

2. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | HVOF | Plasma Spray |
|-----------|------|---------------|
| Heat source temp | $2{,}800\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $12{,}000 - 16{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Particle velocity | $600 - 1{,}000\ \text{m/s}$ | $100 - 300\ \text{m/s}$ |
| Porosity | $< 1\%$ | $2 - 5\%$ |
| Bond strength | $70 - 100\ \text{MPa}$ | $30 - 70\ \text{MPa}$ |

### Model Solution for (b)

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**
[Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.3 — students should reproduce an 8-row table]

**Justification for HVOF on Hydraulic Shafts (3 marks):**
- Hydraulic shafts undergo **reciprocating sliding** under high contact pressure with seals.
- WC-CoCr coatings deposited by HVOF provide:
  - Very high hardness ($> 1{,}200\ \text{HV}$)
  - Low friction against seals
  - Excellent bond strength ($> 70\ \text{MPa}$) to resist spalling
  - Near-zero porosity that prevents corrosive media ingress
  - Dimensional restoration of worn shafts (coating thickness $50 - 300\ \mu m$ feasible)
- These properties make HVOF the **industry standard** for hydraulic shaft remanufacturing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Comparative Questions:**
> 1. **Less than 8 parameters:** For a 7-mark "compare" question, examiners expect at least **6 to 8 comparison rows**. Only 3 to 4 rows will fetch partial marks (3 to 4 of 7).
> 2. **One-sided justification:** "HVOF is good" is not justification. You must link *each property* to a *specific operating condition* of hydraulic shafts (sealing, reciprocation, pressure).
> 3. **Mermaid/Table Format:** Use tables — examiners give full credit only if the comparison is structured (bullet points alone often lose **1 mark** for poor presentation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- **PVD** = vacuum ($10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ Pa) + vapor transport + thin film ($1 - 10\ \mu m$); **no hydrogen embrittlement**.
- **Thermal Spray** = molten droplet + kinetic impact + lamellar structure; **substrate stays cold** ($< 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Electroplating** = Faraday's Laws + DC current + aqueous bath; **risk of hydrogen embrittlement**.
- **Faraday's First Law:** $m = (I \cdot t \cdot M)/(n \cdot F)$ — *memorize this verbatim*.
- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485\ \text{C mol}^{-1}$.
- **Plating Thickness:** $h = I t M / (n F \rho A)$ — *workhorse formula for numericals*.
- **HVOF** = supersonic ($600 - 1{,}000\ \text{m/s}$) + porosity $< 1\%$ + bond strength $70 - 100\ \text{MPa}$ = **best for wear**.
- **Plasma Spray** = highest temperature ($> 12{,}000\ ^\circ\text{C}$) = best for **ceramic** coatings like $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ and $\text{ZrO}_2$.
- **TiN (gold-colored PVD coating)** is the **classic tribological coating** for cutting tools and drills.
- **Cr electroplating** is used for **corrosion + wear**; **Ni electroplating** is used for **undercoating + wear**.
- **Hydrogen embrittlement** is the **single most important failure mode** in electroplated high-strength steel parts.
- **Coating thickness order (ascending):** PVD ($1 - 10\ \mu m$) $<$ Electroplating ($5 - 50\ \mu m$) $<$ Thermal Spray ($50 - 500\ \mu m$).
- **Substrate temperature order (ascending):** Thermal Spray ($< 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$) $<$ PVD ($200 - 500\ ^\circ\text{C}$) $<$ Electroplating bath ($40 - 80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ — *but only bath temp, not substrate*).
- **KTU frequently tests:** Faraday's derivation (3-5 marks), comparison tables (5-7 marks), HVOF vs. plasma spray (3-5 marks).
- **Numerical problems** in KTU almost always revolve around **plating thickness** — practice at least 3 numericals with different metals (Cr, Ni, Cu, Zn).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tribology in Industries: Tribological Challenges in Engines, Transmissions, and Braking Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET633 - INDUSTRIAL TRIBOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tribology in Industries: Tribological Challenges in Engines, Transmissions, and Braking Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Tribology — The Engineering Backbone of Mechanical Motion

**Industrial Tribology** is the applied science and engineering discipline dedicated to the study of friction, wear, and lubrication between interacting surfaces in real-world machines, systems, and industrial components. While classical tribology focuses on idealized contact mechanics, industrial tribology extends these fundamentals to predict, analyze, and solve the operational challenges encountered in practical machinery — particularly in **internal combustion (IC) engines**, **mechanical power transmission systems**, and **braking systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Industrial tribology encompasses the study of surface interactions, lubricant behavior, wear mechanisms, and frictional energy dissipation in operating machinery, with the objective of enhancing **energy efficiency, component life, reliability, and environmental sustainability** of industrial systems.

The three core variables that govern every tribological contact in industry are:

1. **Friction (μ)** — resistance to relative motion at the contact interface.
2. **Wear (W)** — progressive loss of material from contacting surfaces.
3. **Lubrication Regime (λ)** — hydrodynamic, mixed, boundary, or EHL, characterized by the **lambda ratio (λ = h_min / σ_combined)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **lambda ratio (λ)** is a dimensionless KTU-recommended metric. A value **λ > 3** indicates full-film lubrication, **1 < λ < 3** indicates mixed lubrication, and **λ < 1** indicates boundary lubrication.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sliding Hand on a Surface" Analogy

Imagine sliding your hand across three different surfaces — a **wet glass table**, a **slightly oily metal plate**, and a **dry sandpaper sheet**.

- The **wet glass table** feels effortless — this mimics **hydrodynamic lubrication** in a journal bearing, where a full oil film separates the surfaces.
- The **oily metal plate** feels slight resistance with occasional stick-slip — this mimics **mixed lubrication** in piston ring–liner contact, the dominant regime in IC engines.
- The **dry sandpaper** feels rough, noisy, and your palm heats up — this is analogous to **boundary lubrication** in brake pads, where direct asperity contact governs friction and wear.

Just as the comfort, effort, and skin wear differ between these surfaces, every industrial component operates in a specific tribological regime that must be carefully designed for durability and efficiency.

## 1.3 Why Industrial Tribology Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Impact:** Approximately **23% of the world's total energy consumption** is lost to friction and wear-related losses, with transportation systems (engines, transmissions, brakes) accounting for nearly half of this loss. A single percentage point reduction in friction in IC engines can save **billions of liters of fuel annually** worldwide.

For KTU examination purposes, three industrial application domains are emphasized:

- **Engines** — piston rings, cylinder liners, camshafts, bearings, valves.
- **Transmissions** — gears, constant-velocity (CV) joints, synchronizers, clutches.
- **Braking systems** — brake pads, discs/drums, friction materials, calipers.

Each of these subsystems has a unique **tribosystem triad** (load, speed, temperature, environment) that must be engineered carefully.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tribological Challenges in Internal Combustion (IC) Engines

### 2.1.1 Piston Ring — Cylinder Liner Interface

The piston ring–cylinder liner contact is widely regarded as the **most critical tribological interface** in an IC engine, contributing to **20–30% of the total frictional losses** in the engine. Three rings typically seal the combustion chamber: **top compression ring, second compression ring, and oil control ring**.

- **Operating Conditions:** Reciprocating motion with peak contact pressures up to **100–700 MPa**, sliding speeds of **5–15 m/s**, and bulk oil temperatures of **80–150 °C**.
- **Lubrication Regime:** Predominantly **mixed lubrication**, transitioning to boundary lubrication at top dead center (TDC) where velocity approaches zero.
- **Key Failure Modes:** Scuffing, adhesive wear, abrasive wear, and bore polishing.

### 2.1.2 Cam — Follower Interface

The cam-follower system operates under **high Hertzian contact stress** (up to 1.5 GPa) with sliding-rolling motion. This is a **boundary-lubricated, high-load, low-speed** contact dominated by **EHL and boundary regimes**.

- **Failure Modes:** Pitting, spalling, scuffing, and fatigue wear.
- **Mitigation:** Anti-wear (AW) and extreme-pressure (EP) additives in engine oil, along with hardened follower surfaces.

### 2.1.3 Engine Bearings (Main and Big-End Bearings)

Crankshaft bearings operate under **hydrodynamic or elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** with hydrodynamic pressure generated by journal rotation. The **Sommerfeld number (So)** and **specific load** are critical design parameters.

- **Failure Modes:** Fatigue (pitting), cavitation erosion, and adhesive wear during start-up (boundary regime).
- **Design Constraint:** Minimum oil film thickness must exceed the combined surface roughness to prevent metal-to-metal contact.

### 2.1.4 Valve Train and Tappets

These components experience **impact loading** at every cam rotation, leading to **fretting wear** and **brinelling** if lubrication is inadequate. Roller followers reduce sliding friction, but boundary lubrication is still essential.

## 2.2 Tribological Challenges in Power Transmission Systems

### 2.2.1 Gears

Gear contacts are predominantly **EHL contacts** where the lubricant film is entrained by the rolling action. Sliding along the line of action introduces frictional energy.

- **Operating Conditions:** Hertzian contact stress up to **1.5–2.0 GPa**, sliding speeds up to **20 m/s**, bulk temperatures **60–120 °C**.
- **Key Tribological Issues:**
  - **Scuffing (Galling):** Adhesive wear at high sliding speeds and low lambda ratio.
  - **Pitting:** Subsurface-originated fatigue failure due to repeated Hertzian stress cycles.
  - **Micropitting:** Surface-initiated fatigue from low lambda ratio (λ < 1).
  - **Wear and Abrasive Scoring:** From contaminated oil.
- **Design Solution:** Optimal lubricant viscosity, EP/AW additives, surface finish (Ra < 0.4 μm), and gear geometry.

### 2.2.2 Constant-Velocity (CV) Joints

CV joints transmit torque at varying angles and must maintain near-constant velocity. The ball-and-cage or tripod designs operate under **mixed EHL**.

- **Failure Modes:** Brinelling from impact loading, grease degradation, and fretting wear.

### 2.2.3 Synchronizer Rings and Clutch Systems

The synchronizer ring–gear cone interface generates high friction briefly to **equalize speeds before gear engagement**. Clutch plates experience **sliding friction** during engagement/disengagement.

- **Key Challenge:** Heat dissipation during repeated engagement and minimizing judder/vibration.

## 2.3 Tribological Challenges in Braking Systems

Braking systems are unique in tribology because they are **designed to convert kinetic energy into heat through controlled friction**. Unlike engines and gears, braking requires **deliberate wear** in a controlled manner.

### 2.3.1 Disc Brake (Pad–Rotor Interface)

- **Operating Conditions:** Contact pressures of **1–5 MPa** (lower than gears), sliding speeds up to **30 m/s** during emergency braking, and rotor surface temperatures up to **600–800 °C** in severe use.
- **Key Tribological Issues:**
  - **Brake Fade:** Reduction in coefficient of friction (μ) at elevated temperatures due to decomposition of organic binder materials.
  - **Thermal Cracking and Disc Distortion (DTV — Disc Thickness Variation):** Caused by cyclic thermal loading.
  - **Pad Glazing:** Formation of a hard, smooth transfer film on the rotor surface that reduces μ.
  - **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness):** Squeal from stick-slip instabilities.
  - **Wear Debris Generation:** Environmental and health concerns from particulate matter.

### 2.3.2 Drum Brake (Shoe–Drum Interface)

- **Operating Conditions:** Lower sliding speed but higher contact area; bulk temperatures up to **400 °C**.
- **Key Issues:** Self-energization effects, hot spots, and lining wear.

### 2.3.3 Friction Materials

Brake friction materials are **composite materials** engineered to balance **μ stability, wear rate, noise, and environmental compliance**. They typically contain:

- **Binders** (phenolic resins) — hold the composite together.
- **Friction Modifiers** (abrasives like alumina, silicates, iron oxides).
- **Lubricants** (graphite, metal sulfides) — to stabilize μ at high temperatures.
- **Reinforcements** (steel/aramid fibers) — for structural integrity.
- **Fillers** (barytes, vermiculite) — for cost and density control.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Variables & Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Archard's Wear Equation** | $W = \dfrac{k \cdot F_N \cdot L}{H}$ | $W$ = wear volume [m³], $k$ = wear coefficient (dimensionless), $F_N$ = normal load [N], $L$ = sliding distance [m], $H$ = hardness [Pa] | Predicts wear volume in sliding contacts (rings, pads, gears) |
| **Lambda Ratio** | $\lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma^*}$ | $h_{min}$ = minimum film thickness [m], $\sigma^* = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ = combined RMS roughness [m] | Determines lubrication regime |
| **Stribeck Curve Friction** | $\mu = f\!\left(\dfrac{\eta \cdot N}{P}\right)$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s], $N$ = rotational speed [rev/s], $P$ = load per unit projected area [Pa] | Maps friction vs. Hersey number |
| **Hersey Number** | $He = \dfrac{\eta \cdot N}{P}$ | Dimensionless | Grouping variable on Stribeck curve |
| **Sommerfeld Number** | $So = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\mu \cdot N}{P}$ | $r$ = journal radius [m], $c$ = radial clearance [m] | Bearing design and load capacity |
| **Reynolds Equation (2D)** | $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(h^3 \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(h^3 \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 \eta U \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | $h$ = film thickness [m], $p$ = pressure [Pa], $U$ = entraining velocity [m/s] | Predicts hydrodynamic pressure in bearings and EHL contacts |
| **Hertzian Contact Pressure** | $p_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 F_N}{2 \pi a b}}$ | $a$, $b$ = semi-axes of contact ellipse [m] | Cam-follower, gear tooth contact |
| **Friction Power Loss** | $P_{loss} = \mu \cdot F_N \cdot v$ | $v$ = sliding velocity [m/s], $P_{loss}$ [W] | Engine friction, brake heat generation |
| **Brake Specific Energy** | $BSE = \dfrac{0.5 \, m \, v^2}{A_{pad}}$ | $m$ = vehicle mass [kg], $v$ = velocity [m/s], $A_{pad}$ = pad swept area [m²] | Braking performance metric [J/m²] |
| **PV Limit** | $PV = P \cdot v$ | $P$ = pressure [Pa], $v$ = sliding velocity [m/s] | Wear and temperature limit for sliding contacts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice the **PV limit** is shared across all three applications — engines, gears, and brakes all must operate below a critical PV threshold. Exceeding it triggers thermal runaway and accelerated wear.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles studied here form the foundation of industries such as:

- **Automotive OEMs** (engine efficiency, transmission durability, brake safety).
- **Aerospace tribology** (landing gear, turbine bearings).
- **Locomotive braking systems** (high-energy friction).
- **Off-highway machinery** (heavy-duty gear oils and greases).
- **EV development** — tribology of electric drivetrains, regenerative braking, and e-axles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Archard's Wear Equation (Sliding Contact)

The **Archard equation** is the cornerstone of industrial wear prediction. We derive it from the physical mechanics of asperity contact.

**Assumption:** When two surfaces slide against each other, the real contact occurs only at the tips of asperities, and the probability of an asperity producing a wear particle is captured by a dimensionless constant $k$.

**Step 1 — Real Area of Contact**

The real area of contact $A_r$ is the sum of all asperity junctions. From plastic deformation theory:

$$A_r = \dfrac{F_N}{H}$$

where $F_N$ is the normal load and $H$ is the hardness (flow pressure) of the softer material.

> **Logic:** Asperities plastically deform until they support the applied load, and hardness is the material's resistance to plastic indentation.

**Step 2 — Volume of Wear Per Unit Sliding Distance**

As the harder surface slides a distance $\delta L$ over the softer, the volume of wear debris $dV$ generated is proportional to the real contact area:

$$dV = k \cdot A_r \cdot \delta L$$

> **Logic:** $k$ is the dimensionless wear coefficient representing the probability that an asperity encounter produces a wear fragment; it is typically in the range $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-4}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Integrate**

Substituting $A_r$ from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$dV = k \cdot \dfrac{F_N}{H} \cdot \delta L$$

Integrating over a finite sliding distance $L$:

$$\int_0^V dV = k \cdot \dfrac{F_N}{H} \int_0^L dL$$

$$V = \dfrac{k \cdot F_N \cdot L}{H}$$

This is **Archard's wear equation**, expressing the wear volume as a linear function of normal load and sliding distance.

**Step 4 — Linear Wear Rate**

The linear wear depth $h_w$ is obtained by dividing the wear volume by the apparent contact area $A$:

$$h_w = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{k \cdot F_N \cdot L}{H \cdot A} = \dfrac{k \cdot p \cdot L}{H}$$

where $p = F_N / A$ is the apparent contact pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Stribeck Curve and Hersey Number

**Stribeck (1902)** discovered that the friction coefficient in lubricated contacts is not constant but varies with operating conditions. He grouped the relevant variables into a single dimensionless parameter.

**Step 1 — Petroff's Friction Equation (for fully flooded journal bearing)**

Petroff assumed a thin, continuous, viscous oil film with no net side flow:

$$F_{friction} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \mu N \eta L r^3}{c}$$

where $N$ is rotational speed [rev/s], $L$ is bearing length, $r$ is journal radius, $c$ is radial clearance, $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\mu$ is the friction coefficient.

**Step 2 — Petroff's Friction Coefficient**

Rearranging for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \dfrac{F_{friction}}{F_N} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 \eta N L r^3}{c \cdot P \cdot A}$$

where $P \cdot A = F_N$ is the load. The projected bearing area is $A = 2 r L$, so:

$$\mu = 2 \pi^2 \cdot \dfrac{\eta N}{P} \cdot \dfrac{r}{c}$$

**Step 3 — Define the Hersey Number and Sommerfeld Number**

The grouped dimensionless term $\dfrac{\eta N}{P}$ is the **Hersey number (He)**, and multiplying by $\dfrac{r}{c}$ gives the **Sommerfeld number (So)**:

$$He = \dfrac{\eta N}{P}, \qquad So = \dfrac{r}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\eta N}{P}$$

> **Logic:** Both He and So are the master variables on the Stribeck curve. As He increases from low to high values, the system transitions from boundary → mixed → hydrodynamic lubrication, with corresponding changes in $\mu$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tribological Comparison Across Applications

This matrix summarizes the **regime, dominant wear mode, and design strategy** for each application:

| **Application** | **Component** | **Contact Type** | **Lubrication Regime** | **Contact Pressure** | **Sliding Speed** | **Dominant Wear Mode** | **Design Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IC Engine** | Piston ring – liner | Reciprocating sliding | Mixed → Boundary | 100–700 MPa | 5–15 m/s | Scuffing, bore polishing | Multi-grade oil, low-friction coatings (DLC) |
| **IC Engine** | Cam – follower | Sliding-rolling impact | Boundary / EHL | Up to 1.5 GPa | 1–4 m/s | Pitting, scuffing | EP/AW additives, hardened followers |
| **IC Engine** | Main bearing | Conformal rotating | Hydrodynamic / EHL | 20–50 MPa | 5–15 m/s | Fatigue (pitting) | Optimal viscosity, oil grooves |
| **Transmission** | Spur/Helical gear | Rolling-sliding (EHL) | EHL | 0.5–2.0 GPa | 5–20 m/s | Pitting, scuffing, micropitting | EP gear oil, surface finish |
| **Transmission** | CV joint | Sliding-rolling | Mixed EHL | 200–800 MPa | 1–5 m/s | Fretting, brinelling | High-quality grease, boots |
| **Braking** | Pad – disc | Sliding, dry/semi-dry | Boundary (intentional) | 1–5 MPa | 0–30 m/s | Adhesive/abrasive wear, fade | Composite friction materials |
| **Braking** | Shoe – drum | Sliding, dry | Boundary | 0.5–3 MPa | 0–15 m/s | Hot spots, lining wear | Heat-resistant linings |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that **engines and gears** operate in *lubricated* regimes where minimizing friction is the goal, whereas **brakes** operate in *boundary/dry* regimes where **maximizing and stabilizing friction** is the design objective. This is a fundamental philosophical inversion in tribological design.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Calculating Engine Friction Loss

**Given:** A 4-cylinder 2000 cc gasoline engine operating at 3000 RPM with average ring tension $F_N = 50$ N per piston, sliding speed $v = 8$ m/s, and oil viscosity giving $\mu = 0.06$ at operating temperature. There are 4 rings per piston.

**Step 1 — Friction Force per Ring**

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot F_N = 0.06 \times 50 = 3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Friction Power per Ring**

$$P_{ring} = F_{friction} \cdot v = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Total Friction Power (4 rings × 4 cylinders)**

$$P_{total} = 24 \times 4 \times 4 = 384 \text{ W} = 0.384 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Comment**

This is a substantial portion of engine friction loss and explains why modern engine oils and low-friction ring coatings (e.g., DLC, CrN) are intensely researched.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Industrial Tribosystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **interactions of tribological variables** across engines, transmissions, and braking systems, illustrating how load, speed, lubrication, materials, and environment converge in each application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Tribosystem Inputs] --> B[Operating Parameters]
    A --> C[Material Properties]
    A --> D[Lubricant Properties]
    A --> E[Environmental Conditions]

    B --> B1[Normal Load F_N]
    B --> B2[Sliding Speed v]
    B --> B3[Bulk Temperature T]

    C --> C1[Hardness H]
    C --> C2[Surface Roughness Ra]
    C --> C3[Elastic Modulus E]

    D --> D1[Dynamic Viscosity eta]
    D --> D2[Additive Package EP AW]
    D --> D3[Lambda Ratio lambda]

    E --> E1[Ambient Temperature]
    E --> E2[Dust and Contamination]
    E --> E3[Humidity]

    B1 --> F1[IC Engine Subsystem]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    C1 --> F1
    C2 --> F1
    D1 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1

    B1 --> F2[Transmission Subsystem]

    C2 --> F2
    D1 --> F2
    D2 --> F2
    E2 --> F2

    B1 --> F3[Braking Subsystem]
    B2 --> F3
    B3 --> F3

    C2 --> F3
    D1 --> F3
    E1 --> F3
    E2 --> F3

    F1 --> G1[Piston Ring Liner Contact]

    F2 --> H1[Gear Tooth EHL Contact]

    G1 --> J[Tribological Outputs]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J

    I2 --> J

    J --> K1[Friction Coefficient mu]

```

## 4.2 Lubrication Regime Map (Stribeck Curve Schematic)

The Stribeck curve is the master diagnostic tool for industrial tribologists. It relates the **Hersey number (ηN/P)** to the **friction coefficient (μ)**, defining the three primary lubrication regimes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boundary Lubrication] -->|Lambda less than 1| B[Mixed Lubrication]
    B -->|Lambda between 1 and 3| C[Hydrodynamic Full Film]
    A -->|High friction, high wear| A1[Direct asperity contact]
    A -->|Brake pads, sliding at low speed| A2[Brake Pad and Ring Liner TDC]
    B -->|Partial asperity and fluid load support| B1[Most IC engine contacts]
    B -->|Cam followers, mid-speed gears| B2[Typical Industrial Regime]
    C -->|Full fluid film, low wear| C1[Journal bearings, well-designed EHL gears]
    C -->|Lowest friction| C2[Hydrodynamic Journal Bearing]

    A --> D[Stribeck Curve Operating Path]
    B --> D
    C --> D

    D --> E[Energy Efficiency]
    D --> F[Component Life]
    D --> G[Heat Generation]
```

## 4.3 Braking System Tribology Flow

The following block diagram explains how the energy of a moving vehicle is converted into heat via the pad-disc interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Kinetic Energy] --> B[Hydraulic System Actuation]
    B --> C[Brake Caliper Piston]
    C --> D[Normal Force F_N on Pad]
    D --> E[Pad Disc Sliding Contact]
    E --> F[Friction Force F_friction = mu F_N]
    F --> G[Rotational Deceleration of Disc]
    G --> H[Heat Generation Q]
    H --> I[Thermal Distribution in Disc and Pad]
    I --> J[Fade, Glazing, NVH]
    I --> K[Material Wear W over Time]
    J --> L[Performance Feedback to ABS ECU]
    K --> M[Service Interval Trigger]
    L --> N[Closed Loop Braking Control]
    M --> O[Maintenance Action]
```
