# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define micro and nano tribology. Why is it important for MEMS/NEMS devices?**

**Model Answer:**

Micro and nano tribology is the study of friction, wear, adhesion, and lubrication phenomena occurring at micro- and nanometer length scales, where surface and intermolecular forces dominate over body forces.

**Importance for MEMS/NEMS:**

1. **High surface-to-volume ratio:** Surface forces (van der Waals, capillary) dominate over gravitational/inertial forces, causing *adhesion and stiction* failures.
2. **Smooth contacting surfaces:** Real contact area approaches nominal contact area, increasing adhesion.
3. **Low contact forces:** Applied loads (µN–nN) are comparable to surface forces, so classical friction laws fail.
4. **Reliability bottleneck:** Studies (e.g., Sandia National Labs) report that **> 70 % of MEMS field failures** are tribological.
5. **NEMS devices** (sub-100 nm features) are even more sensitive, where Casimir and quantum effects matter.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List and briefly explain the four dominant forces at the micro/nano scale.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Van der Waals force:** Long-range (~ nm) attractive force from temporary dipoles in atoms. Governed by Hamaker constant $A_H$. Always present.
2. **Capillary force:** Attractive force from liquid meniscus (usually water) condensed between contacting surfaces in ambient air. Depends on relative humidity.
3. **Electrostatic force:** Attractive or repulsive force from surface charges. Tunable by applied voltage (used in capacitive MEMS).
4. **Chemical bonding force:** Strongest (covalent/ionic/metallic), but acts only at atomic contact (~0.2–0.3 nm separation).

> [!NOTE]
> *Gravitational force is negligible at this scale. (2 marks for listing, 1 mark for brief explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the van der Waals force between a sphere of radius R and a flat surface separated by a minimum distance D. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon microcantilever of dimensions $100 \, \mu m \times 10 \, \mu m \times 1 \, \mu m$ is in contact with a silicon substrate. Calculate the adhesion force, given Hamaker constant $A_H = 2.5 \times 10^{-19}$ J and contact gap $D = 0.5$ nm. Use the sphere–flat approximation with effective radius $R = 50 \, \mu m$ (accounting for beam curvature). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Pairwise Force Between Two Molecules (1 mark)**

The van der Waals interaction between two molecules separated by distance $r$ is given by the London potential:

$$u(r) = -\frac{C}{r^6}$$

The pairwise force is:

$$f(r) = -\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{6C}{r^7}$$

**Step 2 — Hamaker Constant (1 mark)**

Define the Hamaker constant as the combined material property:

$$A_H = \pi^2 \, n_1 n_2 \, C$$

where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the molecular number densities of the two bodies.

**Step 3 — Pressure Between Two Parallel Plates (2 marks)**

The pressure between two flat parallel surfaces separated by distance $z$ is:

$$P(z) = -\frac{A_H}{6\pi z^3}$$

**Step 4 — Derjaguin Approximation (1 mark)**

For a sphere of radius $R$ at minimum gap $D$, integrate the pressure over the sphere geometry (Derjaguin approximation):

$$F = 2\pi R \int_D^\infty P(z) \, dz$$

**Step 5 — Final Integration (2 marks)**

$$F = 2\pi R \int_D^\infty \left( -\frac{A_H}{6\pi z^3} \right) dz$$

$$F = -\frac{A_H R}{3} \int_D^\infty z^{-3} \, dz$$

$$F = -\frac{A_H R}{3} \left[ \frac{z^{-2}}{-2} \right]_D^\infty$$

$$F = -\frac{A_H R}{3} \left( \frac{1}{2 D^2} \right)$$

$$\boxed{F_{vdW} = -\frac{A_H R}{6 D^2}}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Retarded van der Waals regime (close-range limit).
- Bodies are rigid and non-deformable.
- Surfaces are smooth (roughness < D).
- Pairwise additivity of London forces.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values (1 mark)**
- $R = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $A_H = 2.5 \times 10^{-19}$ J
- $D = 0.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m $= 5 \times 10^{-10}$ m

**Step 2 — Write the Formula (1 mark)**

$$F_{vdW} = -\frac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values (2 marks)**

$$F_{vdW} = -\frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-19}) \times (5 \times 10^{-5})}{6 \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Denominator (1 mark)**

$$6 \times (5 \times 10^{-10})^2 = 6 \times 25 \times 10^{-20} = 150 \times 10^{-20} = 1.5 \times 10^{-18}$$

**Step 5 — Final Division (1 mark)**

$$F_{vdW} = -\frac{12.5 \times 10^{-24}}{1.5 \times 10^{-18}} = -8.33 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{vdW} \approx -8.33 \, \mu N}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation (1 mark)**

The negative sign indicates an attractive force of ~8.33 µN, which is large enough to cause *stiction* in the MEMS cantilever, demonstrating why van der Waals forces cannot be neglected in MEMS design.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain the phenomenon of stiction in MEMS devices. Discuss the role of capillary force and surface energy. How is stiction mitigated in practice? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) as a boundary lubricant in MEMS. State two advantages and two limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition of Stiction (1 mark)**

Stiction is the unintentional adhesion and locking of two contacting MEMS surfaces, where the static friction force exceeds the restoring force of the device, preventing it from returning to its original position.

**Step 2 — Role of Capillary Force (2 marks)**

In ambient air, water vapor condenses into a thin meniscus between two surfaces in close proximity. The Laplace pressure of this meniscus creates an attractive force:

$$F_c = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$

This force pulls the two surfaces together. For MEMS with sub-µm gaps, the meniscus force can be several µN — large enough to permanently stick the surfaces.

**Step 3 — Role of Surface Energy (2 marks)**

The work of adhesion $W = 2\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ (Young–Dupré) defines the energy per unit area required to separate two surfaces. Higher surface energy means higher adhesion. Silicon, silicon dioxide, and most metals have high surface energies, making them prone to stiction.

**Step 4 — Mitigation Strategies (2 marks)**

1. **Surface coatings:** Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) such as octadecyltrichlorosilane (OTS) on Si, or alkanethiols on Au.
2. **Surface texturing:** Dimples or pillars that reduce real contact area.
3. **Hermetic packaging:** Sealing the device in dry nitrogen or vacuum.
4. **Geometric design:** Stiffening structures so the restoring force exceeds adhesion.
5. **Use of low-surface-energy materials** (e.g., PTFE-like coatings, fluoropolymers).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — SAM Definition (1 mark)**

A self-assembled monolayer (SAM) is a single layer of molecules that spontaneously organizes on a substrate, with a **head group** that chemically bonds to the surface and a **tail group** that provides the desired surface properties.

**Step 2 — Diagram (already covered in SECTION 4.3 block diagram) (2 marks)**

The schematic is: **Substrate → Head Group (covalent bond) → Hydrocarbon/Fluorocarbon Chain → Terminal Group (CH₃ or CF₃).**

**Step 3 — Working as Boundary Lubricant (2 marks)**

- The **head group** anchors the SAM strongly to the substrate, ensuring robustness.
- The **chain** is held to neighboring chains by weak van der Waals forces, allowing low-shear sliding.
- The **terminal group** reduces surface energy, making the surface hydrophobic — which *eliminates capillary force* by repelling water.
- Combined effect: a 1 – 3 nm thick film that drastically reduces both adhesion and friction.

**Step 4 — Advantages (1 mark)**

1. Ultra-thin (~1–3 nm) — does not change device geometry.
2. Highly ordered, dense, and chemically stable.

**Step 5 — Limitations (1 mark)**

1. Sensitive to contamination and wear — once damaged, regeneration is hard.
2. Limited thermal stability (most SAMs degrade above ~200 °C).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Micro/Nano Tribology Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $F_{vdW}$** — many students write the magnitude and forget the attractive nature. (Lose 0.5 mark.)
> 2. **Unit mismatch in Hamaker constant** — confusing J (energy) with N·m (work). Always state units explicitly.
> 3. **Using $D^3$ instead of $D^2$ in sphere–flat geometry** — the parallel-plate formula has $D^3$, the sphere–flat has $D^2$. Mixing them is a common error.
> 4. **Confusing JKR and DMT** — JKR is for soft, large-radius, high-adhesion; DMT is for hard, small-radius, low-adhesion. State the regime explicitly.
> 5. **Stiction vs. Friction** — stiction is *static* locking, friction is *dynamic* resistance. Examiners test this distinction.
> 6. **Not relating micro-tribology to MEMS applications** — purely theoretical answers score less; mention at least one real device (HDD, AFM, micro-engine).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Notes — Module 4, Micro and Nano Tribology**

### A. Core Concepts
- ✅ Micro/nano tribology = friction, wear, adhesion, lubrication at µm/nm scales.
- ✅ MEMS feature size: 1 – 1000 µm; NEMS: < 100 nm.
- ✅ Surface-to-volume ratio scales as $1/L$ — increases as size shrinks.
- ✅ Surface forces dominate body forces at small scales.

### B. Key Forces (in order of strength at contact)
- ✅ Chemical bonding (0.2 – 0.3 nm range)
- ✅ Adhesion / van der Waals (long-range, $A_H$ governed)
- ✅ Capillary (ambient humidity dependent)
- ✅ Electrostatic (tunable)
- ✅ Casimir (sub-10 nm)
- ✅ Magnetic (material-specific)

### C. Critical Formulas (must memorize)
- ✅ $F_{vdW} = -\dfrac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$ (sphere–flat)
- ✅ $F_c = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ (capillary)
- ✅ $F_{JKR} = -\dfrac{3}{2}\pi R W$ (JKR adhesion)
- ✅ $F_f = \mu (N + F_{adh})$ (modified Amontons)

### D. Failure Modes in MEMS
- ✅ **Stiction** — static locking from adhesion + capillary.
- ✅ **High friction** — when $F_{adhesion} \gg N_{applied}$.
- ✅ **Wear** — adhesive or abrasive at asperity level.
- ✅ **Fatigue** — cyclic loading on compliant structures.

### E. Mitigation Strategies
- ✅ **Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs)** — OTS, alkanethiols, fluorosilanes.
- ✅ **Perfluoropolyether (PFPE) lubricants** — for HDDs.
- ✅ **Surface texturing** — reduce real contact area.
- ✅ **Hermetic packaging** — control humidity.
- ✅ **Hard coatings** — DLC, SiC, TiN.

### F. Diagnostic Tools
- ✅ **AFM** — Atomic Force Microscope (topography + force).
- ✅ **FFM** — Friction Force Microscope (lateral force).
- ✅ **SFA** — Surface Forces Apparatus (direct force vs. distance).
- ✅ **LFM** — Lateral Force Microscopy.

### G. Applications to Remember (for KTU 2-mark questions)
- ✅ Hard disk drives (head-disk interface).
- ✅ Micro-engines and micromotors.
- ✅ Bio-MEMS (drug delivery, lab-on-chip).
- ✅ RF-MEMS switches.
- ✅ NEMS mass sensors and resonators.
- ✅ AFM/FFM probes.

### H. Numerical Knob
- ✅ A typical MEMS component with $R = 10 \, \mu m$ and $D = 1$ nm has $F_{vdW} \sim 0.4 \, \mu N$, which can be **20× its weight** — a vivid illustration of adhesion dominance.
